description: Unvalidated LLM finding outputs before evidence validation and filtering.

finding.summary: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module accepts APB writes to interrupt control registers (INT, INT_SET, INT_CLR, MASK, MASK_SET, MASK_CLR, ACK, ACK_SET, ACK_CLR) without any privilege, authentication, or lock check. Additionally, core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0 at the instantiation site, permanently disabling any secure-mode protection intended by the ENA_SEC_IRQ parameter.

evidence.description: APB write enable is determined solely by psel & penable & pwrite with no privilege or security check: 'assign s_is_apb_write = apb_slave.psel & apb_slave.penable & apb_slave.pwrite;'
evidence.supports_claim: Any APB master can write to interrupt control registers without any privilege gate.

evidence.description: The interrupt mask register (r_mask), interrupt pending register (r_int), and ACK register are updated directly from APB write data whenever s_is_apb_write is asserted and the address matches, with no additional guard: e.g., 's_mask_next = apb_slave.pwdata;' and 's_int_next[i] = apb_slave.pwdata[i];'
evidence.supports_claim: Interrupt mask and pending bits are unprotected security-critical resources that can be freely manipulated.

evidence.description: At the fc_subsystem instantiation of apb_interrupt_cntrl, core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0: '.core_secure_mode_i ( 1'b0 )'. This permanently disables any secure-mode enforcement the ENA_SEC_IRQ parameter was intended to provide.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure mode input is permanently disabled, bypassing the intended security partition for interrupt control registers.

evidence.description: The module declares parameter ENA_SEC_IRQ=1 and accepts input core_secure_mode_i, but the source code in SNIP-0001 and SNIP-0002 shows no conditional logic gating register writes on core_secure_mode_i or ENA_SEC_IRQ anywhere in the synthesized logic paths.
evidence.supports_claim: Despite the ENA_SEC_IRQ parameter, no guard using core_secure_mode_i is present in the register-write paths, making the security feature ineffective.

evidence.description: The read path in the always_comb block returns r_int, r_ack, r_mask, and r_fifo_event to any APB reader without privilege check: e.g., 'apb_slave.prdata = r_int;' when s_is_apb_read && s_is_int.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive interrupt state (pending interrupts, ACK state, masks) is readable by any APB master, enabling information disclosure of interrupt system state.

finding.reasoning_summary: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module implements interrupt mask, pending, and acknowledgment registers accessible via APB. The write decode logic gates updates only on APB protocol signals (psel/penable/pwrite) with no privilege level, lock, or security check. Although the module has a core_secure_mode_i input and an ENA_SEC_IRQ parameter, no guard logic using these signals appears in the register-write combinational blocks (SNIP-0002). Furthermore, at the instantiation in fc_subsystem (SNIP-0007), core_secure_mode_i is tied to 1'b0, meaning even if such logic existed, it would be permanently bypassed. Any software running on the fabric controller that can generate APB transactions can freely modify interrupt masks, artificially set or clear interrupt pending bits, and read the full interrupt state, violating the intended security isolation.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged APB master can: (1) disable interrupts by clearing the mask register, causing denial-of-service; (2) inject spurious interrupts by setting INT_SET bits, causing incorrect interrupt handling; (3) suppress interrupt acknowledgment by manipulating ACK registers; (4) read out the current interrupt pending state, leaking information about system activity. With core_secure_mode_i permanently 0, the ENA_SEC_IRQ security partition is entirely non-functional.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full implementation of any ENA_SEC_IRQ conditional gating is not visible in the provided snippets; it is possible such gating exists in unshown portions of the file. However, the hardwired core_secure_mode_i=1'b0 at instantiation (SNIP-0007) is concrete evidence of bypass regardless.

finding.summary: core_clock_en_o is hardwired to 1'b1 in apb_interrupt_cntrl, permanently enabling the CPU core clock with no gating mechanism, bypassing any power or security control over clock enablement.

evidence.description: Line 85: 'assign core_clock_en_o = 1'b1;' permanently asserts the clock enable output unconditionally. No register, permission check, mode flag, or lock guards this assignment.
evidence.supports_claim: The clock enable signal is hardcoded to always-on, meaning no software or hardware mechanism can gate the core clock through this path.

evidence.description: Line 283: core_secure_mode_i is driven with constant 1'b0 when instantiating apb_interrupt_cntrl, and supervisor_mode_o is hardwired to 1'b1 (line 273). Security mode inputs are ignored, further confirming that security-sensitive controls are bypassed.
evidence.supports_claim: Even if apb_interrupt_cntrl had internal logic conditional on core_secure_mode_i, the signal is permanently deasserted at the instantiation site, preventing any secure-mode gating.

evidence.description: Line 228: clock_en_i of zeroriscy_core is driven directly by core_clock_en, which originates from core_clock_en_o. The CPU core clock enable is therefore permanently asserted and cannot be gated.
evidence.supports_claim: The zeroriscy CPU core clock gate input is permanently enabled, meaning the core cannot be halted or power-gated via this control path.

evidence.description: clock_en_i in zeroriscy_core controls whether the clock is gated (comment: 'enable clock, otherwise it is gated'). With it permanently 1, the core clock is never gated regardless of system state.
evidence.supports_claim: Intended clock gating security/power control is entirely defeated; the core always runs, which may allow unauthorized execution or prevent secure shutdown.

finding.reasoning_summary: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module contains a hardcoded 'assign core_clock_en_o = 1'b1' with no conditional logic, no register backing, and no privilege or security check. This signal propagates through fc_subsystem as core_clock_en and connects directly to the zeroriscy_core's clock_en_i port, which is documented as gating the core clock. The instantiation also hardwires core_secure_mode_i to 0 and supervisor_mode_o to 1, confirming that all security-mode signals are bypassed. The result is that the CPU core clock enable mechanism is permanently defeated.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or misconfigured system cannot gate the CPU core clock through this control path. This bypasses any intended power-management-based security control (e.g., forced halt, secure shutdown, clock-based isolation). It also means any software attempting to use clock gating as a trust boundary enforcement mechanism is silently ineffective.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: No evidence is available for whether a separate clock gating cell elsewhere in the hierarchy could independently gate the clock, which could partially mitigate the impact. The intent of the clock enable as a security vs. power-management control is inferred from context.

finding.summary: The `core_secure_mode_i` input of `apb_interrupt_cntrl` is permanently hardwired to `1'b0` at the instantiation site in `fc_subsystem`, disabling any secure-mode protection the controller may use to gate privileged interrupt operations.

evidence.description: At line 283, `core_secure_mode_i` is tied to the constant `1'b0` rather than being driven by an actual secure-mode signal. This permanently forces the controller into non-secure mode regardless of the system's true security state.
evidence.supports_claim: The secure-mode input intended to distinguish privileged from unprivileged interrupt controller access is hardwired off, meaning the controller can never enter secure mode and any guard logic conditioned on `core_secure_mode_i` is permanently bypassed.

evidence.description: The `apb_interrupt_cntrl` module declares `core_secure_mode_i` as an input (line 47) and exposes `ENA_SEC_IRQ` as a parameter (line 26), indicating the design intends to support a secure interrupt path. The parameter `ENA_SEC_IRQ` defaults to 1, implying secure IRQ support is expected to be active.
evidence.supports_claim: The module architecture anticipates a functioning secure mode; tying `core_secure_mode_i` to 0 defeats this protection mechanism entirely.

evidence.description: The interrupt registers `r_int`, `r_mask`, and `r_ack` (lines 192-204) are modified by APB writes and core IRQ acknowledgements without any check on `core_secure_mode_i` in the combinational logic, confirming that all interrupt state is accessible in the forced non-secure mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Interrupt mask, pending, and acknowledge registers are writable by any APB master because the secure-mode guard is permanently disabled.

evidence.description: The read-data path (lines 213-225) returns `r_int`, `r_ack`, `r_mask`, and `r_fifo_event` to any APB reader without a privilege check, enabled by the forced non-secure state. Any bus master can read sensitive interrupt state.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive interrupt state is readable by unprivileged APB masters because the secure-mode signal is permanently zero.

finding.reasoning_summary: The `apb_interrupt_cntrl` module is parameterized with `ENA_SEC_IRQ=1` (secure IRQ support enabled by default) and accepts a `core_secure_mode_i` input meant to reflect the processor's current privilege/security level. However, the instantiation in `fc_subsystem` permanently drives this input with the constant `1'b0`. Any internal logic that conditionally restricts interrupt register access or sets `core_irq_sec_o` based on `core_secure_mode_i` is therefore never activated. This means the interrupt controller always behaves as if the system is in non-secure mode, allowing any APB master to read and write interrupt mask, pending, and acknowledge registers without privilege enforcement.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or malicious APB master can freely read sensitive interrupt state (pending IRQs, ACK status, mask configuration) and write to interrupt control registers to suppress, inject, or acknowledge interrupts—actions that should be restricted to secure/privileged software. This can be used to escalate privileges, hide malicious activity by masking security-critical interrupts, or cause denial-of-service by clearing legitimate interrupt state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact internal use of `core_secure_mode_i` within `apb_interrupt_cntrl` is not fully shown in the snippets (the parameter `ENA_SEC_IRQ` gated logic body is not visible), but the constant-zero binding at the instantiation site is concrete evidence of the bypass regardless of the internal implementation detail.

finding.summary: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module exposes sensitive interrupt controller registers (r_int, r_ack, r_mask, r_fifo_event) as readable via APB without any privilege or security check. Additionally, core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0 at instantiation, permanently disabling any secure-mode protection that the module parameter ENA_SEC_IRQ=1 might imply.

evidence.description: Interrupt state registers r_int, r_ack, r_mask, and r_fifo_event are returned directly to apb_slave.prdata when s_is_apb_read is asserted, with no privilege level, security mode, or access permission check.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive interrupt controller state is readable by any APB master without restriction.

evidence.description: s_is_apb_read is defined as apb_slave.psel & apb_slave.penable & ~apb_slave.pwrite (line 91). There is no check on core_secure_mode_i, privilege level, or any other permission signal before granting read access to interrupt registers.
evidence.supports_claim: No permission or privilege check guards read access to interrupt control registers.

evidence.description: At instantiation in fc_subsystem.sv (line 283), core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0, permanently disabling any secure-mode protection, even though the module declares ENA_SEC_IRQ=1 as a parameter and exposes a core_secure_mode_i input port.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure mode input is permanently suppressed, bypassing intended security controls.

evidence.description: The read data path (lines 211-226) uses s_is_apb_read to gate register readback, but the guard is purely a bus-protocol check with no security or privilege dimension.
evidence.supports_claim: Any bus master capable of performing an APB read transaction can read interrupt state registers.

evidence.description: The module has a test_mode_i input (line 32) and ENA_SEC_IRQ parameter (line 26), but the source shows core_secure_mode_i is accepted as a port yet not used in any guard logic visible in the read or write paths.
evidence.supports_claim: Security mode signal exists at the interface level but is not applied in the access control logic.

finding.reasoning_summary: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module implements interrupt mask, interrupt pending, and interrupt acknowledgement registers that are security-sensitive. Reading these registers reveals which interrupts are pending and which are masked, and writing them can suppress or inject interrupt state. The only guard on register access is the standard APB handshake (psel & penable & pwrite/~pwrite). The module has a core_secure_mode_i port and an ENA_SEC_IRQ parameter suggesting intent to enforce secure-mode checks, but the instantiation in fc_subsystem.sv hardwires core_secure_mode_i=1'b0, eliminating any such protection. No privilege check, lock, or access control is applied before sensitive register readback or write.
finding.security_impact: Any APB master can read interrupt pending/mask/ack state without restriction, leaking information about which interrupts are active. Any APB master can also write these registers to suppress, inject, or acknowledge interrupts, potentially disrupting the security-critical interrupt delivery path between peripherals and the processor core. Hardwiring core_secure_mode_i to 0 permanently disables the intended secure-world protection mechanism.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full implementation of how core_secure_mode_i and ENA_SEC_IRQ are expected to be used is not visible in the provided snippets — there may be additional logic outside the shown lines that was intended to gate access but is absent. The read path shown (lines 211-226) is complete and confirms no security guard is present there.

finding.summary: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module permanently hardwires core_secure_mode_i to 1'b0 and supervisor_mode_o to 1'b1, and performs no privilege or security-level check before allowing APB writes to modify interrupt mask, interrupt set/clear, and acknowledgment registers. Any APB master can freely manipulate all interrupt control registers without any access guard.

evidence.description: In fc_subsystem.sv line 283, core_secure_mode_i is tied to constant 1'b0 when instantiating apb_interrupt_cntrl, meaning the secure-mode input is never asserted. Additionally, line 273 hardwires supervisor_mode_o = 1'b1 unconditionally.
evidence.supports_claim: Security mode signal is permanently disabled at instantiation, preventing any privilege-based access control within the interrupt controller from being exercised.

evidence.description: s_is_apb_write is derived solely from apb_slave.psel & apb_slave.penable & apb_slave.pwrite (line 90). There is no check of core_secure_mode_i or any privilege level before granting write access to interrupt control registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The only gate for APB writes is the standard APB handshake; no security or privilege check is present, so any APB master can write to interrupt registers.

evidence.description: Lines 129-168 show that s_is_apb_write alone controls updates to s_mask_next (interrupt mask), s_int_next (interrupt pending), and s_ack_next (interrupt acknowledgment) registers. No privilege gate guards these writes.
evidence.supports_claim: Critical interrupt control registers (mask, interrupt pending, ack) are writable by any APB master without privilege verification.

evidence.description: Lines 211-226 show that r_int, r_ack, r_mask, and r_fifo_event are readable via APB without any privilege or security check beyond the standard APB read handshake.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive interrupt state registers are readable by any APB master, enabling information disclosure of interrupt pending and acknowledgment state.

evidence.description: core_secure_mode_i port exists (line 47) but is never used in any combinational or sequential guard logic within the module; it is also driven to 1'b0 from the parent (SNIP-0007 line 283).
evidence.supports_claim: The security mode infrastructure is present but completely unused, leaving the interrupt controller without any privilege enforcement.

finding.reasoning_summary: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module exposes a core_secure_mode_i input intended to convey security mode context, but this signal is hardwired to 1'b0 by the fc_subsystem instantiation and is never used in any guard condition inside the module. The sole condition for writing to interrupt mask, interrupt pending, and acknowledgment registers is the standard APB write handshake (psel & penable & pwrite). There is no privilege check, no security-level gate, and no lock mechanism. This means any unprivileged APB master can set or clear interrupt masks, artificially set or clear interrupt pending bits, and manipulate acknowledgment state, potentially causing denial-of-service or privilege escalation via interrupt spoofing.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged APB bus master can freely manipulate interrupt mask registers (disabling or enabling interrupts for arbitrary sources), forge interrupt pending status, and clear or set acknowledgment registers. This can cause denial of service by masking critical interrupts, or enable privilege escalation by injecting spurious interrupt requests to the processor core.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is possible that APB bus access is restricted at a higher interconnect level (e.g., bus fabric access controls), but no such evidence appears in the provided snippets. The core_secure_mode_i signal exists but its intended enforcement logic is absent from the source.

finding.summary: The interrupt controller's mask register (r_mask) and interrupt register (r_int) are fully readable and writable by any APB bus master without any privilege, authentication, or lock-based access control. Additionally, core_secure_mode_i is permanently hardwired to 0 at the instantiation site, disabling any secure-mode protection intended by the ENA_SEC_IRQ parameter.

evidence.description: The APB write qualification is purely: psel & penable & pwrite. Any bus master that can assert these signals gains full write access to mask, interrupt, and ack registers. No privilege level, secure-mode, or lock check is interposed.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing access guard on interrupt controller register writes.

evidence.description: s_is_apb_write = apb_slave.psel & apb_slave.penable & apb_slave.pwrite (line 90). s_is_mask, s_is_mask_set, s_is_mask_clr, s_is_int, s_is_int_set, s_is_int_clr are derived solely from address comparison (lines 96-101). No check on core_secure_mode_i or any privilege signal gating these decode signals.
evidence.supports_claim: Guard logic is address-only; no privilege or secure-mode check exists.

evidence.description: proc_mask (lines 129-140): s_mask_next is updated unconditionally whenever s_is_apb_write is asserted and address matches mask registers. r_mask directly controls which interrupts are forwarded to the core (line 108: core_irq_req_o = |(r_int & r_mask)). An unprivileged APB master can mask or unmask any interrupt.
evidence.supports_claim: Interrupt mask register is writable without privilege check, allowing arbitrary interrupt suppression or activation.

evidence.description: Module parameter ENA_SEC_IRQ = 1 and port core_secure_mode_i are present (lines 26, 47), suggesting an intended secure-mode gating mechanism for interrupt handling.
evidence.supports_claim: A secure-mode permission state signal exists in the design but is unused in access control logic.

evidence.description: At the fc_subsystem instantiation (line 283): .core_secure_mode_i ( 1'b0 ) is hardwired to zero permanently. This means any security behavior that ENA_SEC_IRQ and core_secure_mode_i were intended to gate is permanently disabled, regardless of the actual processor security mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure mode input permanently disabled, bypassing any interrupt security partition the design intended.

evidence.description: APB read path (lines 211-226) exposes r_int, r_ack, r_mask, and r_fifo_event to any APB read with no privilege check. Any master can read the current interrupt pending state and mask configuration.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive interrupt state is readable without access control, enabling information disclosure.

evidence.description: core_irq_req_o = |(r_int & r_mask) (line 108). An attacker with APB access can write r_mask to 0 to suppress all interrupts to the processor core, or write r_int_set to assert spurious interrupts. This can cause denial of service or privilege escalation via forced interrupt handling.
evidence.supports_claim: Unprivileged write to mask/interrupt registers directly affects processor interrupt delivery, enabling denial-of-service or interrupt injection attacks.

finding.reasoning_summary: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module controls interrupt delivery to the processor core. Its mask and interrupt registers are protected only by APB address decoding with no privilege, secure-mode, or lock-based gating. The module includes a core_secure_mode_i input and ENA_SEC_IRQ parameter that suggest security partitioning was intended, but the input is hardwired to 1'b0 at the fc_subsystem instantiation, permanently disabling any such protection. Any APB bus master can freely read or write the interrupt mask and pending interrupt state, enabling interrupt suppression (denial of service) or spurious interrupt injection.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or compromised APB master can: (1) clear the interrupt mask to suppress all interrupts to the processor core, causing denial of service; (2) set arbitrary interrupt bits to inject spurious interrupts; (3) read the full interrupt pending and mask state, disclosing system activity. The secure-mode protection intended by ENA_SEC_IRQ/core_secure_mode_i is permanently disabled by the hardwired 1'b0 tie-off.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal usage of core_secure_mode_i within apb_interrupt_cntrl (if any conditional logic references it beyond the port declaration) is not shown in the available snippets. However, the hardwiring to 1'b0 at instantiation is conclusive regardless. Also, whether the APB fabric itself provides bus-master authentication before reaching this peripheral is not visible in the provided context.

finding.summary: The interrupt mask register (r_mask / s_mask_next) in apb_interrupt_cntrl is freely writable via APB without any privilege, security, or lock check. Additionally, core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 0 at the instantiation site, permanently disabling any secure-mode protection. The mask register controls which interrupts are forwarded to the CPU core, so unprivileged APB writes can silently suppress or enable any interrupt.

evidence.description: APB write detection uses only psel, penable, and pwrite signals. No privilege level, secure-mode, or lock check gates access to the mask register.
evidence.supports_claim: Any APB master can issue a write transaction that is treated as authoritative with no access-control check.

evidence.description: proc_mask always_comb block updates s_mask_next directly from apb_slave.pwdata whenever s_is_apb_write is asserted and the address matches REG_MASK, REG_MASK_SET, or REG_MASK_CLEAR. There is no guard checking privilege level, secure mode, or any lock bit before allowing the write.
evidence.supports_claim: The interrupt mask register can be overwritten by any APB initiator, enabling an attacker to mask legitimate interrupts or unmask spurious ones.

evidence.description: core_secure_mode_i is declared as an input port (line 47) but the logic in the always_comb blocks never conditions mask updates on it. The signal is received but unused in the permission path.
evidence.supports_claim: The secure-mode input exists in the interface but provides no protection over mask register writes.

evidence.description: At the fc_subsystem instantiation site, core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0 (line 283), permanently asserting non-secure mode regardless of the actual system security state.
evidence.supports_claim: Even if the RTL had conditional logic based on core_secure_mode_i, it is hardwired off at integration, making any such protection permanently bypassed.

evidence.description: r_mask (line 62) is the interrupt mask register. core_irq_req_o = |(r_int & r_mask) (line 108) shows that r_mask directly gates all interrupt requests to the processor core.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthorized modification of r_mask directly controls which interrupts reach the CPU, constituting a high-impact security-relevant resource.

evidence.description: apb_slave.prdata is set to r_mask on a read of REG_MASK (line 222) with no access-control check, allowing readback of the current mask configuration by any APB master.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive interrupt-mask state is readable by any bus master, aiding an attacker in crafting targeted mask modifications.

finding.reasoning_summary: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module exposes interrupt mask registers (REG_MASK, REG_MASK_SET, REG_MASK_CLEAR) over APB with no privilege, lock, or secure-mode guard. The module accepts core_secure_mode_i as an input but never uses it to condition mask writes. Worse, the instantiating module fc_subsystem ties core_secure_mode_i permanently to 1'b0, completely negating any future protection that could be added. Because r_mask directly gates core_irq_req_o, an unprivileged APB master can suppress or inject interrupt visibility to the CPU core.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged APB bus master can write to the interrupt mask register to suppress critical interrupts (denial-of-service on interrupt delivery) or enable spurious interrupts to the CPU. Reading the mask register reveals current interrupt configuration. The permanent hardwiring of core_secure_mode_i=0 ensures this weakness persists across all system security states.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not visible from the provided snippets whether the APB bus itself has upstream access-control (e.g., a firewall or MPU) that restricts which masters can reach this peripheral. If such infrastructure exists at the SoC level, the impact would be reduced, but the RTL-level absence of any guard is confirmed.

finding.summary: The test_mode_i/test_en_i signal propagates without any guard or authentication check to security-sensitive modules including the interrupt controller, CPU core, and hardware accelerator, enabling scan/test-mode bypass of normal operational security controls.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem connects test_en_i directly to the apb_interrupt_cntrl instance (fc_eu_i) via test_mode_i port, and also hardwires core_secure_mode_i to 1'b0, meaning the interrupt controller always operates in non-secure mode regardless of actual security state.
evidence.supports_claim: test_en_i is routed without any guard to the interrupt controller; simultaneously, secure mode is permanently disabled (1'b0), creating a compound security weakness.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem connects test_en_i directly to the zeroriscy_core CPU instance (lFC_CORE) via the test_en_i port, which per the core's documentation enables all clock gates for testing.
evidence.supports_claim: The CPU's test mode is ungated, allowing a test-mode assertion to override clock gating and potentially other security controls in the processor.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem connects test_en_i directly to fc_hwpe (hardware accelerator) instance via test_mode_i port, propagating test mode to the accelerator's MAC top wrap without any guard.
evidence.supports_claim: The hardware accelerator's test mode is ungated, extending the attack surface to the memory-accessible accelerator.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_core documents test_en_i as 'enable all clock gates for testing'. No filtering, authentication, or lifecycle-state check is applied before this signal affects the core's clock gating behavior.
evidence.supports_claim: Asserting test_en_i unconditionally enables all clock gates, which can expose internal state and bypass power/clock gating-based isolation.

evidence.description: apb_interrupt_cntrl manages interrupt routing (core_irq_req_o, core_irq_id_o, core_irq_sec_o, fetch_en_o, core_clock_en_o). It accepts test_mode_i without any guard logic shown in the module, while fetch_en_o and core_clock_en_o are hardwired to 1'b1.
evidence.supports_claim: The interrupt controller, which controls security-relevant signals such as fetch enable and secure IRQ, receives the unguarded test mode signal.

evidence.description: core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0 at the fc_eu_i instantiation, meaning the interrupt controller never operates in secure mode regardless of system security state. Combined with unguarded test_mode_i propagation, security-mode enforcement is entirely absent.
evidence.supports_claim: Hardwiring core_secure_mode_i=0 disables the secure interrupt path permanently, and test mode can further alter interrupt controller behavior with no authentication or lifecycle gate.

finding.reasoning_summary: The test_en_i signal from fc_subsystem is propagated directly and without any guard, authentication check, or lifecycle-state condition to three security-sensitive submodules: the zeroriscy CPU core (where it enables all clock gates), the APB interrupt controller, and the HWPE hardware accelerator. No snippet shows any filtering logic (e.g., checking a secure boot state, fuse, or lifecycle register) before test_mode_i/test_en_i reaches these modules. Additionally, the interrupt controller instance hardwires core_secure_mode_i to 1'b0, permanently disabling secure-mode interrupt routing. Together, these represent a missing guard vulnerability where an adversary who can assert test_en_i (e.g., through a scan chain or debug interface) gains unrestricted test-mode access to the CPU, interrupt controller, and hardware accelerator.
finding.security_impact: An attacker able to assert test_en_i can: (1) enable all CPU clock gates, potentially exposing internal pipeline state via scan chains; (2) influence interrupt controller behavior in an unguarded test mode while secure mode is permanently disabled; (3) place the hardware accelerator in test mode, potentially enabling unauthorized memory access or state readback. The permanent disabling of secure mode in the interrupt controller means secure interrupts are never enforced, which could allow privilege escalation or bypass of interrupt-based access control.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact functional effect of test_mode_i within apb_interrupt_cntrl is not fully visible in the provided snippets (only port declaration shown, not internal use). Similarly, the internal use of test_en_i in zeroriscy_id_stage is declared but its downstream logic is not shown. The origin/controllability of test_en_i at the top level (whether it is a pin, register, or fuse-controlled signal) is not shown in the provided context, which affects exploitability assessment.

finding.summary: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module allows any APB master to directly write interrupt registers (INT, MASK, ACK) without any privilege or security check. Additionally, the core_secure_mode_i input is hardwired to 1'b0 at instantiation, permanently disabling any secure-mode protection that the controller was designed to support.

evidence.description: The write-enable signal s_is_apb_write is derived solely from APB handshake signals (psel, penable, pwrite) with no privilege, security-level, or ownership check. Any APB master that can address the peripheral can write to interrupt mask and pending registers.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing privilege/security guard on APB write path to sensitive interrupt registers.

evidence.description: proc_mask, proc_int, and proc_ack all gate updates solely on s_is_apb_write (an unprivileged APB transaction flag). There is no check against core_secure_mode_i or any other security signal before allowing writes to r_mask, r_int, or r_ack.
evidence.supports_claim: An unprivileged APB master can overwrite interrupt mask, pending, and acknowledge registers, enabling interrupt suppression or spurious injection.

evidence.description: At instantiation in fc_subsystem, core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0, permanently asserting non-secure mode. Even if the controller contained secure-mode gating logic, it is bypassed by this constant tie-off.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure mode protection is permanently disabled by tying core_secure_mode_i to 0 at instantiation.

evidence.description: APB read path exposes r_int (pending interrupts), r_ack (acknowledged interrupts), r_mask (interrupt mask), and r_fifo_event (event FIFO data) to any APB reader with no read-access control.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive interrupt state is readable by any APB master without privilege enforcement.

evidence.description: The parameter ENA_SEC_IRQ=1 at module definition suggests secure IRQ support was intended, and core_secure_mode_i is declared as an input port, but neither is functionally used to gate any register access in the observed logic.
evidence.supports_claim: Security feature exists in interface but is never enforced in combinational logic.

finding.reasoning_summary: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module receives a core_secure_mode_i input and has an ENA_SEC_IRQ parameter, indicating an intent to support secure/non-secure access differentiation. However, the combinational logic for updating r_mask, r_int, and r_ack (SNIP-0002) only checks s_is_apb_write, which is an unprivileged APB transaction indicator. No check on core_secure_mode_i or privilege level is present. Furthermore, at instantiation (SNIP-0006), core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0, ensuring the controller always operates as if in non-secure mode. This allows any software or hardware agent with APB access to suppress interrupts, inject spurious interrupts, or clear acknowledged interrupt state.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with APB bus access (e.g., a compromised peripheral or software running at any privilege level) can: (1) clear interrupt masks to suppress delivery of critical security interrupts, (2) inject spurious interrupt pending bits to cause denial-of-service or privilege escalation via unexpected interrupt handlers, (3) read back current interrupt state to infer system activity. The permanent tie of core_secure_mode_i to 0 means no hardware-enforced security domain separation exists for interrupt control.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is possible that ENA_SEC_IRQ gating logic exists elsewhere in the file (lines not shown between 118-124 or 194-207), but the provided snippets cover all combinational register-update logic and show no security checks. The instantiation tie-off of core_secure_mode_i to 0 is unambiguous.

finding.summary: Debug registers in address range 6'b10_0000 (RD_DBGS) are readable without requiring the core to be halted, allowing sensitive debug state readback while the CPU is running.

evidence.description: The debug unit accepts external debug requests via debug_req_i and debug_addr_i with no privilege or authentication check at the module boundary. Any agent able to assert debug_req_i can access debug registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface is accessible without any privilege enforcement at the entry point.

evidence.description: For the write path to CSR registers (debug_addr_i[14]=1), access is gated by 'if (debug_halted_o)' at line 151. Similarly for GPR write at line 210 and GPR read at line 249. However, for the RD_DBGS read path at lines 240-244, rdata_sel_n is set to RD_DBGS and debug_gnt_o is granted with NO check on debug_halted_o.
evidence.supports_claim: The halted-core guard is present for CSR and GPR accesses but is missing for the RD_DBGS (6'b10_0000) read path.

evidence.description: Lines 240-244 show: '6'b10_0000: begin // Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug / debug_gnt_o = 1'b1; // grant it even when invalid access to not block / rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGS; end'. The comment says 'only accessible when in debug' but there is no conditional check on debug_halted_o before setting rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGS. The read is always granted and data always selected, violating the stated access policy.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive debug register data (RD_DBGS) is returned to the requester regardless of whether the core is halted, bypassing the intended access control.

evidence.description: The RD_DBGS register group at address range 6'b10_0000 is explicitly commented as 'Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug', indicating these registers are intended to be restricted to halted-core state. They contain debug-sensitive state that should not be readable during normal execution.
evidence.supports_claim: These registers are a protected resource whose access should be conditioned on debug_halted_o.

evidence.description: A similar inconsistency exists on the write side: lines 196-205 show the 6'b10_0000 write path grants debug_gnt_o unconditionally but only performs the write (jump_req_n) when debug_halted_o. For reads, however, RD_DBGS data is unconditionally selected and returned, so an attacker can read sensitive debug registers (e.g., dbg_cause, dbg_ssth) while the core is running.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing halt guard on read path exposes sensitive core debug state to any debug-bus requester during normal CPU operation.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem declares debug_addr as a 15-bit logic signal with no explicit protection or validation, which is wired directly to zeroriscy_core's debug_addr_i port.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug address is sourced from an unprotected subsystem-level signal with no access control upstream.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit implements address-based access control for debug register reads and writes. For CSR accesses (debug_addr_i[14]=1) and GPR accesses (addr[13:8]=6'b00_0100), access to sensitive operations is correctly gated on debug_halted_o. However, for the 6'b10_0000 address range (RD_DBGS), the read path at lines 240-244 sets rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGS and grants debug_gnt_o unconditionally without checking debug_halted_o. The comment explicitly states these are 'only accessible when in debug', but no enforcement is present on the read path. This allows any entity with access to the debug bus to read these registers while the processor is running.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug bus can read the RD_DBGS register group (containing debug cause, single-step state, and other sensitive debug metadata) without requiring the core to be in the halted/debug state. This leaks internal CPU state and debug configuration during normal operation, potentially aiding privilege escalation or exploitation of debug-triggered behaviors.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact contents of the RD_DBGS registers are not fully shown in the provided snippets (the read data mux output logic is not included). However, the missing halt guard on the read path is clearly visible in the source snippet at lines 240-244 and is unambiguous given the explicit comment and the pattern of halted_o checks present elsewhere.

finding.summary: The zeroriscy debug unit grants debug access (debug_gnt_o=1) to certain register regions without verifying that the core is halted, allowing unauthenticated debug reads and writes to proceed while the CPU is running.

evidence.description: At line 163-164, the 'Debug Registers, always accessible' region (addr[13:8]==6'b00_0000) unconditionally asserts debug_gnt_o=1 on write without any debug_halted_o check. Similarly at line 196-197, the 'Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug' region grants (debug_gnt_o=1) before checking debug_halted_o for the actual operation — the grant is issued regardless of halt state. At line 207-208, GPR write access grants unconditionally and only conditionally executes the write, but the grant itself is not gated. At lines 222-223, 234-235, 240-241, 246-247, 255, read accesses to CSR, debug register, and GPR regions also issue debug_gnt_o=1 without requiring the core to be halted first.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_gnt_o is asserted for multiple address regions without requiring debug_halted_o to be true, meaning a debug master can obtain grants and trigger operations even when the CPU is not in a halted/debug state.

evidence.description: The debug_halted_o check at lines 151, 199, 210, 226, 249 only gates the internal side-effects (csr_req_n, regfile_wreq, regfile_rreq_n, rdata_sel_n) but does NOT gate the debug_gnt_o signal itself. The grant is issued unconditionally for most paths, so the debug interface acknowledges the transaction regardless of halt state.
evidence.supports_claim: The missing guard on debug_gnt_o means the debug bus handshake completes even without halt, bypassing the intended protection that restricts debug register access to halted-core state.

evidence.description: debug_rvalid_o is driven as debug_gnt_o delayed by one cycle (line 339). Because debug_gnt_o is asserted without a halt check, debug_rvalid_o will also be asserted for non-halted accesses, completing the read transaction protocol.
evidence.supports_claim: The rvalid completion of debug transactions without halt gating confirms the full debug read/write handshake proceeds without authorization.

evidence.description: debug_halted_o is only asserted (line 386) when the stall FSM is in HALT state. Until that state is reached, any debug_req_i with matching address will receive a grant from the combinational logic in SNIP-0006.
evidence.supports_claim: The permission state (debug_halted_o) is not enforced at the grant level, confirming that debug access is granted before authorization is established.

evidence.description: The debug unit exposes register file read/write (regfile_rreq_o, regfile_wreq_o), CSR read/write (csr_req_o, csr_we_o), NPC jump (jump_req_o), and halt/resume control (dbg_halt, dbg_resume) — all sensitive CPU resources accessible through the debug interface.
evidence.supports_claim: These are security-sensitive resources that should only be accessible when the core is halted under debug authority; the missing grant gate exposes them to unauthorized access.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit combinational logic (lines 139-259) issues debug_gnt_o=1 for most address regions without first requiring debug_halted_o to be asserted. While the internal side-effects (register file write, CSR access, NPC jump) are conditioned on debug_halted_o, the grant signal itself — which completes the debug bus handshake — is not. This means an external debug master can issue requests and receive grants while the CPU is running, and the rvalid response (which is gnt delayed by one cycle) will also be asserted. The 'always accessible' debug register region (addr[13:8]==0) and the default case both grant unconditionally with no halt check at all.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or malicious debug master with access to the debug interface can issue requests and receive acknowledgments without the CPU being halted. Depending on timing and the specific address path, this could allow: reading CPU register state or CSR values while the core is running (information disclosure); triggering halt or resume of the core without proper authorization; or in worst case, corrupting register file or CSR state if the internal operation fires on a race condition. The 'always accessible' debug register region allows unconditional write access to halt control (DBG_CTRL at addr offset 0) without any privilege check.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets confirm the combinational grant logic clearly. Uncertainty remains about whether there is an external access-control wrapper (e.g., in the debug bridge or JTAG TAP) in fc_subsystem or pulp_soc that gates debug_req_i before it reaches zeroriscy_core, which could mitigate this at a higher level. The fc_subsystem snippet (SNIP-0001, SNIP-0002) does not show such a wrapper.

finding.summary: The debug_rdata_o output in zeroriscy_debug_unit exposes CPU register file contents (GPR) and CSR values unconditionally through the debug interface without any privilege/authentication check, and this data is directly connected to the fc_subsystem debug_rdata output port which is externally accessible.

evidence.description: The zeroriscy_debug_unit module exposes debug_rdata_o as an output port (line 44) that can return CSR values (csr_rdata_i) and register file contents (regfile_rdata_i) to any requester. The module accepts debug_req_i from external input with no privilege level or authentication signal anywhere in the port list.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no privilege/authentication input port in zeroriscy_debug_unit; any requestor that drives debug_req_i can read CSRs and GPRs.

evidence.description: Lines 318-329 show that debug_rdata_o is assigned directly from csr_rdata_i (line 323) or regfile_rdata_i (line 324) based purely on rdata_sel_q, with no authentication or privilege guard. Any debug request that sets rdata_sel_q to RD_CSR or RD_GPR will receive sensitive register data.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive CSR and GPR data is unconditionally muxed to debug_rdata_o without any access control check.

evidence.description: In fc_subsystem, zeroriscy_core is instantiated as lFC_CORE and the debug_rdata signal (line 89, SNIP-0001) is connected to the core's debug_rdata_o output, making the unguarded debug readback reachable at the fc_subsystem level.
evidence.supports_claim: The unguarded debug_rdata_o from the CPU core propagates to the fc_subsystem boundary as debug_rdata.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_core exposes debug_rdata_o (line 83) as an output port. This port carries the result from the debug unit, including CSR and GPR contents, with no indication of any access gating at the core boundary.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive CPU state (CSR values, register file contents) is exposed via debug_rdata_o without protection.

evidence.description: On reset, rdata_sel_q is set to RD_NONE (line 426), which means debug_rdata_o defaults to zero. However, once any debug request arrives, rdata_sel_q transitions to RD_CSR, RD_GPR, etc. (line 451) with no authorization check governing the transition.
evidence.supports_claim: The state machine controlling data selection has no authorization gate; reset behavior is safe but runtime operation is not protected.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit provides a debug interface that allows reading CSR values (csr_rdata_i) and general-purpose register file contents (regfile_rdata_i) through the debug_rdata_o output. The read data mux (lines 318-329 of zeroriscy_debug_unit.sv) selects among these sensitive data sources based solely on rdata_sel_q, which is set by any incoming debug request without any authentication, privilege level check, or lifecycle lock. This debug readback path propagates through zeroriscy_core's debug_rdata_o port and ultimately to fc_subsystem's debug_rdata signal, which is externally accessible. No guard logic involving privilege modes, debug authentication tokens, or lifecycle fuses is present in the provided source snippets.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or unprivileged bus master with access to the debug interface can read arbitrary CSR values (including security-sensitive CSRs such as mstatus, mtvec, or custom security registers) and general-purpose register file contents from the CPU core by issuing debug read transactions. This enables information disclosure of CPU architectural state without any authentication barrier.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The chain does not include the top-level SoC interconnect or any external debug access controller that might impose authentication before reaching the debug_req_i input. If a higher-level debug authentication module (e.g., JTAG TAP with authentication) gates debug_req_i before it reaches zeroriscy_debug_unit, the vulnerability severity would be reduced. However, no such gating is visible in the provided source snippets.

finding.summary: The debug interface (debug_req_i) is wired directly from fc_subsystem to zeroriscy_core without any privilege check, lifecycle guard, or lock bit. The debug unit grants access and issues debug_gnt_o unconditionally for several address regions, independent of whether the core is halted or whether an authenticated debug session is in progress.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem declares debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata, debug_gnt, debug_rvalid, and debug_rdata as plain internal logic signals with no associated permission state or lock register.
evidence.supports_claim: No access control or privilege gating surrounds the debug request signals at the subsystem boundary.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem instantiates zeroriscy_core and connects debug_req directly: `.debug_req_i (debug_req)`. There is no intervening guard, lifecycle check, or fuse/strap evaluation shown.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_req signal reaches the CPU core without any permission check.

evidence.description: In zeroriscy_debug_unit, the combinational always_comb block initialises debug_gnt_o to 0 but immediately grants it for multiple address regions. The 6'b00_0000 region ('Debug Registers, always accessible') grants unconditionally without checking debug_halted_o, as does the default case.
evidence.supports_claim: Certain debug register address regions issue a grant regardless of whether the core is halted or the request is authorised.

evidence.description: Lines 234-238 and 241-243 and 255: for the 6'b00_0000 ('Debug Registers, always accessible') region, `debug_gnt_o = 1'b1` is asserted with `rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGA` and no `debug_halted_o` check. Similarly, 6'b10_0000 grants (line 241) and returns RD_DBGS without verifying halt state. The default branch at line 255 also unconditionally grants.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug register reads and the grant handshake are issued without verifying that the core is halted or that the requester holds any privilege.

evidence.description: For CSR access (debug_addr_i[14] set), in the SECOND state the code issues `debug_gnt_o = 1'b1` and sets `csr_we_o = 1'b1` unconditionally (line 156-158), even though the guard `if (debug_halted_o)` that enables csr_req_n was only in the FIRST state. This means the write enable is asserted in SECOND regardless of halt state.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR write enable (csr_we_o) is asserted in the SECOND state without rechecking debug_halted_o, potentially allowing a CSR write even if the halt condition changed.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_core muxes CSR access between normal execution and debug: when dbg_csr_req==1, the debug unit directly controls csr_addr and csr_wdata with csr_op forced to CSR_OP_WRITE. No privilege level check separates debug CSR writes from M-mode-only CSRs.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug unit can write any CSR, including security-sensitive ones (mstatus, mepc), without privilege verification.

evidence.description: core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0 when connecting apb_interrupt_cntrl, meaning the secure mode input is permanently de-asserted. Combined with an always-enabled debug interface, secure-mode protections in the interrupt controller are never active.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure mode is permanently disabled, compounding the lack of debug access controls.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug_req signal flows from fc_subsystem directly into zeroriscy_core and zeroriscy_debug_unit without any authentication, lifecycle, or privilege gate. Inside the debug unit, multiple address regions (6'b00_0000 and default) unconditionally assert debug_gnt_o without checking debug_halted_o. In the two-cycle CSR access flow, csr_we_o is asserted in the SECOND state without rechecking halt status. The core's CSR mux accepts debug CSR writes without any privilege-level filter, and core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 0. Together, these mean any agent able to assert debug_req can read/write internal registers and CSRs without authentication.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or external agent that can assert the debug_req line gains the ability to: (1) read debug registers without halt verification, (2) write arbitrary CSRs including mstatus and mepc, enabling privilege escalation or disabling interrupt enables, and (3) redirect the PC via jump_req. With core_secure_mode_i permanently 0, no secure-mode firewall compensates for these gaps.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of debug_req at higher system levels (e.g., pulp_soc) is not in the provided snippets, so it is unclear whether an upstream gate exists before fc_subsystem. Additionally, the full zeroriscy_debug_unit state machine (lines 260-488) is not shown, so there may be additional guards not captured in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: The zeroriscy debug unit allows write access to certain debug registers (DBG_CTRL, DBG_HIT, DBG_IE at address range 6'b00_0000) without requiring the core to be halted, while a separate register block at 6'b10_0000 (DNPC) correctly gates access behind debug_halted_o. This inconsistency means an attacker with debug interface access can modify halt/resume control and debug settings (single-step, exception-break enables) even when the core is running.

evidence.description: The always_comb block at line 139 checks debug_req_i and debug_we_i. For address range 6'b00_0000 (Debug Registers), the grant is unconditional (debug_gnt_o = 1'b1 at line 164) and writes to settings_n, dbg_halt, dbg_resume are performed without checking debug_halted_o. Only the CSR path (debug_addr_i[14]) and the DNPC register (6'b10_0000) check debug_halted_o before allowing the sensitive operation.
evidence.supports_claim: Writes to DBG_CTRL (halt/resume/single-step) and DBG_IE (exception break enables) are gated only by debug_req_i, not by any privilege or lifecycle check, allowing these to be modified while the core is running.

evidence.description: At line 164, debug_gnt_o is set unconditionally for the 6'b00_0000 address range. Lines 172, 177, 181, 187-190 then perform writes to dbg_halt, dbg_resume, settings_n[DBG_SETS_SSTE/ECALL/ELSU/EBRK/EILL] without any guard on debug_halted_o. In contrast, line 199 wraps the DNPC write inside 'if (debug_halted_o)', demonstrating the design intent to protect sensitive registers but missing the guard for the debug control registers.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard on debug control register writes allows halt/resume and debug settings to be changed by any entity that can assert debug_req_i, regardless of core lifecycle state.

evidence.description: The debug unit exposes a full debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i) that directly controls register file reads/writes, CSR access, jump injection, and core halt/resume via settings_o and jump_req_o outputs.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface has broad authority over the processor, making missing access guards on its control registers a significant security risk.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_core exposes debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i as top-level inputs at lines 77-82 with no visible authentication or privilege gating at the core boundary.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface inputs are directly accessible from outside the core without any hardware-enforced access control, meaning any agent that can drive these signals can trigger the missing-guard vulnerability.

evidence.description: The debug rdata path at lines 272-280 reads back debug_halted_o, settings_q (single-step and exception break enables), and sleeping_i through the debug interface, confirming these are security-sensitive state values.
evidence.supports_claim: The registers that can be written without a halt guard are also readable, confirming they are security-sensitive resources controlling core debug behavior.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy debug unit implements an inconsistent access control policy: the DNPC register correctly requires debug_halted_o before allowing writes, but the DBG_CTRL, DBG_HIT, and DBG_IE registers in the same 6'b00_0000 block grant unconditional write access to any agent asserting debug_req_i. This allows halt/resume control and exception-break enable bits to be modified while the core is actively running, potentially enabling covert debugging, forced halts, or disabling of trap mechanisms without proper privilege checks.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can: (1) forcibly halt or resume the core at arbitrary times via DBG_CTRL without authentication; (2) enable or disable single-step execution and exception-based breakpoints via DBG_IE; (3) clear the single-step hit flag via DBG_HIT. Combined with the lack of any hardware privilege gate on debug_req_i, this represents a complete bypass of core execution control protections.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is unclear from the available snippets whether there is an upstream hardware mechanism (e.g., a lifecycle fuse or SoC-level access control) that prevents unauthorized agents from asserting debug_req_i. If such a mechanism exists and is correctly enforced, the severity is reduced. The fc_subsystem snippet does not show whether debug_req is gated before reaching the core.

finding.summary: Debug register writes to DBG_CTRL (halt/resume/single-step settings) and DBG_IE (exception break settings) are accessible without requiring the core to be halted, allowing an attacker with debug bus access to modify sensitive debug control settings while the core is running.

evidence.description: The debug unit accepts debug_req_i with debug_we_i to write to debug registers. The outer condition at line 139 only checks debug_req_i, with no authentication or privilege check.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug write access is gated only by debug_req_i with no privilege/authentication guard.

evidence.description: At lines 163-194, writes to debug address range 6'b00_0000 (DBG_CTRL, DBG_HIT, DBG_IE registers) are labeled 'always accessible' and proceed with debug_gnt_o=1 unconditionally without checking debug_halted_o. Specifically, settings_n[DBG_SETS_SSTE] (single-step enable), settings_n[DBG_SETS_ECALL/ELSU/EBRK/EILL] (exception break conditions), dbg_halt, dbg_resume, and ssth_clear are all written without requiring the core to be in a halted state.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive debug control settings (single-step, exception triggers, halt/resume) can be modified while the core is actively running, bypassing the halted-state guard that protects other debug register accesses.

evidence.description: The comment at line 163 explicitly states 'Debug Registers, always accessible' for the 6'b00_0000 address range. Compare with lines 196-204 (6'b10_0000 range) and lines 207-213 (GPR range) which both check debug_halted_o before performing sensitive operations, showing an intentional but asymmetric protection policy.
evidence.supports_claim: The guard (debug_halted_o check) is intentionally omitted for DBG_CTRL/DBG_IE/DBG_HIT writes, creating a missing guard vulnerability for those registers.

evidence.description: settings_n[DBG_SETS_SSTE] (single-step enable at line 181), settings_n[DBG_SETS_ECALL/ELSU/EBRK/EILL] (exception intercept bits at lines 187-190), and dbg_halt/dbg_resume (lines 172,177) are sensitive control signals that affect core execution behavior and debug state machine.
evidence.supports_claim: These are security-sensitive control signals that can alter core execution flow and should require a higher privilege or halted-state check before modification.

evidence.description: debug_wdata is declared as a plain logic signal in fc_subsystem at line 86 with no visible access control or qualification before being passed to the core.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug write data path originates as an unguarded signal in fc_subsystem and is forwarded directly to the core's debug interface.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_core exposes debug_wdata_i as a direct input port (line 82) alongside debug_we_i and debug_req_i, forming the debug write path that feeds into the debug unit without any core-level permission filtering.
evidence.supports_claim: The core passes debug write data directly to the debug unit with no intermediate permission check at the core boundary.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit implements a two-tier access policy: some debug registers (GPR writes, DNPC jumps, CSR reads/writes) correctly require debug_halted_o to be asserted before taking effect, but the 'always accessible' debug registers (DBG_CTRL, DBG_HIT, DBG_IE at address range 6'b00_0000) can be written at any time via the debug interface. This means an entity with debug bus access can enable single-step mode, configure which exceptions trigger a debug halt, issue halt/resume commands, and clear the single-step hit flag while the CPU is actively executing code. The debug_wdata signal flows unguarded from fc_subsystem through zeroriscy_core into zeroriscy_debug_unit where these writes are accepted unconditionally.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug bus can manipulate core execution behavior (force halt, enable single-stepping, configure exception intercepts) without the core being in a halted/stopped state. This could enable privilege escalation, denial of service, or covert extraction of execution state information by engineering specific exception conditions to trigger debug halts at chosen points during secure code execution.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The chain context does not show what entity controls the debug bus (debug_req_i, debug_we_i, debug_addr_i, debug_wdata_i) at the system level, so it is unknown whether external access to the debug interface is itself gated by a higher-level access control mechanism (e.g., a lifecycle fuse or JTAG authentication). If the debug bus is already protected at the system boundary, the missing guard within the debug unit would be a defense-in-depth issue rather than a direct vulnerability.

finding.summary: Debug write access to debug control registers (DBG_CTRL, DBG_HIT, DBG_IE) in zeroriscy_debug_unit is permitted without requiring the core to be halted, allowing an active (non-halted) core's debug settings, halt/resume state, and single-step configuration to be manipulated through the debug interface.

evidence.description: The debug write path begins at line 139: if (debug_req_i) begin / if (debug_we_i) begin — any external entity asserting debug_req_i and debug_we_i can enter the write controller without any privilege or authentication check.
evidence.supports_claim: No authentication or privilege guard exists at the top of the debug write path.

evidence.description: Lines 163–193 (case 6'b00_0000, 'Debug Registers, always accessible'): debug_gnt_o is unconditionally set to 1 and writes to DBG_CTRL (halt/resume via dbg_halt/dbg_resume, single-step via settings_n[DBG_SETS_SSTE]), DBG_HIT (ssth_clear), and DBG_IE (settings_n[DBG_SETS_ECALL/ELSU/EBRK/EILL]) are performed without checking debug_halted_o. The comment 'always accessible' confirms this is intentional but constitutes a missing halted-core guard for sensitive control operations like asserting halt or modifying exception-trigger settings.
evidence.supports_claim: Writes to halt control, single-step, and exception-trigger settings proceed without requiring debug_halted_o, unlike CSR and GPR write paths which do check debug_halted_o.

evidence.description: By contrast, lines 151–153 (CSR write) and lines 199–212 (DNPC jump and GPR write) gate their write effects behind 'if (debug_halted_o)'. The absence of this guard for the 6'b00_0000 block is inconsistent and exposes control registers to modification while the core is running.
evidence.supports_claim: The CSR and GPR write paths have halted-core guards; the debug control register write path does not, showing an inconsistent protection model.

evidence.description: settings_n[DBG_SETS_SSTE] (single-step enable), settings_n[DBG_SETS_ECALL/ELSU/EBRK/EILL] (exception-triggered debug entry), dbg_halt, dbg_resume, and ssth_clear are all security-sensitive debug control state written in the unguarded block.
evidence.supports_claim: These registers control core execution mode, breakpoint behavior, and halt/resume lifecycle — sensitive resources that should require the core to be halted before modification.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem.sv declares debug_we as a plain logic signal (line 85) with no gating or permission check before being connected to zeroriscy_core.debug_we_i (line 220, SNIP-0002), which feeds directly into the debug unit's debug_we_i input.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug write-enable signal originates from fc_subsystem with no access control wrapper, enabling the unguarded debug register writes.

evidence.description: csr_op is derived from dbg_csr_we (line 616): 'dbg_csr_we == 1'b1 ? CSR_OP_WRITE : CSR_OP_NONE'. While CSR writes themselves require debug_halted_o, the ability to halt the core (dbg_halt) without that guard means an attacker can force the core into halted state and then proceed with further privileged debug accesses.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthorized assertion of dbg_halt via the unguarded debug register write path can force the processor into halted mode, enabling subsequent privileged debug operations.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit implements two tiers of debug write access: 'always accessible' debug control registers (DBG_CTRL, DBG_HIT, DBG_IE at address offset 6'b00_0000) and 'only accessible when halted' registers (CSR, DNPC, GPR). The always-accessible group omits the debug_halted_o guard that is consistently applied to the other groups. This allows any entity with access to the debug interface (debug_req_i + debug_we_i) to: (1) assert dbg_halt forcing the core to stop, (2) modify single-step enable (DBG_SETS_SSTE), (3) configure which exceptions trigger debug entry. The fc_subsystem connects debug_we without any upstream access control. The inconsistency between the guarded and unguarded write paths, confirmed by source code, constitutes a concrete permission violation.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug bus can halt the processor without authorization, enable single-stepping, and configure exception-triggered debug traps while the core is running — potentially enabling denial-of-service, covert observation of execution, or facilitating further privileged debug register access after forcing the core into halted state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets do not show what drives debug_req_i and debug_we_i at the SoC level (e.g., whether there is a higher-level access-control filter on the debug bus). If such a filter exists upstream of fc_subsystem, the impact would be reduced. However, within the RTL provided, no such guard is visible.

finding.summary: The SLEEP state wakes the CPU on any raw IRQ signal (irq_i) without checking interrupt enable (m_IE_i / irq_enable_int), allowing an interrupt to resume execution even when interrupts are globally disabled in the CSR.

evidence.description: In the SLEEP FSM state, the transition back to FIRST_FETCH is triggered solely by `irq_i` (line 254), with no check against `irq_enable_int` or `m_IE_i`. This bypasses the machine-mode interrupt enable gate that is correctly applied in FIRST_FETCH and DECODE states.
evidence.supports_claim: An interrupt can wake the CPU from WFI sleep even when the global interrupt enable bit (MIE in mstatus) is cleared, violating the RISC-V privilege specification which requires interrupts to be enabled before they can cause a trap or wake from WFI in M-mode.

evidence.description: In FIRST_FETCH state (line 270), the IRQ transition to IRQ_TAKEN correctly gates on `irq_req_ctrl_i & irq_enable_int`, showing that `irq_enable_int` is the intended guard. The SLEEP state omits this guard.
evidence.supports_claim: The guard `irq_enable_int` exists and is used in other states but is absent in the SLEEP state wake-up condition, confirming the omission is a vulnerability rather than an intentional design.

evidence.description: In DECODE state (line 316), IRQ acceptance also requires `irq_req_ctrl_i & irq_enable_int`, further confirming the pattern that interrupt enable must be checked before acting on an interrupt.
evidence.supports_claim: Consistent use of irq_enable_int in FIRST_FETCH and DECODE but not in SLEEP demonstrates the missing guard in SLEEP is a security/correctness defect.

evidence.description: `irq_enable_int` is declared at line 127 as the internal interrupt enable signal derived from CSR state (m_IE_i), representing the architectural interrupt enable permission.
evidence.supports_claim: irq_enable_int is the correct permission signal that should gate interrupt-driven state transitions, including wake from sleep.

evidence.description: The IRQ_TAKEN state (line 378-390) sets csr_save_cause_o, csr_cause_o, csr_save_if_o, and redirects the PC to the exception vector. If reached via the unguarded SLEEP wake-up path, it will overwrite mepc and mcause CSRs and redirect execution to the interrupt handler without privilege authorization.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthorized wake from sleep leads to CSR state corruption (mepc, mcause overwritten) and unauthorized transfer of control to the interrupt handler, constituting a privilege/permission violation.

finding.reasoning_summary: The controller FSM has three places where an IRQ can cause a state transition: SLEEP, FIRST_FETCH, and DECODE. In FIRST_FETCH and DECODE, the transition is gated by `irq_enable_int` (which reflects the MIE bit from mstatus CSR). In SLEEP, the wake-up condition at line 254 checks only the raw `irq_i` signal with no enable gate. This means a pending interrupt will unconditionally wake the CPU from WFI and proceed through IRQ_TAKEN, overwriting mepc/mcause and jumping to the interrupt vector, regardless of whether interrupts are enabled in the CSR. This violates the RISC-V specification requirement that WFI may resume but should not take the interrupt trap if MIE=0 (though the exact behavior is implementation-defined, the subsequent IRQ_TAKEN path does perform a full trap including CSR save, which is incorrect when MIE=0).
finding.security_impact: An attacker or malicious peripheral that can assert an interrupt line can force the CPU out of a sleep state and redirect execution to the interrupt handler even when the software has disabled interrupts (MIE=0). This corrupts the saved exception PC (mepc) and cause register (mcause), potentially hijacking control flow or disrupting security-critical sleep/low-power sequences that rely on interrupt masking.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact definition of `irq_enable_int` (whether it is purely m_IE_i or a combination) is not shown in the snippets, but the pattern of its use in FIRST_FETCH and DECODE versus its absence in SLEEP is clear from the source. The RISC-V spec allows implementation-defined WFI behavior, but the subsequent full trap path (IRQ_TAKEN with CSR save) makes this a concrete functional and security defect.

finding.summary: The MRET instruction (csr_restore_mret_id_o) restores machine-mode context from CSR mepc/mstatus without verifying that the current privilege level is actually M-mode, allowing an unprivileged or lower-privilege context to execute MRET and potentially escalate privileges or corrupt exception return state.

evidence.description: The combinational FSM defaults csr_restore_mret_id_o to 1'b0 but there is no privilege-level check or MPP field verification before it can be set. The signal is driven in the FLUSH state based solely on mret_insn_i without any privilege guard.
evidence.supports_claim: No privilege check guards the assertion of csr_restore_mret_id_o.

evidence.description: In the DECODE state, when mret_insn_i is asserted (along with ecall, pipe_flush, ebrk, illegal, or csr_status), the FSM unconditionally transitions to FLUSH with halt_if_o and halt_id_o asserted. There is no check that the core is currently executing in M-mode (e.g., no check of mstatus.MPP or current privilege level) before accepting the MRET instruction and ultimately restoring supervisor context.
evidence.supports_claim: MRET is accepted and processed from DECODE state without any privilege level guard.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_cs_registers receives csr_restore_mret_i and restores mepc/mstatus including MIE from MPIE. The MPP bits in MSTATUS (bits 12:11) represent the privilege level to return to, but there is no hardware enforcement that MRET was issued from M-mode.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR restore on MRET updates privilege-controlling registers (mstatus MIE/MPIE/MPP and mepc) without a privilege guard upstream.

evidence.description: The controller module port list shows mret_insn_i as a plain input with no associated privilege_level_i or mode_i input that would enable the FSM to gate MRET on current privilege. The only relevant CSR input is csr_status_i (a single bit indicating a CSR status instruction) which does not carry privilege mode information.
evidence.supports_claim: The controller has no privilege-mode input to condition MRET handling.

evidence.description: The debug unit can directly write CSRs (dbg_csr_req, dbg_csr_we, dbg_csr_wdata mux) without going through the normal privilege-check path, and the csr_addr/csr_wdata mux bypasses normal instruction-path checks when dbg_csr_req==1. This provides an additional vector to manipulate privilege-related CSRs (mepc, mstatus) outside the normal permission model.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug CSR write path bypasses normal privilege enforcement for CSR access.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_controller processes the MRET instruction by detecting mret_insn_i in the DECODE state and transitioning to FLUSH, which ultimately asserts csr_restore_mret_id_o. This causes the CS-register module to restore mepc into PC and restore mstatus (re-enabling MIE from MPIE, and potentially changing privilege level via MPP). However, no source-code evidence exists of any privilege-level check before this path is taken: the controller has no privilege_mode input, and the FLUSH state handling does not condition csr_restore_mret_id_o on the current execution privilege. Additionally, the debug unit provides a direct CSR write path that bypasses normal instruction-path privilege checks, allowing manipulation of mepc and mstatus independently.
finding.security_impact: An attacker executing code in a lower privilege mode (U-mode, if implemented) could issue an MRET instruction to restore M-mode context (mepc, mstatus.MPP→M-mode) and gain machine-level privilege. Even without U-mode, the debug interface can arbitrarily manipulate mepc and mstatus CSRs to engineer a privilege escalation scenario on the next MRET. This could lead to complete compromise of the RISC-V privilege model.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full FLUSH state handling code is not shown in snippets (only the transition into FLUSH from DECODE is visible in SNIP-0008). It is possible that additional privilege checks exist in the FLUSH state or in zeroriscy_cs_registers when csr_restore_mret_i is asserted, but no such evidence appears in any provided snippet. The zeroriscy_cs_registers snippet (SNIP-0018) only shows the port list; the register update logic is not shown. The core also lacks explicit U-mode support signals in these snippets, which would affect exploitability.

finding.summary: The debug unit can directly write CSR registers and redirect the program counter without any privilege or authentication check, allowing an external debug interface to arbitrarily manipulate processor state including CSR saves, exception cause registers, and execution flow.

evidence.description: CSR address, write data, and operation are multiplexed between normal pipeline operation and debug unit requests using only dbg_csr_req as a selector: 'assign csr_addr = (dbg_csr_req == 1'b0) ? csr_addr_int : dbg_csr_addr; assign csr_wdata = (dbg_csr_req == 1'b0) ? alu_operand_a_ex : dbg_csr_wdata;'. There is no privilege level check or authentication before the debug unit can write any CSR.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit can unconditionally overwrite any CSR (including mcause, mepc, mstatus) without privilege verification.

evidence.description: The debug unit exposes direct CSR read/write ports (csr_req_o, csr_addr_o, csr_we_o, csr_wdata_o, csr_rdata_i), register file read/write ports, and a jump_req_o to redirect PC, all controlled only by debug_req_i with no authentication or privilege gate visible in the interface.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit interface provides unconstrained access to CSRs and register file from the external debug interface.

evidence.description: In DBG_WAIT state, when dbg_jump_req_i is asserted, the controller unconditionally sets pc_mux_o = PC_DBG_NPC and pc_set_o = 1'b1 with no privilege or authentication check: 'if (dbg_jump_req_i) begin pc_mux_o = PC_DBG_NPC; pc_set_o = 1'b1;'
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit can redirect execution to any arbitrary address without privilege verification.

evidence.description: In the RESET state, a debug request (dbg_req_i) immediately transitions the FSM to DBG_SIGNAL even before fetch_enable is asserted: 'else if (dbg_req_i) begin ctrl_fsm_ns = DBG_SIGNAL; end'. This allows debug takeover before normal boot completes, with no authentication check.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug can intercept the processor before it has completed its secure boot sequence.

evidence.description: csr_save_cause_o is defaulted to 1'b0 and set to 1'b1 during IRQ_TAKEN and exception handling. This signal gates whether the exception cause is written to the CSR. If the debug unit can manipulate CSR state directly, exception cause records can be corrupted.
evidence.supports_claim: The csr_save_cause_o controlled exception state can be tampered with via the unrestricted debug CSR write path.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippets show that the debug unit (zeroriscy_debug_unit) is connected to the CSR register file through a multiplexer in zeroriscy_core that selects between normal pipeline CSR operations and debug-initiated CSR operations solely based on dbg_csr_req, with no privilege level check, authentication, or lock mechanism. The debug unit also directly controls PC redirection via jump_req_o/dbg_jump_req_i and can halt/stall the CPU. Furthermore, a debug request can take over the CPU even during the RESET state before normal execution begins.
