evidence.description: In the HALT_REQ state, the FSM transitions to RUNNING if (dbg_resume | debug_resume_i) is asserted, regardless of whether dbg_ack_i has been received. This means a resume signal can abort the halt handshake before the core has acknowledged the debug request, potentially leaving debug state inconsistent.
evidence.supports_claim: The HALT_REQ -> RUNNING transition via dbg_resume/debug_resume_i has no guard requiring dbg_ack_i, allowing the halt sequence to be aborted before the core acknowledges it.

evidence.description: In the SECOND state of the CSR write path, csr_we_o is set to 1'b1 and debug_gnt_o is set to 1'b1 unconditionally, without re-checking debug_halted_o. The guard 'if (debug_halted_o)' only applies in FIRST state (line 151), so the write enable fires in the SECOND cycle even if the core is no longer halted (e.g., resumed between cycles).
evidence.supports_claim: CSR writes via the debug interface can proceed in the second cycle without verifying that the core is still halted, bypassing the halt-before-access protection.

evidence.description: For the RD_DBGS read path (address 6'b10_0000), debug_gnt_o is granted with comment 'grant it even when invalid access to not block', and rdata_sel_n is set to RD_DBGS without checking debug_halted_o. Debug status registers meant to be accessible only when halted are thus readable regardless of halted state.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug-only registers (RD_DBGS) are granted and their readback selector set without verifying debug_halted_o, allowing sensitive debug state readback without proper halt guard.

evidence.description: The settings_n fields (DBG_SETS_SSTE, DBG_SETS_ECALL, DBG_SETS_ELSU, DBG_SETS_EBRK, DBG_SETS_EILL) are written based on debug_wdata_i without checking debug_halted_o for the DBG_IE register case, allowing modification of debug exception trigger settings from the debug interface at any time.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug exception enable settings can be modified without halting the core first, potentially altering security-relevant trap behavior while the core is executing.

evidence.description: The debug halt is triggered by dbg_halt | debug_halt_i | trap_i with no authentication or privilege check on the debug_halt_i input, and the stall/halt control is fully driven by external signals that are accepted unconditionally.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug halt mechanism has no authentication guard; any external asserter of debug_halt_i can halt the core.

finding.reasoning_summary: The chain shows the debug unit (zeroriscy_debug_unit) managing halt/resume of the core. Two concrete violations are present in the source snippets: (1) In SNIP-0008, the CSR write enable (csr_we_o) is asserted in the SECOND cycle unconditionally, while the debug_halted_o guard only applies in the FIRST cycle - meaning a write can proceed if the core resumed between the two cycles. (2) In SNIP-0010, the RD_DBGS selector is set without a debug_halted_o guard, exposing debug-state registers outside of halt. (3) In SNIP-0012, the HALT_REQ state permits a resume escape without dbg_ack_i, creating a race where the core may never fully halt. These collectively represent missing or bypassed permission guards in the debug access path.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can: (a) read debug-internal state registers without halting the core; (b) write CSRs through the debug interface even if the core resumes between the two-cycle handshake, potentially corrupting machine state; (c) abort the halt handshake via resume before the core acknowledges, leaving the debug unit and core in inconsistent states that may bypass debug-mode protections.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full resolution of the SECOND-cycle CSR write guard depends on timing of debug_halted_o being a registered or combinational signal, which is not fully visible in these snippets. Also, the threat model for who can assert debug_halt_i and debug_resume_i externally is not shown. The exact security boundary (trusted vs untrusted debug master) is not explicit in these snippets.

finding.summary: The debug unit CSR mux in zeroriscy_core allows the debug interface to unconditionally override CSR access (address, write-data, and operation) without any privilege or lifecycle guard, and the apb_interrupt_cntrl permanently asserts core_clock_en and fetch_en regardless of any security state.

evidence.description: Lines 612-617 of zeroriscy_core.sv show that when dbg_csr_req is asserted, csr_access is forced to 1'b1, csr_addr is replaced with dbg_csr_addr, csr_wdata is replaced with dbg_csr_wdata, and csr_op is forced to CSR_OP_WRITE — all without any privilege check, lifecycle lock, or authentication guard. Any agent that can assert debug_req_i and drive the debug interface can read or write any CSR.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard on debug-path CSR access allows unprivileged CSR read/write via debug interface.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_core exposes debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, and debug_halt_i as plain top-level inputs with no access-control qualification shown in the port list or the core instantiation.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug interface inputs are architecturally exposed with no gating at the core boundary.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem instantiates zeroriscy_core and connects fc_subsystem.debug_req directly to zeroriscy_core.debug_req_i with no intervening permission check visible in the snippet.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_req is wired directly from subsystem to core without a guard.

evidence.description: The debug unit writes DBG_SETS_SSTE, DBG_SETS_ECALL, DBG_SETS_ELSU, DBG_SETS_EBRK, and DBG_SETS_EILL from debug_wdata_i. The guard at line 199 ('if (debug_halted_o)') applies only to the 'Debug Registers' region (6'b10_0000); the settings region (lines 181-191) has no such halted check, meaning debug exception-trigger settings can be modified even when the core is not halted.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug settings registers are writable without requiring the core to be in a halted/authenticated debug state.

evidence.description: Lines 85-86 of apb_interrupt_cntrl.sv unconditionally assign core_clock_en_o = 1'b1 and fetch_en_o = 1'b1. These signals cannot be deasserted by any security policy, lifecycle state, or permission check, meaning clock-enable and fetch-enable protections are permanently bypassed.
evidence.supports_claim: core_clock_en and fetch_en are hardwired to 1, eliminating any ability to gate the core through the interrupt controller's security-relevant outputs.

evidence.description: Line 283 of fc_subsystem.sv passes core_secure_mode_i = 1'b0 (hardwired) to apb_interrupt_cntrl, permanently disabling secure-mode signalling regardless of actual system security state.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure mode is permanently disabled, defeating any secure-interrupt or secure-mode permission distinction in the interrupt controller.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two distinct violations are present in source code. First, in zeroriscy_core.sv the CSR mux (SNIP-0017) unconditionally routes debug-supplied address, write-data, and write-enable to the CSR file whenever dbg_csr_req is high, with no privilege level check, lifecycle guard, or authentication requirement visible anywhere in the chain. The debug interface inputs are connected without guards (SNIP-0008, SNIP-0012). Second, the debug unit's exception-trigger settings (DBG_SETS_* in SNIP-0022) lack the 'debug_halted_o' guard that protects only the higher address region, allowing those settings to be modified freely. Additionally, apb_interrupt_cntrl hardwires core_clock_en and fetch_en to 1 (SNIP-0001) and fc_subsystem hardwires core_secure_mode to 0 (SNIP-0009), permanently defeating two additional protection mechanisms.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface (e.g., via JTAG or an untrusted debug bus master) can: (1) read or overwrite any CSR including privilege-control registers (mstatus, mepc, mcause) without being in a privileged mode; (2) configure exception-trap-into-debug settings without halting the core; (3) benefit from permanently enabled clock and fetch that cannot be blocked through the interrupt controller. Combined, these allow privilege escalation, arbitrary CSR corruption, and persistent core execution that cannot be halted via the intended secure-mode path.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The debug bus master's own access-control logic (upstream of fc_subsystem) is not present in this chain, so it is possible (but not shown) that an outer wrapper gates debug_req. The zeroriscy_debug_unit's full authentication or lifecycle state machine is only partially shown; however, the CSR mux bypass in SNIP-0017 and the hardwired constants in SNIP-0001/SNIP-0009 are unconditional in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: The debug unit can directly access and write any CSR without privilege or mode checks. The CSR access multiplexer in zeroriscy_core unconditionally routes debug CSR requests (dbg_csr_req, dbg_csr_addr, dbg_csr_we, dbg_csr_wdata) to the CSR register file, bypassing any privilege-level validation or runtime permission guard that would otherwise protect sensitive registers such as mstatus, mepc, and mcause.

evidence.description: Lines 612-617 show a pure combinational mux that, when dbg_csr_req==1, unconditionally drives csr_access=1, csr_addr=dbg_csr_addr, csr_wdata=dbg_csr_wdata, and csr_op=CSR_OP_WRITE (if dbg_csr_we==1). There is no privilege-level check, no lock, and no mode guard on the debug path before it reaches the CSR register file.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface bypasses all privilege/protection checks on CSR access.

evidence.description: Signals dbg_csr_req, dbg_csr_addr, dbg_csr_we, and dbg_csr_wdata are declared as plain internal logics with no gating or authentication wrapper; they flow directly into the CSR mux.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug CSR control signals are architecturally unguarded internal wires.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_cs_registers exposes mstatus (mie, mpie, mpp), mepc, mcause, and all performance-counter CSRs via csr_addr_i/csr_wdata_i/csr_op_i without any privilege enforcement inside the register file itself; protection is expected from the caller.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive architectural state (interrupt enable, exception return address, machine cause) is reachable through the unguarded debug path.

evidence.description: The zeroriscy_core module exposes debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, and debug_wdata_i as top-level input ports with no indication of external authentication before assertion.
evidence.supports_claim: An external agent that can assert debug_req_i can trigger the unguarded CSR write path.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem instantiates zeroriscy_core and connects the debug_req signal from an unguarded internal wire, meaning any subsystem component that drives debug_req can trigger the bypass.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug request reaches the core without interposing a privilege or authentication check at the subsystem boundary.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0016 is the core evidence: the CSR-access multiplexer at lines 612-617 of zeroriscy_core.sv selects between the normal pipeline path and the debug path purely based on dbg_csr_req, with no privilege-level, lock, or lifecycle check on the debug side. Because the CSR register file (zeroriscy_cs_registers) performs no independent privilege enforcement and relies on its caller to supply only authorised accesses, any entity that can assert dbg_csr_req and supply an arbitrary dbg_csr_addr can read or write any CSR, including machine-mode interrupt control and exception-return registers.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can inject a debug request (e.g., via a compromised debug host, a JTAG interface left open in production, or a rogue on-chip bus master) can arbitrarily overwrite mstatus, mepc, mcause, and other privileged CSRs, enabling privilege escalation, disabling interrupts, redirecting exception return addresses, or corrupting performance-counter configuration without any software-visible protection mechanism.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet set does not include the debug unit instantiation that drives dbg_csr_req, so it is not possible to confirm whether the debug unit itself enforces an authentication protocol before asserting the request. If a robust external authentication layer exists in the debug unit, the practical exploitability would be reduced, but the structural bypass in the CSR mux remains a design-level violation regardless.

finding.summary: The debug unit can directly access and overwrite any CSR without privilege or halt-state verification. The mux logic in zeroriscy_core.sv unconditionally routes dbg_csr_req, dbg_csr_addr, dbg_csr_we, and dbg_csr_wdata to the CSR register file whenever dbg_csr_req is asserted, bypassing all normal instruction-path privilege checks.

evidence.description: Lines 612-617 of zeroriscy_core.sv show that when dbg_csr_req==1, csr_access is forced to 1'b1, csr_addr is taken from dbg_csr_addr, csr_wdata from dbg_csr_wdata, and csr_op is set to CSR_OP_WRITE (or CSR_OP_NONE). There is no guard checking that the core is halted, that the debug unit is authenticated, or that the target CSR address is accessible at the current privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug-unit CSR access path bypasses all privilege and halt-state checks, allowing arbitrary CSR reads/writes via dbg_csr_req.

evidence.description: dbg_csr_req, dbg_csr_addr, dbg_csr_we, and dbg_csr_wdata are declared as plain internal logic signals in zeroriscy_core.sv with no protection state associated with them. They are driven directly by the debug unit block without any intervening authorization check visible in this file.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug CSR interface signals are unguarded internal wires, confirming no inline permission gate exists at the point of use.

evidence.description: The debug interface ports (debug_req_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_addr_i) are exposed as top-level inputs of zeroriscy_core with no privilege-level qualifier, meaning any external agent asserting debug_req_i can trigger the debug CSR path.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug request is accepted from an external input with no hardware-enforced authentication or privilege check at the module boundary.

evidence.description: In fc_subsystem.sv the zeroriscy_core is instantiated and debug_req is wired directly from the subsystem-level debug_req signal to debug_req_i of the core. No gating condition, lifecycle check, or security-mode interlock is applied before this connection.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug request reaches the core without any system-level privilege gate, confirming the bypass is end-to-end.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_cs_registers.sv exposes the full CSR register file (including mstatus, mepc, mcause, and performance counters) via the csr_access_i / csr_addr_i / csr_wdata_i / csr_op_i interface. These registers control interrupt enables, exception return addresses, and privilege state, making unauthorized write access security-critical.
evidence.supports_claim: The CSR register file targeted by the unguarded debug path contains security-sensitive machine-mode state.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0016 provides direct source-code proof that the debug unit unconditionally overrides the normal CSR access mux (csr_access forced to 1, address/data/op taken from debug signals) when dbg_csr_req is high. No halt-state, privilege-level, or authentication check gates this path anywhere in the snippets. SNIP-0007 confirms the external debug_req signal reaches the core unguarded. SNIP-0017 confirms the targeted resource (the CSR file) holds security-critical machine-mode state. Together these form a complete permission-bypass chain.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or untrusted debug agent that can assert debug_req_i and supply arbitrary dbg_csr_addr / dbg_csr_wdata values can read or write any CSR—including mstatus (privilege mode), mepc (exception return address), and interrupt-enable bits—without the core being in a halted or authenticated debug state. This can escalate privilege, disable interrupts, or redirect exception handling to attacker-controlled code.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The debug unit module itself (zeroriscy_debug_unit.sv) is not included in the snippets, so it is not possible to verify whether the unit itself performs an internal authentication or halt check before asserting dbg_csr_req. If such a check exists inside the debug unit it would mitigate the risk, but the core-level mux provides no such protection, which is itself an architectural weakness.

finding.summary: The debug unit can directly write arbitrary data to any CSR without privilege or mode checks, bypassing normal instruction-path access controls.

evidence.description: Lines 612-617 in zeroriscy_core.sv show that when dbg_csr_req is asserted, csr_access is forced to 1'b1, csr_addr is taken from dbg_csr_addr, csr_wdata is taken directly from dbg_csr_wdata, and csr_op is forced to CSR_OP_WRITE (when dbg_csr_we is set). There is no privilege-level check, no mode guard, and no lifecycle check protecting this path. Any agent that can drive debug_req_i can issue arbitrary CSR writes.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface path overrides normal CSR access arbitration with no authorization or permission guard beyond dbg_csr_req being asserted.

evidence.description: Lines 212-216 declare dbg_csr_req, dbg_csr_addr, dbg_csr_we, and dbg_csr_wdata as unguarded internal signals driven from the debug unit and fed directly into the CSR mux.
evidence.supports_claim: These signals form the unauthenticated input path to the CSR write mux.

evidence.description: The debug interface ports (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i) are exposed as top-level module inputs with no authentication gating shown in the port declarations.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface is a top-level entry point with no visible authentication or lifecycle restriction at the port boundary.

evidence.description: The debug register block (6'b10_0000) at line 196 checks debug_halted_o before responding, but the CSR write path through dbg_csr_req shown in SNIP-0016 does not use this halted guard—the mux fires on dbg_csr_req alone.
evidence.supports_claim: The only guard that exists (debug_halted_o check) is in the debug register sub-block, not on the CSR write mux, leaving the CSR write path unprotected by halt-state verification.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_cs_registers receives csr_wdata_i, csr_addr_i, and csr_op_i from zeroriscy_core with no internal privilege check—it trusts the inputs completely, making privileged CSRs (mstatus, mepc, mcause) writable via the debug path.
evidence.supports_claim: Privileged CSRs are the protected resource reachable through the unguarded debug CSR write path.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0016 provides direct source evidence that the CSR access mux in zeroriscy_core unconditionally routes debug-sourced address and write-data into the CSR register file whenever dbg_csr_req is asserted, with csr_op forced to CSR_OP_WRITE. There is no privilege-level check, no lifecycle (boot/production) lock, and no halt-state requirement on this mux path. The debug interface inputs are exposed at the top-level module boundary (SNIP-0011). The debug register sub-block does gate some operations behind debug_halted_o (SNIP-0020), but this guard does not cover the dbg_csr_req mux path. As a result, any entity that can drive the debug interface can overwrite arbitrary CSRs including mstatus, mepc, and mcause.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can overwrite privileged CSRs (e.g., mstatus to escalate privilege mode, mepc to redirect exception return, mcause to falsify exception records) without requiring the core to be halted or any authentication step, enabling privilege escalation and control-flow hijack.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not shown in the provided snippets whether any external SoC-level access control (e.g., life-cycle controller or debug authentication module) gates the debug_req_i signal before it reaches fc_subsystem/zeroriscy_core. If such a gate exists upstream, severity would be reduced. However, no such gate is visible in the provided source_snippets.

finding.summary: Debug CSR write enable (csr_we_o) is asserted unconditionally in the SECOND state of the debug unit FSM regardless of whether the core is halted, allowing a potential CSR write to proceed without the required halt guard.

evidence.description: In the SECOND FSM state for CSR write access (debug_addr_i[14] high), csr_we_o is set to 1'b1 at line 158 without any check on debug_halted_o. The halt guard only applies to csr_req_n in the FIRST state (line 151-154), but the actual write-enable output is asserted in SECOND unconditionally.
evidence.supports_claim: csr_we_o is driven high in the SECOND state without verifying that the core is halted, meaning a CSR write via the debug interface can be enabled even if the core was not halted when the request was processed.

evidence.description: The debug unit declares csr_we_o as an output to the core and initializes it to 1'b0 at the top of the always_comb block, but the halt check for CSR access is only applied to csr_req_n in FIRST, not to csr_we_o in SECOND.
evidence.supports_claim: The guard (debug_halted_o check) is incomplete: it gates csr_req_n but not the subsequent csr_we_o assertion.

evidence.description: The CSR mux in zeroriscy_core selects debug CSR write path when dbg_csr_req==1 and asserts CSR_OP_WRITE when dbg_csr_we==1, directly writing to the CSR register file. This path is driven by the debug unit's csr_we_o output.
evidence.supports_claim: An unguarded csr_we_o assertion from the debug unit results in an actual CSR write operation to the processor's control/status registers.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_cs_registers accepts csr_op_i and csr_wdata_i without any privilege or lifecycle check of its own; protection is expected from the caller (the core's debug mux and debug unit).
evidence.supports_claim: CSR registers (including machine-mode privilege and interrupt registers) are written based solely on the debug unit's signals, making the missing halt guard a direct path to unauthorized CSR modification.

evidence.description: The debug interface signals (debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata) are declared as internal wires in fc_subsystem and driven externally, providing the entry point for the debug access chain.
evidence.supports_claim: External debug inputs flow directly into the core's debug unit without any access control at the fc_subsystem boundary.

finding.reasoning_summary: In zeroriscy_debug_unit, the FSM handles CSR write requests in two cycles. In the FIRST state, csr_req_n is only asserted if debug_halted_o is true (line 151-154 of SNIP-0014). However, in the SECOND state, csr_we_o is set to 1'b1 unconditionally (line 158) without re-checking debug_halted_o. Because csr_req_n is registered as csr_req_q and then driven as csr_req_o, if a race or glitch causes the FSM to reach SECOND with csr_req_q set but the core no longer halted, or if the FIRST-state check is bypassed, the write-enable will fire. More directly, the write-enable in SECOND has no independent halt guard, creating an asymmetry in protection. The resulting csr_we_o drives dbg_csr_we in zeroriscy_core, which selects CSR_OP_WRITE in the CSR mux, causing an unprotected write to the processor's CSR register file.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or faulty debug host could write arbitrary values to machine-mode CSRs (including mstatus, mepc, mcause) without the processor being in the required halted debug state. This could be used to escalate privilege, disable interrupts, corrupt exception handling, or redirect execution.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact timing relationship between state_q transitions and csr_req_q registration is not fully captured in the snippets. It is possible the combinational path always ensures csr_req_n=0 propagates before SECOND is reached without halt, but the source shows no re-check of debug_halted_o in the SECOND state for csr_we_o, which is the structural violation. Full simulation or formal proof would be needed to confirm exploitability.

finding.summary: The debug unit's jump_addr_o (dbg_jump_addr) can redirect the CPU program counter via PC_DBG_NPC without verifying that the core is in a halted/debug state at the point of PC mux selection, and the debug interface accepts requests and grants them without a privilege or authentication check, allowing any entity with access to the debug interface to redirect execution.

evidence.description: In the IF stage fetch address mux, PC_DBG_NPC unconditionally takes fetch_addr_n from dbg_jump_addr_i with no guard checking debug_halted_o or any privilege condition at this mux selection point.
evidence.supports_claim: The CPU program counter can be redirected to an arbitrary debug-supplied address (dbg_jump_addr_i) via PC_DBG_NPC without a halt-state guard at the mux itself.

evidence.description: The debug unit exposes jump_addr_o and jump_req_o as outputs directly controllable through the debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_we_i) with no privilege level check on the incoming debug port signals.
evidence.supports_claim: Any agent driving the debug interface signals can issue a jump request and supply an arbitrary jump address to the CPU's IF stage.

evidence.description: The debug register write path at address 6'b10_0000 checks debug_halted_o before acting on internal debug registers, but the jump_req and jump_addr path does not consistently enforce halt-state at the mux level in the IF stage.
evidence.supports_claim: The halt-state guard exists for some debug register accesses but is not enforced for the PC_DBG_NPC mux selection in the IF stage.

evidence.description: For CSR and GPR reads via the debug interface, debug_halted_o is checked before granting access, but grants are issued (debug_gnt_o = 1'b1) even for invalid accesses to avoid blocking, indicating a permissive grant policy.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface grants are permissive by design ('grant it even when invalid access to not block'), weakening the access control model.

evidence.description: The zeroriscy_core debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i) is exposed as plain input ports with no authentication or privilege tier differentiation at the module boundary.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no privilege or authentication check on the debug interface inputs entering the core, making any connected master able to issue debug commands.

evidence.description: In fc_subsystem, the debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata signals are wired directly from the subsystem's debug bus to the zeroriscy_core with no interposing access control or lifecycle lock.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug path from the subsystem-level debug bus to the CPU core has no gating based on chip lifecycle or security state.

evidence.description: dbg_jump_addr_i is a 32-bit input to the IF stage that directly controls where the CPU fetches its next instruction when pc_mux_i == PC_DBG_NPC, making it a high-value target for unauthorized redirection.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthorized control of dbg_jump_addr_i allows arbitrary instruction pointer redirection of the CPU.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug unit in zeroriscy allows an external debug master to supply an arbitrary jump address (jump_addr_o / dbg_jump_addr) that is used in the IF stage PC mux under the PC_DBG_NPC case. While some internal debug register accesses check debug_halted_o, the PC mux in the IF stage (SNIP-0023) applies the debug jump address without a halt-state guard at that point. Furthermore, the debug interface itself (debug_req_i and associated signals) has no privilege, authentication, or lifecycle check at the core or subsystem boundary; any agent with bus access to the debug port can issue requests. The grant policy is explicitly permissive ('grant it even when invalid access to not block'). Together, these properties mean an unprivileged or attacker-controlled debug master can redirect CPU execution to an arbitrary address.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug bus can redirect the CPU program counter to arbitrary addresses via the debug jump mechanism, enabling code execution hijacking, bypassing secure boot or privilege boundaries, and potentially extracting or corrupting protected data.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not confirmed from the available snippets alone whether the debug interface is gated at a higher level (e.g., by a JTAG authentication block or lifecycle fuse outside this chain). The jump_req_o signal gating logic within the debug unit is not fully shown in the snippets, so there may be additional guards not visible here. The exact conditions under which pc_mux_i is set to PC_DBG_NPC are not shown in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: The debug unit can redirect the CPU program counter (via dbg_jump_req_i / PC_DBG_NPC) and directly read/write CSRs (via dbg_csr_req / dbg_csr_we) without any privilege-level check, authentication gate, or lifecycle lock in the RTL. Any agent that can assert the debug interface signals gains unconditional control over instruction fetch address and architectural state.

evidence.description: In DBG_WAIT state, dbg_jump_req_i unconditionally causes pc_mux_o = PC_DBG_NPC and pc_set_o = 1 with no privilege, authentication, or lifecycle guard. Any assertion of dbg_jump_req_i redirects the fetch PC to the debugger-supplied address.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard on debug jump request allows arbitrary PC redirection.

evidence.description: CSR access mux unconditionally substitutes debugger-supplied address and write-data (dbg_csr_addr, dbg_csr_wdata) whenever dbg_csr_req is asserted, with no privilege check. A write is performed when dbg_csr_we is also asserted, enabling unchecked modification of machine-mode CSRs such as mstatus, mtvec, mepc.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug CSR access path bypasses all privilege checks, allowing CSR read/write without authorization.

evidence.description: The controller module exposes dbg_req_i, dbg_stall_i, dbg_jump_req_i, and dbg_settings_i as plain inputs with no documented lock or authentication handshake in the port declarations.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug control inputs are exposed with no access-control mechanism visible in the port interface.

evidence.description: Even in RESET state, a dbg_req_i assertion immediately moves the FSM to DBG_SIGNAL, meaning the debug interface gains control before normal boot has completed and before fetch_enable is asserted, with no lifecycle or secure-boot interlock.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug access is possible before normal boot, with no lifecycle guard.

evidence.description: DBG_SIGNAL and DBG_SIGNAL_SLEEP states immediately assert dbg_ack_o and proceed to DBG_WAIT with no check of any permission register, secure mode signal, or authentication token.
evidence.supports_claim: No permission check exists between debug request acknowledgement and granting debug control.

evidence.description: core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0 when the interrupt controller is instantiated in fc_subsystem, meaning secure mode is permanently disabled at the SoC integration level.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure mode is permanently disabled, removing any potential runtime privilege barrier for the debug path.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy controller and core implement a debug interface (dbg_jump_req_i, dbg_csr_req/we) that can redirect the CPU's program counter and read/write any CSR without any privilege level check, authentication step, or lifecycle lock. The SNIP-0016 code shows pc_set_o is asserted whenever dbg_jump_req_i is high in DBG_WAIT, and SNIP-0019 shows CSR accesses are fully substituted by debugger-supplied values whenever dbg_csr_req is asserted. Additionally, SNIP-0012 shows that debug access is granted even before normal boot completes, and SNIP-0007 shows core_secure_mode_i is permanently tied to 0 at integration, removing any secure-mode barrier. There is no visible authentication, privilege, or lifecycle guard on these debug paths in the provided snippets.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface signals can: (1) redirect the CPU to execute arbitrary code by asserting dbg_jump_req_i with a controlled NPC value; (2) read or overwrite machine-mode CSRs (mstatus, mtvec, mepc, etc.) bypassing all privilege controls; (3) gain debug control before the normal secure boot flow completes. This amounts to full processor takeover from the debug port with no authentication or lifecycle protection.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets do not show the debug unit module itself (which drives dbg_jump_req_i, dbg_csr_req, dbg_csr_addr, dbg_csr_wdata, dbg_csr_we), so it is theoretically possible that access controls exist upstream. However, the core RTL provides no receiving-side guard whatsoever, and core_secure_mode_i is confirmed hardwired to 0 at SoC level, making any upstream guard ineffective even if present.

finding.summary: The debug unit's GPR (general-purpose register) read path issues a register file read request (regfile_rreq_n) only when debug_halted_o is asserted, but the debug_gnt_o grant is issued unconditionally before the halt check, and the rdata_sel_n is set to RD_GPR only inside the halt guard. However, the critical issue is that the debug interface exposes full register file read/write access, CSR access, and PC control through an external debug port (debug_req_i) that is connected directly from fc_subsystem without any privilege/authentication check. The debug_req_i signal from fc_subsystem is wired directly to the core with no lifecycle lock, fuse-based disable, or authentication gate anywhere in the chain.

evidence.description: The fc_subsystem declares debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata, debug_gnt, debug_rvalid, debug_rdata as plain logic signals with no protection or authentication qualifier.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface entry point in fc_subsystem has no access control or authentication logic surrounding the debug request signals.

evidence.description: The zeroriscy_core is instantiated with debug_req directly wired from fc_subsystem's unguarded debug_req signal to the core's debug_req_i port, with no interposing permission check.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_req flows from fc_subsystem directly into zeroriscy_core without any authentication or privilege gate.

evidence.description: The zeroriscy_core module exposes a full debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_rdata_o, debug_halt_i, debug_resume_i) as plain input/output ports with no privilege level or lifecycle state check at the module boundary.
evidence.supports_claim: The core's debug port is fully open at the module interface level with no access gating.

evidence.description: The zeroriscy_debug_unit accepts debug_req_i as a plain input and provides full register file read/write, CSR read/write, and PC jump capabilities through the debug interface. No authentication, privilege check, or lifecycle fuse is present at the debug unit boundary.
evidence.supports_claim: Any agent that can assert debug_req_i gains access to internal core state through the debug unit without any permission verification.

evidence.description: The only guard present is debug_halted_o: GPR reads (regfile_rreq_n, RD_GPR) and CSR reads (csr_req_n, RD_CSR) are only issued when debug_halted_o is asserted. However, this is a functional sequencing guard (ensuring the core is stopped), not an authentication or privilege check. The debug_gnt_o is still granted unconditionally for GPR address range even when not halted (line 247).
evidence.supports_claim: The only protection is a halted-state check, not an authentication or privilege check, meaning any caller of debug_req_i can halt and read internal registers.

evidence.description: dbg_reg_raddr is declared as a 5-bit signal giving access to any of the 32 general-purpose registers. This signal flows to both the debug unit (regfile_raddr_o) and the ID stage (dbg_reg_raddr_i), exposing all GPR contents to the debug requestor.
evidence.supports_claim: All CPU general-purpose registers are readable via the debug path, making this a high-value target if the debug interface is unauthenticated.

evidence.description: The stall control FSM transitions to HALT state upon debug_req_i assertion, after which the debug interface gains full read/write access to CPU internal state. The transition to RUNNING from HALT requires only dbg_resume or debug_resume_i, both of which are also unauthenticated external signals.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug halt/resume lifecycle is entirely controlled by external unauthenticated signals, allowing any caller to halt the CPU and access its internals.

evidence.description: CSR access muxing (csr_addr, csr_wdata, csr_op) is controlled by dbg_csr_req with no authentication guard. When dbg_csr_req is asserted via the debug interface, the debug unit can overwrite any CSR including mstatus, mepc, and privilege control registers.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthenticated debug access can modify privilege-controlling CSRs, representing a security violation with no permission check in the source code.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug interface chain from fc_subsystem through zeroriscy_core to zeroriscy_debug_unit provides full read/write access to GPRs, CSRs, and PC with no authentication, privilege-level check, lifecycle fuse, or debug-disable mechanism. The only guard in the debug unit is the debug_halted_o check, which is a functional sequencing requirement rather than a permission check. Any agent that can drive debug_req_i can halt the CPU and read/write all internal state. The debug_req signal in fc_subsystem is a plain logic wire with no protection.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface bus (e.g., via a compromised peripheral or direct hardware access) can: (1) halt the CPU at will, (2) read all general-purpose registers exposing cryptographic keys or sensitive data, (3) write arbitrary values to any GPR, (4) read and write all CSRs including privilege control registers, (5) redirect the program counter to arbitrary addresses. This constitutes a complete CPU takeover capability with no authentication barrier.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is possible that authentication or access control exists at a higher level in the SoC interconnect before signals reach fc_subsystem's debug port (e.g., an APB/AXI slave with address-based gating), but no such guard is visible in the provided source snippets. The fc_subsystem snippet shows only plain wire declarations for all debug signals with no upstream filtering logic shown.

finding.summary: The debug unit allows read access to General-Purpose Registers (GPRs) and CSRs via regfile_rreq_o without requiring the core to be halted in certain code paths, and the debug interface accepts requests without any authentication or privilege check — any agent with access to debug_req_i can read or write internal CPU registers.

evidence.description: In the read path for GPR access (6'b00_0100), regfile_rreq_n is only set when debug_halted_o is asserted. However, debug_gnt_o is unconditionally asserted ('grant it even when invalid access to not block') regardless of halted state. For the RD_DBGA category (6'b00_0000), rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGA is set with no halted check at all — debug registers are described as 'always accessible'. For RD_DBGS (6'b10_0000), rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGS is set without a halted check on the read path.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug register and status information is readable without verifying debug_halted_o in some paths, and the grant is issued unconditionally even for invalid accesses.

evidence.description: For CSR access (debug_addr_i[14] high), in state SECOND, csr_we_o is set to 1 and debug_gnt_o is granted unconditionally ('grant it even when invalid access'). The halted check on csr_req_n is only in the FIRST state; in SECOND state the write proceeds without re-checking debug_halted_o.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR write enable (csr_we_o) is asserted in the SECOND state without a debug_halted_o guard, allowing potential CSR modification when core may not be halted.

evidence.description: The debug unit module accepts debug_req_i, debug_we_i, debug_addr_i, and debug_wdata_i as plain inputs with no authentication, privilege level check, or lock mechanism. Any master driving these signals can initiate debug operations.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no access control or authentication on the debug interface inputs.

evidence.description: dbg_busy is computed as: dbg_req | dbg_csr_req | dbg_jump_req | dbg_reg_wreq | debug_req_i. The debug_req_i input alone keeps the clock enabled, meaning a perpetual debug request from an unauthenticated source can prevent clock gating and keep the core active.
evidence.supports_claim: An unauthenticated debug_req_i can manipulate clock gating behavior of the core.

evidence.description: At line 223-244, for both the CSR read path and the RD_DBGS path, debug_gnt_o is granted and rdata_sel is set without confirming debug_halted_o. This means sensitive register data (CSR values, debug status) can be read back even when the core is running.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive register readback is possible without the core being halted, violating the intended access control described in comments ('access to internal registers are only allowed when the core is halted').

evidence.description: Settings bits DBG_SETS_SSTE, DBG_SETS_ECALL, DBG_SETS_ELSU, DBG_SETS_EBRK, DBG_SETS_EILL are written directly from debug_wdata_i without any privilege check. These settings control when the debugger traps the CPU, allowing an attacker to modify trap behavior.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug trap settings are writable via the debug interface without privilege or authentication checks.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy debug unit exposes an unauthenticated debug interface. While some accesses (GPR reads, some CSR reads) correctly gate on debug_halted_o, other paths (RD_DBGA always accessible, RD_DBGS read without halted check, CSR write in SECOND state without re-checking halted, debug trap settings writable without privilege check) bypass the intended halted-state guard. Furthermore, debug_gnt_o is issued unconditionally in many branches ('grant it even when invalid access to not block'), which means the interface accepts and processes requests regardless of validity. There is no authentication mechanism on the debug_req_i input at any level in the chain from fc_subsystem through zeroriscy_core to zeroriscy_debug_unit.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug bus can: (1) read CPU internal state (CSRs, debug status registers) without the CPU being halted; (2) modify debug trap settings to suppress or inject debug traps; (3) potentially write CSRs when the halted check is bypassed in the SECOND state; (4) keep the core clock enabled via a persistent debug_req_i. This enables privilege escalation, information disclosure of CPU register state, and manipulation of CPU execution control.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact RTL for the SECOND-state CSR write halted check is partially visible — the FIRST state gates csr_req_n on debug_halted_o but SECOND state sets csr_we_o unconditionally, though it's possible csr_we_o without csr_req_q being set has no effect. Full analysis would require the complete zeroriscy_debug_unit.sv body (lines 159-222 are not shown). The upstream trust boundary for debug_req_i (whether it is gated by a secure debug authentication module in the SoC) is not visible in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: The debug unit allows GPR writes via regfile_wreq_o/regfile_waddr_o without consistently enforcing a halt check, and CSR write path in the SECOND state proceeds without re-checking debug_halted_o, enabling potential unauthorized register file and CSR modification through the debug interface.

evidence.description: In the FIRST state, CSR access sets csr_req_n only when debug_halted_o is asserted (line 151-154). However, in the SECOND state (else branch, lines 155-159), csr_we_o is asserted unconditionally without re-checking debug_halted_o. This means a write enable to the CSR can be issued even if the halted condition changes between cycles.
evidence.supports_claim: The CSR write enable (csr_we_o) is asserted in the SECOND state without verifying debug_halted_o, bypassing the halt guard that was checked in the FIRST state.

evidence.description: At line 196-199, the 6'b10_0000 address range (Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug) issues debug_gnt_o=1 unconditionally, and only the subsequent operations are gated by debug_halted_o at line 199. The grant is given even for invalid/unauthorized accesses.
evidence.supports_claim: Grant is issued unconditionally for debug-only register ranges, with only internal operation (not the grant itself) gated on halted status.

evidence.description: In zeroriscy_id_stage, when dbg_reg_wreq_i is asserted (line 395), the register file write address (regfile_waddr_mux) and data are set directly from debug inputs (dbg_reg_waddr_i, dbg_reg_wdata_i) with regfile_we_mux forced to 1, bypassing normal pipeline write-enable logic. There is no additional privilege or halt check at this mux level.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug register write requests directly override normal register file write controls without any additional access guard at the ID stage mux.

evidence.description: The debug unit exposes a full register file write port (regfile_wreq_o, regfile_waddr_o, regfile_wdata_o) and CSR write port (csr_req_o, csr_we_o, csr_wdata_o) driven entirely by the external debug interface inputs (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i) with only a soft halt-state check.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface provides direct write access to GPRs and CSRs, with access control relying solely on the internal debug_halted_o signal which can be bypassed across state transitions.

evidence.description: For GPR reads (6'b00_0100 address range, lines 246-252), debug_gnt_o is granted unconditionally but regfile_rreq_n is only set when debug_halted_o. However, the grant is still issued, potentially leaking timing or state information even when not halted.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug access grants are issued before the halt check, with only the internal request gated on halted status.

evidence.description: The fc_subsystem instantiates zeroriscy_core and connects external debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata signals directly to the core debug interface without any upstream access control or authentication shown in the snippet.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface is exposed at the subsystem boundary without visible access control, meaning any entity driving these signals can attempt debug operations.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug unit in zeroriscy_debug_unit.sv implements a two-cycle handshake for CSR access where the halt check occurs in the FIRST state to set csr_req_n, but csr_we_o is asserted in the SECOND state unconditionally. Additionally, the ID stage register file mux (SNIP-0020) directly accepts debug write requests without any additional guard beyond what the debug unit provides. The debug interface is exposed at the fc_subsystem level without visible authentication. These combined weaknesses mean that a debug master can potentially write to GPRs and CSRs with only the debug_halted_o signal as a guard, which itself is a soft internal signal that could be manipulated or that may not be re-checked at the right cycle.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface (debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata signals) could potentially write arbitrary values to general-purpose registers and CSRs of the RISC-V core. This could allow privilege escalation, corruption of security-critical state (e.g., machine-mode status registers, interrupt enable bits, exception handler addresses), or arbitrary code execution by modifying program counter via NPC register writes.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full implementation of the two-state CSR write path and whether debug_halted_o is guaranteed stable across FIRST-to-SECOND transitions is not fully visible. The upstream access control for the debug interface at higher system levels (e.g., whether the debug_req signal is protected by a secure debug authentication mechanism outside fc_subsystem) is not shown in the available snippets. The exact timing relationship between halt check and write enable assertion needs full simulation evidence to confirm exploitability.

finding.summary: The debug unit allows GPR (general-purpose register) write-back via dbg_reg_wreq/dbg_reg_wdata without requiring the core to be halted, and the register-file write mux unconditionally overrides normal writeback when dbg_reg_wreq_i is asserted, bypassing the halt guard enforced for other debug register accesses.

evidence.description: In zeroriscy_id_stage, the register-file write mux gives dbg_reg_wreq_i unconditional priority (lines 395-398): when dbg_reg_wreq_i is asserted it forces regfile_we_mux=1 and injects dbg_reg_wdata_i/dbg_reg_waddr_i with no check that the core is halted.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug GPR write path bypasses the halt requirement that protects all other debug register accesses.

evidence.description: For GPR read accesses (RD_GPR path, line 249) and CSR accesses (line 226), the debug unit gates the actual request behind 'if (debug_halted_o)', ensuring the core is halted before the access proceeds. This guard is consistently applied for reads but the write path in the ID stage does not enforce the same condition.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit enforces a halt guard for read and CSR accesses but the write-back path in the ID stage has no equivalent guard.

evidence.description: Lines 196-199 show that debug registers in the 6'b10_0000 range are described as 'only accessible when in debug', and access is wrapped in 'if (debug_halted_o)'. This confirms the design intent that sensitive accesses require halted state.
evidence.supports_claim: Design intent is that write access to internal core state requires halt, but the GPR write mux in the ID stage ignores this.

evidence.description: regfile_wreq (line 102) is an internal combinational signal in the debug unit. It is driven by the debug interface without any structural enforcement that debug_halted_o must be true before regfile_wreq_o is asserted.
evidence.supports_claim: The regfile_wreq_o output can be asserted without a halt check at the debug unit level.

evidence.description: dbg_reg_wreq and dbg_reg_wdata are wired directly from the debug unit's regfile_wreq_o and regfile_wdata_o outputs into the core (edges connects:263, connects:265), confirming the path from external debug interface to register file write is active.
evidence.supports_claim: The external debug interface can drive GPR writes into the running core without a halt requirement.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug unit consistently gates CSR and GPR read accesses behind a 'debug_halted_o' check, but the GPR write path is implemented in the ID stage's register-file mux (SNIP-0020) which simply checks dbg_reg_wreq_i with no halt guard. Because regfile_wreq is a combinational signal that can be asserted by the debug interface at any time, an attacker or misconfigured host debugger can inject arbitrary values into any GPR while the core is still running. This undermines the intended security boundary that separates debug-mode state manipulation from normal execution.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can arbitrarily corrupt any general-purpose register (x0–x31) of the running RISC-V core without halting it first, enabling control-flow hijacking, privilege escalation by overwriting a return address or function pointer register, or corruption of security-critical data held in registers.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full body of the debug unit's combinational always_comb block driving regfile_wreq is not shown in the snippets; it is possible (but not evidenced) that regfile_wreq is only asserted during the HALT state via stall_cs. However, SNIP-0020 clearly shows the ID-stage mux has no halt check, and SNIP-0009/SNIP-0012 show the asymmetry between read and write guards.

finding.summary: The debug interface of zeroriscy_core allows unrestricted register file and CSR writes via dbg_reg_wreq and dbg_csr_req without any privilege, authentication, or lifecycle guard. The CSR mux in zeroriscy_core unconditionally forwards debug-unit CSR access (dbg_csr_req) to the live CSR file, and the debug interface is wired directly from the fc_subsystem debug port with no access-control interlock.

evidence.description: Lines 612-617 show that when dbg_csr_req is asserted the CSR address, write data, and operation are unconditionally taken from the debug unit (dbg_csr_addr, dbg_csr_wdata, dbg_csr_we) with no privilege check, lock register, or lifecycle guard. Any agent that can assert debug_req_i and drive the debug interface can overwrite arbitrary CSRs including machine-mode privilege registers.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR write path via debug unit bypasses all privilege and permission checks.

evidence.description: dbg_reg_wreq (line 208), dbg_reg_waddr (line 209), dbg_reg_wdata (line 210), dbg_csr_req (line 213), dbg_csr_addr (line 214), dbg_csr_we (line 215), and dbg_csr_wdata (line 216) are declared as plain internal logic with no gating signals. There is no debug-enable register or lifecycle state conditioned on them.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug write-enable signals are ungated internal logic reachable from the external debug interface.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_core exposes debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i as direct inputs (lines 77-86) with no mention of any authentication or permission token required to drive them.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface is an unauthenticated external entry point into the core.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem instantiates zeroriscy_core and wires fc_subsystem.debug_req directly to zeroriscy_core.debug_req_i (line 227 context, edge:connects:38) without any intervening access-control logic or lifecycle state check.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_req signal is propagated from the subsystem boundary to the core without a guard.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_controller accepts dbg_req_i and dbg_jump_req_i as plain inputs (lines 99-103) with no runtime privilege verification; the controller halts the pipeline and cedes control whenever these signals are asserted.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug halt and jump-request signals can stall the pipeline and redirect PC without privilege enforcement.

evidence.description: core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0 at line 283 when instantiating the interrupt controller, indicating the system permanently operates in non-secure mode, removing any secure-world barrier that might otherwise gate debug access.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure mode is permanently disabled, eliminating a potential second layer of protection against debug-based register manipulation.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_core debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_addr_i) is an unauthenticated, unguarded entry point. SNIP-0016 shows the CSR mux unconditionally routes debug-unit requests to the live CSR file when dbg_csr_req is high, with no privilege-level check, no debug-enable lock register, and no lifecycle state gate. SNIP-0013 confirms the debug GPR-write signals (dbg_reg_wreq, dbg_reg_waddr, dbg_reg_wdata) are plain logic with no conditioning. SNIP-0007 and SNIP-0011 show the debug port is wired straight from the subsystem boundary. SNIP-0010 confirms the controller unconditionally halts on dbg_req_i. SNIP-0008 shows secure mode is hardwired off. Together these create a path where any agent able to drive the debug interface can arbitrarily overwrite general-purpose registers and CSRs (including machine-mode privilege state) with no authentication or permission check.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can overwrite arbitrary CPU registers and CSRs, including machine-mode privilege registers (mstatus, mepc, mtvec), enabling privilege escalation, disabling interrupt handling, redirecting execution flow, and extracting sensitive architectural state, all without any authentication or lifecycle guard.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet context does not include the zeroriscy_debug_unit.sv implementation, so it is not possible to confirm whether the debug unit itself performs any internal authentication before asserting dbg_csr_req or dbg_reg_wreq. However, the core-side mux (SNIP-0016) and signal declarations (SNIP-0013) provide sufficient evidence that the core performs no such check.

finding.summary: The debug unit can directly access and write CPU CSRs (including security-sensitive ones) via the dbg_csr_req/dbg_csr_we/dbg_csr_addr/dbg_csr_wdata signals without any privilege, authentication, or lifecycle guard. Additionally, the debug request signal (dbg_req_i) can redirect CPU control flow even before fetch_enable, and core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 0 in fc_subsystem.

evidence.description: The CSR access mux in zeroriscy_core unconditionally grants the debug unit (dbg_csr_req) full read/write access to all CSRs. When dbg_csr_req==1, the csr_access, csr_addr, csr_wdata, and csr_op signals are taken from debug unit inputs (dbg_csr_addr, dbg_csr_wdata, dbg_csr_we) with no privilege check, lock, or authentication guard.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug unit can write any CSR without privilege or lifecycle guard, bypassing normal CPU privilege enforcement.

evidence.description: In the RESET state, if dbg_req_i is asserted, the FSM transitions directly to DBG_SIGNAL even before fetch_enable_i is set, giving the debug unit control before the CPU has started normal execution. No authentication or lifecycle check gates this transition.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug request can hijack CPU control flow from the very first reset state without any privilege or authentication check.

evidence.description: In fc_subsystem, core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0 when instantiating apb_interrupt_cntrl. This permanently disables any secure mode protections in the interrupt controller, meaning secure interrupt isolation is never enforced.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure mode is permanently disabled for the interrupt controller, removing a layer of interrupt-based privilege separation.

evidence.description: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module accepts a core_secure_mode_i input and has an ENA_SEC_IRQ parameter for secure interrupt control (core_irq_sec_o), but this input is driven with a constant 0 from fc_subsystem, neutralizing the protection.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure interrupt mode input exists but is nullified by hardwired 0 at instantiation.

evidence.description: In DBG_WAIT state, if dbg_jump_req_i is asserted, the CPU PC is redirected to PC_DBG_NPC (the debug unit's next PC) with no guard. The debug unit can redirect execution to arbitrary addresses while the CPU is halted.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug unit has unchecked ability to redirect CPU program counter to arbitrary addresses.

evidence.description: dbg_req_i, dbg_stall_i, dbg_jump_req_i, dbg_settings_i are all plain input signals to the controller with no privilege level, authentication token, or lifecycle state check documented at the port level.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug interface inputs are ungated at the controller boundary.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy core's debug interface provides unchecked access to all CSRs (SNIP-0020), arbitrary PC redirection (SNIP-0017), and can intercept the CPU before normal boot (SNIP-0013). None of these debug capabilities are gated by a privilege check, hardware lifecycle state, or authentication mechanism visible in the source. Furthermore, fc_subsystem hardwires core_secure_mode_i=0 (SNIP-0008), permanently disabling the interrupt controller's secure mode even though the parameter ENA_SEC_IRQ exists (SNIP-0001). Together these represent missing guards on debug and security controls.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can read or write any CPU CSR (including mstatus, mtvec, etc.), redirect program counter to arbitrary addresses, and intercept the CPU before secure boot completes. The hardwired core_secure_mode_i=0 means secure interrupt isolation is never active, potentially allowing unprivileged code to receive or manipulate secure interrupts.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets do not show how dbg_req_i, dbg_csr_req, and related debug signals are driven externally (e.g., whether the debug unit itself has an authentication/access-control layer upstream). If the debug unit has its own access control that is not visible in these files, the CSR access issue may be partially mitigated at a higher level. The lifecycle/eFuse-based debug disable mechanism, if any, is not shown in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: The debug unit's DBG_SETS_SSTE (single-step) setting register and other debug trap settings are writable without verifying that the core is halted, allowing an external debug interface to modify debug behavior settings while the core is actively running.

evidence.description: At line 181, settings_n[DBG_SETS_SSTE] is unconditionally assigned from debug_wdata_i[0] without any check that debug_halted_o is asserted. Compare this to the surrounding code at lines 176-178 where dbg_resume is only set when debug_halted_o is true, and lines 196-199 where Debug Register writes are gated by debug_halted_o. The DBG_SETS_SSTE write path has no equivalent halt check.
evidence.supports_claim: The single-step enable bit can be set by an external debug interface while the CPU core is still running, without requiring the core to be halted first.

evidence.description: Lines 186-191 show DBG_SETS_ECALL, DBG_SETS_ELSU, DBG_SETS_EBRK, and DBG_SETS_EILL are also written unconditionally without a debug_halted_o guard, while the adjacent code for the same register group (line 176) only allows resume when halted.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug exception interception settings can be changed at runtime without the core being halted, potentially causing unexpected debug traps during normal execution.

evidence.description: The dbg_trap_o signal driven from dbg_settings_i[DBG_SETS_SSTE] at line 244 and similar bits at lines 416, 427, 434, 437 will cause the controller to signal a debug trap during SLEEP, DECODE, and exception-handling states. If settings are modified while running, spurious debug traps can be injected.
evidence.supports_claim: Unguarded writes to debug settings can cause unintended debug traps, halting the core at arbitrary points without legitimate authorization.

evidence.description: At lines 416 and 427, dbg_trap_o is computed as OR of DBG_SETS_ECALL/DBG_SETS_EILL/DBG_SETS_SSTE bits from dbg_settings_i. If these bits are set while the core is executing, any subsequent ecall or illegal instruction will trigger a debug trap, transferring control to an attacker-controlled debug session.
evidence.supports_claim: Unguarded debug settings modification enables an attacker with debug bus access to intercept privileged exception flows.

evidence.description: The debug unit exposes settings_o (line 51) carrying DBG_SETS_W bits directly to the core. The settings register is updated via settings_q which is driven by settings_n from the combinational always_comb block. There is no privilege or lifecycle check gating writes to this register.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug settings register lacks access control, making it reachable without privilege verification.

evidence.description: The stall FSM at lines 355-391 shows that the RUNNING state transitions to HALT_REQ when trap_i is asserted. However, because settings can be modified in RUNNING state, an attacker can force trap_i by setting DBG_SETS_SSTE and then waiting for any instruction boundary, without ever going through an authenticated halt sequence.
evidence.supports_claim: Absence of halt guard on settings writes undermines the intended state-machine protection for debug access.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit allows writes to the debug settings register (containing DBG_SETS_SSTE, DBG_SETS_ECALL, DBG_SETS_ELSU, DBG_SETS_EBRK, DBG_SETS_EILL) without requiring the core to be in the halted state. The code at SNIP-0015 lines 181 and 186-191 shows these assignments happen unconditionally, while adjacent code for resume and protected debug register access is properly gated by debug_halted_o. This means any agent with access to the debug bus can set single-step or exception-interception bits while the CPU is running, causing the controller (SNIP-0006, SNIP-0007) to assert dbg_trap_o at the next matching instruction or exception, diverting execution into the debug halt flow without proper authorization.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug bus can modify debug exception settings (single-step, ecall/illegal-instruction intercept) while the core is running. This allows injecting spurious debug traps during normal or privileged operation, potentially redirecting program control, leaking machine-mode state through the debug interface, or mounting a denial-of-service by repeatedly trapping the core.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets do not show the complete address decode for the DBG_CTRL register write path, so it is not possible to confirm whether there is an outer conditional that was missed. However, based on SNIP-0015 lines 181 and 186-191, the write to settings_n fields is clearly not guarded by debug_halted_o in contrast to the explicitly guarded neighboring code paths.

finding.summary: The debug unit can be resumed from HALT_REQ state (before the core has fully halted and acknowledged) via the external debug_resume_i signal, potentially bypassing the halted-state protection and allowing the core to resume without proper debug acknowledgment.

evidence.description: In the HALT_REQ state, both dbg_resume and debug_resume_i can transition the FSM back to RUNNING before dbg_ack_i is received. This means a resume can occur while the debug halt request is still pending — before the core has acknowledged the halt and entered the fully halted HALT state.
evidence.supports_claim: The FSM transition from HALT_REQ to RUNNING via debug_resume_i occurs without requiring dbg_ack_i, meaning the core can be resumed before it has properly halted, bypassing the intended halt/ack handshake.

evidence.description: DBG_SIGNAL and DBG_SIGNAL_SLEEP states assert dbg_ack_o and transition to DBG_WAIT, establishing the intended halt acknowledgment flow. This shows the design expects a proper handshake sequence.
evidence.supports_claim: The intended flow requires going through DBG_SIGNAL -> DBG_WAIT with explicit dbg_ack_o before halting; the HALT_REQ early-resume path in the debug unit skips this coordination.

evidence.description: The RUNNING state transitions to HALT_REQ when dbg_halt, debug_halt_i, or trap_i is asserted. The debug_halt_i and debug_resume_i are external signals directly controlling halt/resume without additional privilege or authentication checks.
evidence.supports_claim: External debug_halt_i and debug_resume_i signals directly control core halt/resume state machine transitions without any privilege level or authentication guard visible in the RTL.

evidence.description: The debug unit port list shows debug_halt_i and debug_resume_i as plain input logic with no documented privilege or authentication gating at the module interface level.
evidence.supports_claim: No guard logic exists at the debug unit interface to restrict who can assert debug_halt_i or debug_resume_i, making these privileged debug controls accessible without permission enforcement.

evidence.description: Debug register access (DBG_SETS_SSTE, DBG_SETS_ECALL, etc.) is gated on debug_halted_o for some registers, but the halt/resume FSM itself is not similarly protected.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent protection: some debug register writes check debug_halted_o but the core halt/resume control via debug_resume_i has no equivalent guard.

evidence.description: In DBG_WAIT, the controller returns to DECODE when dbg_stall_i goes low. If the debug unit prematurely resumes (transitions back to RUNNING before proper halt), stall_o will deassert, causing the controller to exit debug wait state and resume normal execution unexpectedly.
evidence.supports_claim: Premature resume causes the pipeline to exit debug stall and continue instruction execution, undermining the integrity of any debug session and potentially allowing execution to resume at an unintended program counter.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy debug unit implements a three-state FSM (RUNNING, HALT_REQ, HALT) to manage core halting. In the HALT_REQ state, the design waits for dbg_ack_i from the controller before transitioning to the fully HALT state. However, the external debug_resume_i signal can transition the FSM directly from HALT_REQ back to RUNNING without waiting for dbg_ack_i. This creates a race condition where a resume can occur before the core has completed its halt handshake. Additionally, the debug_halt_i and debug_resume_i inputs have no privilege or authentication guard visible in the RTL, meaning any entity with access to these signals can control core execution state. The controller's DBG_WAIT state depends on dbg_stall_i (driven by stall_o from the debug unit) to hold the pipeline; if stall_o prematurely deasserts due to early resume, the pipeline resumes at potentially incorrect state.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or untrusted debug interface with access to debug_resume_i could prematurely resume the core from a debug halt request before the halt handshake completes, potentially allowing execution to continue while the debug unit believes it has control. This could be used to escape debug breakpoints, bypass security checks implemented as debug traps, or cause undefined behavior in the pipeline state machine. The absence of privilege gating on debug_halt_i/debug_resume_i means these powerful execution-control signals are unprotected.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full system integration showing how debug_halt_i and debug_resume_i are driven externally is not visible in the provided snippets. It is possible that external access control exists at a higher level in the SoC integration. The exact timing consequences of the HALT_REQ early-resume race condition depend on clocking and pipeline state not fully visible in these snippets. The zeroriscy_core.sv instantiation of the debug unit (around line 619) is partially visible but the full port connections are truncated.

finding.summary: ebreak instruction unconditionally forces dbg_trap_o to 1, bypassing the debug settings register check that gates all other debug trap conditions

evidence.description: Line 437 shows `dbg_trap_o = 1'b1` for ebrk_insn_i, with the correct conditional expression commented out (`//dbg_settings_i[DBG_SETS_EBRK] | dbg_settings_i[DBG_SETS_SSTE]`). Every other exception (ecall at line 416, illegal instruction at line 427, mret at line 434) gates dbg_trap_o through dbg_settings_i bits. The ebreak case unconditionally asserts the debug trap signal regardless of the DBG_SETS_EBRK or DBG_SETS_SSTE configuration bits.
evidence.supports_claim: dbg_trap_o is asserted unconditionally on ebreak, bypassing the debug settings permission/configuration guard that all other trap conditions respect

evidence.description: DBG_SETS_EBRK is a configurable bit in the settings register (settings_n[DBG_SETS_EBRK] = debug_wdata_i[3]) that is intended to control whether an ebreak instruction triggers a debug trap. This bit exists and is writable but is never consulted in the controller.
evidence.supports_claim: The settings register contains DBG_SETS_EBRK as a permission bit that should gate the ebreak debug trap, confirming the bypass is unintentional

evidence.description: When dbg_trap_o (trap_i in the debug unit) is asserted, the RUNNING state unconditionally issues dbg_req_o and transitions to HALT_REQ, ultimately halting the core and asserting debug_halted_o. Any ebreak instruction will force the processor into debug halt regardless of debug configuration.
evidence.supports_claim: The unconditional dbg_trap_o assertion on ebreak forces the core to halt under debugger control, which cannot be disabled through the debug settings interface

evidence.description: dbg_settings_i[DBG_SETS_W-1:0] is the input carrying debug configuration bits to the controller, and dbg_trap_o is the output that signals a debug trap event. All other trap conditions check the appropriate bit in dbg_settings_i before asserting dbg_trap_o.
evidence.supports_claim: The guard mechanism exists for all other debug trap conditions but is missing for the ebreak case

finding.reasoning_summary: The source code at line 437 of zeroriscy_controller.sv shows `dbg_trap_o = 1'b1` hardcoded for the ebreak instruction case, with the intended conditional logic commented out. All other debug trap conditions (ecall, illegal instruction, mret, sleep/WFI) use dbg_settings_i configuration bits to control whether a debug trap is signaled. The DBG_SETS_EBRK bit exists in the settings register and is writable via the debug interface, but the controller never reads it for the ebreak case. This means any ebreak instruction will unconditionally trigger a debug halt regardless of the debug configuration, bypassing the permission control intended by the DBG_SETS_EBRK setting.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or malicious code executing an ebreak instruction can unconditionally force the processor core into debug halt state (debug_halted_o asserted, pipeline stalled), regardless of whether the debug trap for ebreak has been enabled by the debug configuration. This could be used as a denial-of-service mechanism to halt the CPU, or to force the core into a state where debug register access is permitted, potentially enabling further privilege escalation through the debug interface.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The commented-out code confirms developer intent, making this a clear inadvertent bypass rather than a design choice. No additional files are needed to confirm the finding.

finding.summary: The debug unit grants access to certain debug register regions and issues grants even on invalid accesses without verifying that the core is halted, and the CSR write path (csr_we_o) is asserted in the second cycle without re-checking debug_halted_o, allowing potential debug register and CSR manipulation when the core is not properly halted.

evidence.description: The read path for non-CSR debug register accesses (6'b00_0000 'always accessible' region and 6'b10_0000 region) unconditionally asserts debug_gnt_o=1 without checking debug_halted_o. The comment 'grant it even when invalid access to not block' explicitly documents that grants are issued even when access should be denied.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug grants are issued to restricted regions without enforcing the halted precondition, potentially allowing reads when the core is running.

evidence.description: In the CSR write path, state FIRST conditionally sets csr_req_n only when debug_halted_o is true, but in state SECOND (the cycle where csr_we_o is asserted at line 158), there is no re-check of debug_halted_o before asserting csr_we_o=1'b1. A race or state mismatch could result in a CSR write without the core being halted.
evidence.supports_claim: csr_we_o is unconditionally asserted in the SECOND cycle without re-verifying the halted condition, creating a window where CSR writes occur without the guard.

evidence.description: The debug settings registers (DBG_SETS_SSTE, DBG_SETS_ECALL, DBG_SETS_ELSU, DBG_SETS_EBRK, DBG_SETS_EILL) are written unconditionally via settings_n without checking debug_halted_o, allowing external debug interface to modify core exception/breakpoint settings at any time.
evidence.supports_claim: Security-sensitive debug configuration bits are writable by the debug interface regardless of core halted state.

evidence.description: The debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i) is exposed as module ports of zeroriscy_debug_unit with no authentication or privilege check visible in the port definition or the top-level always_comb block initialization.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface accepts requests with no authentication layer before address decoding begins.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem directly wires the debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata, debug_req signals from its own ports to the zeroriscy_core, showing the debug bus reaches the CPU core without any intermediate filtering or permission gate in the subsystem.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug interface signals flow directly from subsystem boundary to CPU debug unit without any interposing access control.

evidence.description: The HALT state machine transitions to HALT (debug_halted_o=1) only after dbg_ack_i is received, but debug register accesses and grants can occur in HALT_REQ state before the core is fully halted, as no state-gating exists in the address decode logic for the grant path.
evidence.supports_claim: The halted guard checked in address decode (debug_halted_o) can be false during HALT_REQ while grants are still issued for some regions.

evidence.description: CSR writes from the debug interface bypass the normal instruction-path CSR access arbitration: when dbg_csr_req==1, csr_addr and csr_wdata are taken directly from debug values and csr_op is forced to CSR_OP_WRITE if dbg_csr_we==1, representing a high-privilege write path controlled entirely by the debug interface.
evidence.supports_claim: CSRs are a protected resource that can be written via the debug path; insufficient halted-state enforcement in the debug unit undermines this protection.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit implements a halted-core guard (debug_halted_o) for some operations but not consistently. In the read path, certain address regions unconditionally grant access. In the write path, csr_we_o is asserted in the SECOND cycle without re-checking the halted condition. Debug configuration registers (settings_n) are written without any halted check. The debug interface itself has no authentication, and signals flow unfiltered from fc_subsystem into the core. Together these create a permission violation: the debug interface can manipulate CSRs, GPRs, and debug settings without reliably ensuring the core is halted.
