finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug bus can read sensitive debug registers, modify CSR values (including security-critical registers like mstatus, mepc), alter debug exception settings, and potentially redirect execution—all without proper enforcement of the halted-core precondition that is intended as the primary access guard.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full body of the address-decode always_comb block is not entirely shown (lines between SNIP-0009 and SNIP-0010 may contain additional guards). It is also unclear whether an external authentication wrapper exists above fc_subsystem that restricts who can assert debug_req. The SECOND-cycle csr_we_o path analysis assumes no intervening reset of csr_req between cycles.

finding.summary: The debug unit grants access and issues debug_gnt_o for several debug register regions without requiring the core to be halted, allowing debug reads/writes to sensitive internal state while the core is running.

evidence.description: At line 223, debug_gnt_o is asserted unconditionally ('grant it even when invalid access to not block') for the CSR read path (non-write path), and at line 241 and 247, grants are similarly issued without halted check. For the RD_DBGS region (line 240-244), grant is given regardless of debug_halted_o. For GPR reads (line 246-252), grant is asserted but register file read only happens when halted — yet the grant itself is unconditional.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_gnt_o is asserted for multiple address regions without verifying core is halted, bypassing the halt-before-access guard.

evidence.description: For CSR write path (line 156), debug_gnt_o is granted even when not halted: 'grant it even when invalid access to not block'. The comment acknowledges this is intentional but the csr_we_o is asserted at line 158 in the SECOND state without re-checking debug_halted_o.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR write enable (csr_we_o) can be asserted in SECOND state without a halted check, potentially allowing CSR modification while core is running.

evidence.description: At line 197, for the 'Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug' region (6'b10_0000), debug_gnt_o is granted unconditionally. Although the actual register read/write is conditional on debug_halted_o (line 199), the grant is always issued. More critically, settings_n updates (lines 181-190) including DBG_SETS_SSTE (single-step), DBG_SETS_ECALL, DBG_SETS_ELSU, DBG_SETS_EBRK, DBG_SETS_EILL are performed without a halted check.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug settings controlling trap behavior can be modified without requiring the core to be halted, allowing an attacker to manipulate debug trap settings while the core runs.

evidence.description: The stall/halt FSM transitions to HALT_REQ on debug_halt_i or debug_req_i, but the debug register interface grants and some writes occur before the core has acknowledged halting (dbg_ack_i). The settings register writes at lines 181-190 happen in the combinational always_comb block without being gated by stall_cs == HALT.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug configuration registers (settings_n) controlling step/exception-trap behavior can be written while core is in RUNNING state.

evidence.description: The debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i) is exposed as top-level ports of zeroriscy_debug_unit with no authentication or privilege check at the entry point. Any agent with access to these signals can issue debug transactions.
evidence.supports_claim: No authentication or privilege guard exists at the debug interface entry point.

evidence.description: In fc_subsystem, the debug signals (debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata) are wired directly to the zeroriscy_core instance without any filtering, gating, or permission check at the subsystem level.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug interface signals flow without any subsystem-level permission guard into the core debug unit.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit issues debug_gnt_o and processes debug write transactions for several register regions (CSR writes in SECOND state, settings registers) without requiring the core to be in the HALT state. While some read operations check debug_halted_o before performing the actual register file or CSR read, grants are issued unconditionally with comments like 'grant it even when invalid access to not block'. Critically, settings_n writes (single-step enable, exception-on-trap settings) at lines 181-190 in SNIP-0009 are not gated by debug_halted_o, and CSR writes via csr_we_o in SNIP-0008 line 158 also lack a halted check. This means a debug master can modify core debug behavior and potentially CSR state while the core is actively executing.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface (which has no authentication at either the fc_subsystem or zeroriscy_core level) can modify debug trap settings (single-step, exception causes) and issue CSR writes while the core is running, potentially hijacking execution flow, disabling security-relevant exception handling, or corrupting CSR state without ever halting the core. This undermines any security model that relies on the debug interface being safe to expose.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full settings write path (lines 181-190) context and whether the enclosing if-else checks debug_halted_o for all settings writes is not fully visible in the snippets — SNIP-0009 shows the inner case but the outer condition from earlier lines is not fully captured. Similarly, the exact condition tree leading to the settings_n writes needs the full always_comb block to confirm definitively. The CSR write path (csr_we_o in SNIP-0008 line 158) also needs confirmation that no outer guard on debug_halted_o exists for the SECOND-state path.

finding.summary: The debug halt signal (debug_halt_i) directly triggers a halt request on the processor without any authentication, privilege, or lifecycle guard check. Any entity that can assert this input can halt the CPU unconditionally.

evidence.description: In the stall control FSM, the RUNNING state unconditionally transitions to HALT_REQ whenever debug_halt_i is asserted: 'if (dbg_halt | debug_halt_i | trap_i)'. There is no privilege level check, no lifecycle state check, no authentication token, and no lock register guarding this path. Any agent that can drive debug_halt_i can halt the processor.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_halt_i is accepted and acted upon without any permission or authentication guard, allowing unauthenticated halt of the CPU core.

evidence.description: The debug_halt_i port is declared as a plain input logic to zeroriscy_debug_unit with no associated enable, lock, or privilege qualifier. It is directly consumed in the stall control logic.
evidence.supports_claim: The signal is exposed as an unguarded external input to the debug unit, providing an attack surface for unauthorized halting.

evidence.description: In zeroriscy_core, debug_halt_i is passed through directly to the debug unit instance without any intermediate gating or permission check at the core level.
evidence.supports_claim: The top-level core provides no additional guard before routing debug_halt_i to the debug unit.

evidence.description: Upon assertion of debug_halt_i, the FSM sets dbg_req_o=1 and transitions to HALT_REQ, then to HALT where stall_o=1 and debug_halted_o=1. This stalls the CPU pipeline entirely, enabling full debug-mode access including register and CSR read/write.
evidence.supports_claim: An unauthenticated halt leads to full debug access to internal processor state including CSRs and GPRs.

evidence.description: CSR and GPR access in the debug unit is gated on debug_halted_o, which is set as a consequence of the unguarded debug_halt_i path. Once halted via debug_halt_i, an attacker can read/write CSRs and GPRs.
evidence.supports_claim: The internal register access protections rely on debug_halted_o being legitimately set, but that state can be reached through the unguarded debug_halt_i.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippets show that debug_halt_i is a raw input port to zeroriscy_debug_unit that, when asserted, unconditionally causes the processor to enter HALT_REQ and then HALT state via the stall control FSM (SNIP-0010, line 359). No privilege check, authentication mechanism, lifecycle state check, or lock register is consulted before acting on this signal. Once halted, the debug unit grants access to CSRs and GPRs (SNIP-0008), making the missing guard on debug_halt_i a security-relevant vulnerability. The signal flows directly from an external port through zeroriscy_core (SNIP-0005) to the debug unit (SNIP-0006) without interception.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug_halt_i signal line can halt the processor at will, gain full debug-mode access to internal processor state (CSRs, GPRs), potentially exfiltrate or tamper with sensitive data, and cause denial of service to the CPU. This breaks the security boundary between normal and debug operation modes.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is possible that an external wrapper or SoC-level integration applies a privilege or lifecycle gate before driving debug_halt_i, but no such gate is visible in the provided RTL sources. The debug unit and core themselves contain no such protection.

finding.summary: The debug unit grants access and processes write operations to General-Purpose Registers (GPR) and certain debug registers without verifying that the core is halted, and in the read path issues grants even for invalid accesses without enforcing the halted-only protection consistently.

evidence.description: At line 246-252, the read path for General-Purpose Registers (addr[13:8]==6'b00_0100) issues debug_gnt_o=1 unconditionally but only sets regfile_rreq_n and rdata_sel_n=RD_GPR when debug_halted_o is asserted. The grant is issued regardless of halt state, meaning the transaction is accepted even when the core is running. Similarly, the RD_DBGS path (line 240-244) grants unconditionally and sets rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGS with no halted check.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug bus transactions are granted (acknowledged) even when the core is not halted, bypassing the intended halted-only access restriction for sensitive register reads.

evidence.description: At line 196-197, the write path for the 6'b10_0000 debug register block issues debug_gnt_o=1 unconditionally ('grant it even when invalid access to not block'), and the actual write processing (line 199) is gated on debug_halted_o. However the grant itself is unconditional, and the settings_n writes at lines 181-190 (DBG_SETS_SSTE, DBG_SETS_ECALL, DBG_SETS_ELSU, DBG_SETS_EBRK, DBG_SETS_EILL) in the enclosing block are not themselves shown to be gated on debug_halted_o.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug control register writes (breakpoint and exception-trigger settings) may be accepted and granted without enforcing halt-state prerequisites, allowing modification of debug behavior while the core is running.

evidence.description: The halt state machine transitions to HALT and asserts debug_halted_o (line 386) only after dbg_ack_i is received (line 377-378). However, the RUNNING state transitions to HALT_REQ on debug_halt_i (line 359) without any authentication or privilege check on the incoming halt signal.
evidence.supports_claim: Any entity that can assert debug_halt_i can unconditionally halt the CPU core with no privilege or authentication check in the RTL.

evidence.description: In fc_subsystem, the debug bus signals (debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata) are plain logic wires declared without any access-control or authentication gating before being connected to the zeroriscy_core debug interface.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface entry point in fc_subsystem has no permission gate or trust-level check before forwarding debug transactions to the CPU core.

evidence.description: At line 155-158, the CSR write path in the SECOND state sets csr_we_o=1 and grants the transaction (debug_gnt_o=1) unconditionally, without re-checking debug_halted_o. The halted check at line 151 only gates csr_req_n in the FIRST cycle; in the SECOND cycle the write proceeds regardless.
evidence.supports_claim: A CSR write via the debug interface can complete (csr_we_o asserted) in the SECOND state even if the core is no longer halted between cycles, constituting a missing guard on the write completion path.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy debug unit is intended to restrict sensitive register access (GPR, CSR, debug state registers) to when the core is halted (debug_halted_o). However, the RTL shows multiple violations: (1) debug_gnt_o is asserted unconditionally for several access classes regardless of halt state, accepting transactions even when the core is running; (2) the CSR write completion in SECOND state does not re-verify halt status; (3) the halt request itself (debug_halt_i) is accepted from the fc_subsystem with no authentication or privilege check; (4) debug write transactions to settings registers controlling breakpoints and exception triggers are granted without halted-state enforcement. The entry point in fc_subsystem also has no access gating.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or untrusted debug master that can drive the debug bus signals in fc_subsystem can: halt the CPU core at will without authentication; read GPR/CSR values (via the granted-but-empty-data path creating a side channel or race); write debug settings (breakpoint enables, exception triggers) while the core is running; and potentially corrupt CSR state via the two-cycle CSR write race. This breaks isolation between debug and normal operation modes.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The upstream access-control gating of the debug bus (i.e., who drives debug_req/debug_addr into fc_subsystem and whether there is a top-level trust zone or SoC-level permission check) is not shown in the provided snippets. Some of the 'grant but no data' patterns may be intentional to avoid bus stalls. The exact behavior of the settings_n writes relative to the debug_halted_o check depends on control flow not fully visible in the snippets.

finding.summary: The debug unit exposes sensitive CPU internal state (CSR registers, general-purpose register file contents, program counters, debug control registers) through debug_rdata_o without any privilege or authentication check on the requester. Any entity capable of asserting debug_req_i can read arbitrary CPU state.

evidence.description: The read data mux at lines 318-329 directly drives debug_rdata_o with csr_rdata_i (CSR contents), regfile_rdata_i (GPR contents), and internal debug state (dbg_rdata) based solely on rdata_sel_q, with no privilege level check, authentication token, or lock bit guarding who can read. Any debug requester gets the data.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive CPU state is returned to debug_rdata_o without any access control or privilege verification on the debug requester.

evidence.description: For debug register writes in the 6'b10_0000 address range, the code checks 'if (debug_halted_o)' before allowing register file or CSR access (line 199). However, for reads, no equivalent halted/privilege check is shown in the read mux (SNIP-0012). The write path has a partial guard that is absent on the read path.
evidence.supports_claim: Read access to sensitive debug data lacks the halt-state guard present on portions of the write path, creating an asymmetric and incomplete access control.

evidence.description: debug_req_i is a plain logic input with no associated credential or privilege signal. Any agent driving debug_req_i high can initiate debug transactions that ultimately result in data being returned on debug_rdata_o.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface entry point has no authentication or privilege qualification on the request signal itself.

evidence.description: debug_rdata_o is a 32-bit output port of zeroriscy_core exposed at the top-level SoC boundary via fc_subsystem, carrying CPU CSR values, GPR values, and program counter contents — all sensitive architectural state.
evidence.supports_claim: The resource exposed through the unguarded debug read path includes privileged CPU architectural state accessible at the chip boundary.

evidence.description: The debug_rdata signal is wired from zeroriscy_core.debug_rdata_o at the fc_subsystem level (lFC_CORE instantiation), making the unguarded debug readback reachable from outside the processor core through the subsystem debug bus.
evidence.supports_claim: The vulnerability is exploitable from outside the core via the subsystem-level debug bus connection.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit implements a debug register read mux (SNIP-0012, lines 318-329) that returns CSR register data, general-purpose register file data, NPC/PPC program counter values, and internal debug control state directly on debug_rdata_o. The selection is controlled only by rdata_sel_q, which is set based on the address decoded from the incoming debug request. There is no check of privilege level, authentication state, or any security lifecycle state before returning data. The write path includes a partial guard (debug_halted_o check at line 199 in SNIP-0011), but this guard is not applied to reads. The debug_req_i input is a plain unauthenticated signal. The exposed data path reaches the fc_subsystem boundary (SNIP-0002), making sensitive CPU state readable by any agent with access to the debug bus.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or malicious agent with access to the debug bus can read arbitrary CSR contents (including machine-mode privilege registers), all 32 general-purpose register values, and program counter state from a running or halted CPU without any authentication, privilege check, or lifecycle gate. This enables full architectural state extraction and could facilitate key material or secrets exfiltration if sensitive data resides in registers.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippets do not show the full upstream arbitration or access control applied to the debug bus before it reaches debug_req_i at the SoC level. It is possible that bus-level access control exists outside this RTL context. However, within the RTL chain provided, no such guard is visible.

finding.summary: The debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i) is wired directly from fc_subsystem to zeroriscy_core with no authentication, authorization, or lifecycle lock guard. The debug unit can read/write GPRs, CSRs, and redirect the program counter without any privilege or enable check in the RTL.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_core exposes a full debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_gnt_o, debug_rvalid_o, debug_rdata_o, debug_halted_o, debug_halt_i, debug_resume_i) as plain top-level ports with no documented access-control qualifier.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug access enters the core as a simple input port; any upstream logic that drives these signals can unconditionally halt and inspect/modify the core.

evidence.description: In fc_subsystem the zeroriscy_core instantiation connects debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata, debug_gnt, debug_rvalid directly from internal wires to the core's debug ports. No enable register, secure-mode check, or lifecycle fuse is interposed between the external debug request and the core.
evidence.supports_claim: The wiring shows there is no guard logic between the debug master and the processor debug interface.

evidence.description: Inside zeroriscy_core, the CSR mux is controlled solely by dbg_csr_req: 'assign csr_access = (dbg_csr_req == 1'b0) ? csr_access_ex : 1'b1'. When the debug unit asserts dbg_csr_req the CSR bus is unconditionally taken over with dbg_csr_addr and dbg_csr_wdata. There is no privilege-level or lifecycle check before granting this access.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit can overwrite any CSR (including mstatus, mepc, etc.) without any authentication or lock check.

evidence.description: Internal signals dbg_reg_rreq/raddr, dbg_reg_wreq/waddr/wdata, dbg_csr_req/addr/we/wdata, and dbg_jump_req/addr are all present, enabling the debug unit to read GPRs, write GPRs, read/write CSRs, and redirect the PC — covering the full architectural state of the core.
evidence.supports_claim: All sensitive CPU state is reachable through the unguarded debug interface.

evidence.description: core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0 in the apb_interrupt_cntrl instantiation inside fc_subsystem, meaning the interrupt controller always operates as if secure mode is disabled. This removes a potential mitigating signal.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure mode is permanently disabled in this subsystem, leaving no software-visible barrier to complement the missing hardware debug guard.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_controller receives dbg_req_i and dbg_jump_req_i directly and uses them to stall the pipeline and redirect the PC. The controller contains no check against a lock register, lifecycle state, or privilege mode before accepting these debug commands.
evidence.supports_claim: The controller blindly honors debug requests, confirming the absence of any permission gate at the point of enforcement.

finding.reasoning_summary: The RTL chain shows that debug_req_i originates externally, is routed through fc_subsystem wire assignments without any lock or authentication mux, and arrives at zeroriscy_core where it unconditionally grants the debug unit full read/write access to GPRs, CSRs, and PC redirection. The CSR access mux in zeroriscy_core (SNIP-0016) explicitly overrides normal CPU CSR access when dbg_csr_req is asserted, with no privilege or lifecycle check. Simultaneously, core_secure_mode_i is tied to 0 (SNIP-0008), disabling whatever secure-mode mitigation the interrupt controller might provide. The result is an unguarded debug interface that can be asserted by any upstream logic to gain full control of the CPU.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or malicious peripheral with access to the debug request signals can halt the CPU, read all general-purpose and control/status registers (including secrets cached in registers), overwrite them, and redirect execution to arbitrary addresses — constituting a complete CPU takeover without requiring any privilege escalation through software.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact source of the debug_req wire driving fc_subsystem (the APB debug adapter or JTAG TAP upstream) is not included in the chain snippets, so it is not possible to confirm whether that upstream master has its own access control. However, the absence of any guard within fc_subsystem and zeroriscy_core is clearly evidenced by the provided snippets.

finding.summary: The debug unit grants access and returns read data for certain debug register address ranges without checking whether the core is halted, allowing an unprivileged requester to read debug state or trigger grant responses from a running core.

evidence.description: Address range 6'b00_0000 ('Debug Registers, always accessible') unconditionally sets debug_gnt_o=1 and rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGA with no check on debug_halted_o. The comment itself says 'always accessible', meaning debug register reads in this range are permitted regardless of whether the core is halted.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug registers can be read without the core being halted, bypassing the intended halt-gated access control.

evidence.description: The adjacent address range 6'b00_0100 (GPR access) and CSR access both gate their substantive actions on debug_halted_o, but the 6'b00_0000 range does not, demonstrating inconsistent application of the halt guard.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent guarding: some ranges check debug_halted_o and others do not.

evidence.description: The RD_DBGA read-data path exposes debug_halted_o status, sleeping_i, dbg_ssth_q, settings_q (including SSTE, ECALL, ELSU, EBRK, EILL bits), and dbg_cause_q – all sensitive internal debug state – to the requester when rdata_sel_q==RD_DBGA, which is set without a halt check.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive internal state (halt status, debug cause, step-single-trace enable) is readable from the debug bus without requiring the core to be halted.

evidence.description: Address range 6'b10_0000 ('Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug') also unconditionally sets debug_gnt_o=1 and rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGS, only gating write-side actions on debug_halted_o (SNIP-0010 line 199). The grant and the rdata_sel assignment are unconditional, so a non-halted read still produces a response.
evidence.supports_claim: Grant is issued for the 'only accessible when in debug' range even when the core is not halted, leaking the fact that the access was accepted.

evidence.description: For CSR access (debug_addr_i[14] set), write access grants are issued unconditionally ('grant it even when invalid access to not block', line 156) and csr_we_o is set in SECOND state regardless of debug_halted_o, while csr_req_n in FIRST state is correctly gated. This means a CSR write can be acknowledged even though the core is running.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR write grant path does not consistently enforce the halt requirement.

evidence.description: The debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_we_i) is directly exposed as module ports with no authentication or privilege-level check before entering the address-decode logic.
evidence.supports_claim: Any bus master that can drive debug_req_i can access the debug unit without privilege gating upstream.

evidence.description: The stall FSM transitions to HALT_REQ when dbg_halt, debug_halt_i, or trap_i are asserted, but in the RUNNING state the debug register interface is still responding to read requests (RD_DBGA path), so read access happens before halting is complete.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug register reads can occur while the core is still in RUNNING state.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit implements access controls that are inconsistently applied. The intent is that accesses to internal core state (GPRs, CSRs, debug registers) should only be permitted when debug_halted_o is asserted. However, the address-decode combinational block unconditionally grants and sets rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGA for address range 6'b00_0000 ('always accessible') and unconditionally grants for 6'b10_0000 ('only accessible when in debug') without actually enforcing debug_halted_o on the grant or read-data-select path. The RD_DBGA response path then returns sensitive fields (debug_halted_o, sleeping_i, dbg_ssth_q, full settings_q). Additionally, the CSR write grant in SECOND state is unconditional. This allows a requester to probe debug state and receive valid grant responses from a non-halted core.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug bus can read sensitive internal debug and security configuration registers (halt status, single-step trace enable, exception-break configuration, debug cause) without first halting the core, and can receive unconditional grant responses for address ranges that are supposed to be debug-mode-only. This undermines the access-control model of the debug subsystem and could be used to probe execution state or manipulate debug settings without proper privilege.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The upstream bus infrastructure (who can drive debug_req_i in fc_subsystem) is not fully analyzed here; if the debug bus is physically restricted to a trusted debugger only, severity is reduced. The full rdata mux output for RD_DBGS is not shown in the snippets provided. However, the guard logic violation for RD_DBGA and the unconditional grant paths are clearly evidenced in SNIP-0011 and SNIP-0012.

finding.summary: Debug write data (debug_wdata_i) can modify certain debug settings registers without requiring the core to be halted, allowing an attacker with debug interface access to alter debug behavior while the CPU is running.

evidence.description: Lines 181-191 show that writes to DBG_CTRL (settings_n[DBG_SETS_SSTE]) and DBG_IE (settings_n[DBG_SETS_ECALL/ELSU/EBRK/EILL]) are performed unconditionally from debug_wdata_i without checking debug_halted_o. The halt check at line 176 only gates the dbg_resume signal, not the settings writes. Similarly, DBG_HIT ssth_clear at line 184 is unconditional.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug settings registers controlling single-step, exception breakpoints, and halt-on-trap can be written while the core is running, bypassing the halted guard that protects other sensitive register accesses.

evidence.description: Lines 226-252 show that CSR access (csr_req_n) and GPR read (regfile_rreq_n) are correctly gated behind debug_halted_o checks. This contrast highlights that the settings writes in SNIP-0010 intentionally or accidentally omit such guards.
evidence.supports_claim: Other sensitive debug accesses properly check debug_halted_o, confirming the missing check for settings writes is a discrepancy.

evidence.description: The stall/halt FSM (RUNNING, HALT_REQ, HALT) shows that settings_q (written from settings_n derived from debug_wdata_i) directly controls trap behavior via settings_q[DBG_SETS_SSTE] and exception masking, which are evaluated in the RUNNING state at line 364.
evidence.supports_claim: Modifying debug settings while the core is running directly affects trap handling and single-step behavior, constituting a security impact.

evidence.description: The debug interface accepts debug_wdata_i as a 32-bit external input with no authentication or privilege check at the module boundary. The signal flows from fc_subsystem through zeroriscy_core into zeroriscy_debug_unit without any access control layer.
evidence.supports_claim: Any entity with access to the external debug interface pins can drive debug_wdata_i and trigger the unguarded settings writes.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem instantiates zeroriscy_core and connects debug_wdata directly to debug_wdata_i with no interposing permission checks, showing the attack surface reaches from the SoC debug port to the core debug unit.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug write data path is unprotected at the subsystem integration level.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit processes debug_wdata_i writes in an always_comb block that decodes debug_addr_i. For addresses targeting debug settings registers (DBG_CTRL, DBG_HIT, DBG_IE), the writes to settings_n (controlling single-step, exception-on-load/store, breakpoint, illegal instruction trapping) are applied unconditionally — without checking debug_halted_o. By contrast, accesses to CSRs and GPRs do check debug_halted_o. An attacker with debug interface access can therefore modify the core's trap/debug settings while it is actively executing, enabling them to suppress or force debug traps, clear single-step state, or alter exception-triggered halts without first halting the CPU.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can manipulate debug trap settings (single-step enable, exception masking for ecall/LSU/breakpoint/illegal instruction) while the core is running. This can be used to prevent legitimate debuggers from stopping the core on security-relevant exceptions, or to force unexpected debug halts, potentially aiding privilege escalation or defeating security monitoring.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippets show the combinational write path but do not show the full always_ff block that registers settings_n into settings_q, nor the complete case statement surrounding lines 175-194. It is possible that an outer condition not visible in the snippets guards the entire block, but based on the available code the writes appear unguarded. The external authentication mechanism for debug_req_i (if any) at the system level above fc_subsystem is also not visible.

finding.summary: The debug write-enable signal (debug_we_i) is propagated from fc_subsystem through zeroriscy_core to zeroriscy_debug_unit without any authentication or privilege guard. Specifically, in the SECOND-cycle path for CSR writes via the debug interface, csr_we_o is asserted unconditionally regardless of whether the core is halted, bypassing the debug_halted_o guard that is applied in the FIRST cycle.

evidence.description: The debug interface inputs (debug_req_i, debug_we_i, debug_addr_i, debug_wdata_i) are raw ports of zeroriscy_debug_unit with no authentication or privilege level enforcement at the port boundary.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug write access enters the core without any access control check at the interface boundary.

evidence.description: In the FIRST cycle (state_q == FIRST), csr_req_n is only set when debug_halted_o is asserted (line 151-154), enforcing a halted-core guard. However, in the SECOND cycle (else branch, lines 155-159), csr_we_o is set to 1'b1 unconditionally without re-checking debug_halted_o. An attacker who can manipulate state_q to SECOND can force a CSR write without the core being halted.
evidence.supports_claim: The halted-core guard applied in FIRST cycle is not re-checked in SECOND cycle where the actual write (csr_we_o=1) occurs, creating a guard bypass.

evidence.description: csr_op is set to CSR_OP_WRITE when dbg_csr_we is asserted, allowing the debug unit to overwrite any CSR in the core. This is the protected resource that can be illegitimately written if the guard is bypassed.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR registers are written based on dbg_csr_we (connected to csr_we_o from debug unit), which can be set without a valid halted-core check in SECOND state.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem directly wires debug_we, debug_addr, debug_wdata, debug_req, debug_gnt to zeroriscy_core inputs with no interposing permission filter or security check.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface is fully exposed from fc_subsystem to the core without any hardware access control layer.

evidence.description: For non-CSR debug register reads, debug_gnt_o is granted 'even when invalid access to not block' (line 223, 241, 247, 255) with guards only applied inside the grant (e.g., regfile_rreq_n gated by debug_halted_o). The comment 'grant it even when invalid access' indicates intentional bypass of access validation for timing reasons.
evidence.supports_claim: Grant is given unconditionally for multiple address regions; actual resource access is conditionally guarded by debug_halted_o but the grant itself is not, which may allow side-channel timing observations.

evidence.description: The stall/halt FSM in the debug unit controls debug_halted_o. The HALT state sets stall_o and debug_halted_o=1. If the FSM is in HALT_REQ or RUNNING, debug_halted_o is 0, meaning the SECOND-cycle CSR write bypass is exploitable whenever the core is not in HALT state.
evidence.supports_claim: The condition under which the SECOND-cycle guard bypass is exploitable corresponds to the HALT_REQ and RUNNING FSM states where debug_halted_o=0.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug unit implements a two-cycle handshake for CSR accesses. In the FIRST cycle, csr_req_n is gated by debug_halted_o, enforcing that CSR reads are only initiated when the core is halted. However, in the SECOND cycle, csr_we_o is set unconditionally (line 158: csr_we_o = 1'b1) without re-checking debug_halted_o. This means if an attacker can drive the state machine to SECOND while the core is not halted, a CSR write will be committed. Additionally, the entire debug interface path from fc_subsystem through zeroriscy_core to zeroriscy_debug_unit lacks any authentication, privilege, or lifecycle gating, meaning any agent with access to the debug bus can issue debug requests.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug bus can write arbitrary CSR values (including machine-mode privilege registers, interrupt configuration, etc.) potentially without the core being in a debug-halted state, bypassing the intended security invariant that debug CSR access requires the core to be halted. This could enable privilege escalation, disabling of security features (e.g., PMP), or arbitrary code execution.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact mechanism by which state_q reaches SECOND without debug_halted_o being set would require tracing the full FSM transition logic more carefully. It is possible that the FIRST-cycle guard (csr_req_n gated by debug_halted_o) effectively prevents the SECOND cycle from being reached with a pending CSR write when not halted, since csr_req_q would be 0. However, the csr_we_o assignment in SECOND cycle does not check csr_req_q or debug_halted_o, leaving the logic formally incomplete. Full exploit feasibility depends on whether state_n=SECOND can be committed without csr_req_n being set, which requires deeper FSM analysis not fully covered by available snippets.

finding.summary: The debug unit can directly request halt of the CPU instruction fetch (halt_if) and stall/jump the pipeline via dbg_req_i, dbg_stall_i, and dbg_jump_req_i signals without any privilege or authentication check visible in the RTL chain. The debug interface signals are passed straight from the debug unit through the ID stage controller to halt the IF stage, with no access-control guard interposed.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_debug_unit exposes debug_req_i, debug_halt_i, debug_resume_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i and produces stall_o, dbg_req_o, jump_req_o, jump_addr_o — all of which feed directly into the core pipeline with no visible authentication or privilege-level check in the module interface.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit accepts external debug requests and drives halt/stall/jump signals without any access-control gating shown in the source.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_controller receives dbg_req_i, dbg_stall_i, and dbg_jump_req_i as plain inputs and drives halt_if_o directly. No privilege check, lock register, or authentication condition is present in the module port list or the visible FSM default-value block (SNIP-0005) before these debug signals influence halt_if_o.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug signals enter the controller and affect halt_if_o without any guard logic being present in the visible source.

evidence.description: halt_if is declared as a plain logic wire in zeroriscy_core (line 170) and is directly driven from the controller/ID stage output and fed to the IF stage, representing the CPU fetch-halt mechanism that can stop instruction execution.
evidence.supports_claim: halt_if is the security-sensitive resource that stops CPU fetch; it is driven by unauthenticated debug control paths.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_id_stage passes dbg_req_i, dbg_stall_i, dbg_jump_req_i through to the controller and outputs halt_if_o back to the core, with no intermediate permission check in the module port declarations.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug signals traverse the ID stage to the controller without any interposed access check.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_if_stage accepts halt_if_i as a plain input (line 66) that unconditionally halts instruction fetch when asserted, and also accepts dbg_jump_addr_i (line 64) to redirect the PC from the debug unit, with no authentication gate at this level.
evidence.supports_claim: An unauthenticated debug request can halt instruction fetch and redirect the program counter, enabling full CPU control without any privilege check.

evidence.description: Lines 613-617 show that csr_addr, csr_wdata, and csr_op are muxed between normal operation and debug unit values (dbg_csr_req, dbg_csr_we, dbg_csr_wdata) with only a single-bit dbg_csr_req flag as the selector — no privilege-level or authentication check guards the debug CSR write path.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug CSR writes bypass normal privilege checks, allowing the debug unit to write arbitrary CSR values if debug_req_i is asserted.

finding.reasoning_summary: The RTL chain shows that external debug signals (debug_req_i, debug_halt_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i) enter zeroriscy_debug_unit, which produces stall_o, dbg_req_o, jump_req_o and CSR write signals. These flow through zeroriscy_id_stage and zeroriscy_controller to drive halt_if, which halts the IF stage, and also mux in debug CSR writes — all without any access-control guard, lock register, or privilege check visible in the source snippets. Any entity that can assert debug_req_i can halt the CPU and overwrite CSRs.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface (e.g., via JTAG or a compromised debug master) can unconditionally halt CPU instruction fetch, redirect the program counter to an arbitrary address, and overwrite CSR registers including machine-mode privilege state, without satisfying any authentication or privilege check. This enables full CPU takeover.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full body of zeroriscy_controller's FSM case statements is not shown (only the port list and default-value block are visible), so it is possible that some debug-enable gating exists deeper in the FSM logic that is not captured in the provided snippets. Similarly, the debug unit's internal authentication FSM body is not fully shown. The finding is based on the absence of visible guards in the provided evidence.

finding.summary: The debug unit can directly write arbitrary data to any general-purpose register via dbg_reg_wreq/dbg_reg_waddr/dbg_reg_wdata without any privilege level check, authentication, or halted-state guard in the register file write mux.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_debug_unit exposes regfile_wreq_o, regfile_waddr_o, and regfile_wdata_o ports that are driven directly from the external debug interface (debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_addr_i). There is no evidence of a privilege check or halted-state requirement before asserting regfile_wreq_o.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit can assert a register file write request based solely on external debug interface inputs.

evidence.description: dbg_reg_wreq is ORed into dbg_busy and clock_en, showing it is treated as a normal operational signal rather than a guarded privileged operation. No halted/stalled check gates the signal.
evidence.supports_claim: dbg_reg_wreq propagates freely without a CPU-halted or privilege check interlock.

evidence.description: In the register file write mux (always_comb block), the first priority branch is: if(dbg_reg_wreq_i) begin regfile_wdata_mux = dbg_reg_wdata_i; regfile_waddr_mux = dbg_reg_waddr_i; regfile_we_mux = 1'b1; end. This unconditionally overrides normal instruction writeback with debug-supplied data and address whenever dbg_reg_wreq_i is asserted, with no check that the core is actually halted, in a privileged debug mode, or that the requestor has been authenticated.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug register writes bypass all normal pipeline permission checks and can occur while the core is executing normal code.

evidence.description: The register file write port (waddr_a_i, wdata_a_i, we_a_i) is the protected resource. It receives regfile_waddr_mux, regfile_wdata_mux, and regfile_we_mux, which can be overridden by the debug path at any time.
evidence.supports_claim: The general-purpose register file can be written arbitrarily by the debug unit without halting the core.

evidence.description: assign dbg_reg_rdata_o = regfile_data_rb_id also exposes register read data back to the debug interface without restriction, enabling both read and write of all GPRs from the debug port without privilege enforcement.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive register contents can be read back and overwritten through the debug interface at any privilege level.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug unit drives dbg_reg_wreq (connected to zeroriscy_id_stage.dbg_reg_wreq_i) from the external debug interface. In the ID stage register file write mux (SNIP-0015), dbg_reg_wreq_i is the highest-priority condition and unconditionally asserts we_a_i=1 with debug-supplied address and data. There is no guard checking that the CPU is halted, that a debug authentication has been passed, or that the core is in any specific privilege state before this override takes effect. This means an attacker with access to the debug interface can write any value to any GPR while the processor is running normal code, corrupting architectural state, hijacking control flow, or escalating privileges.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug bus can write arbitrary values into any general-purpose register at any time, including during normal execution, enabling control-flow hijacking, privilege escalation, or data corruption without any authentication or halted-state prerequisite.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal logic of zeroriscy_debug_unit that generates regfile_wreq is not fully shown in snippets (only the port declaration and partial internal signals are visible). It is possible that the debug unit internally requires debug_halt_i to be asserted before driving regfile_wreq_o, but this is not evidenced in the provided source snippets. The ID stage mux itself has no such guard.

finding.summary: Debug unit can unconditionally write to the CPU register file without any privilege, lock, or authentication check. The dbg_reg_wreq signal from the debug unit overrides the normal register file write path with the highest priority, bypassing all normal pipeline controls.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_debug_unit exposes regfile_wreq_o, regfile_waddr_o, and regfile_wdata_o as direct outputs. There is no privilege level check, no debug authentication, and no lock register visible at this interface level. Any debug request that arrives via debug_req_i can trigger a register file write.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit has an unconditional path to write arbitrary data to any general-purpose register via regfile_wreq_o without authentication or privilege gating.

evidence.description: In zeroriscy_id_stage, the always_comb block gives dbg_reg_wreq_i the highest priority: when dbg_reg_wreq_i is asserted, regfile_we_mux=1, regfile_waddr_mux=dbg_reg_waddr_i, and regfile_wdata_mux=dbg_reg_wdata_i are set unconditionally, overriding all normal pipeline-driven register write logic. There is no check for a debug-enable bit, privilege mode, or any other guard before this override takes effect.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug register write request unconditionally bypasses all normal write-enable, write-address, and write-data controls with no guard logic present.

evidence.description: dbg_reg_wreq is declared as a plain logic signal in zeroriscy_core and is driven directly from the debug unit's regfile_wreq_o output and forwarded to the ID stage. There is no interposing permission state or enable register visible in the signal chain.
evidence.supports_claim: The signal path from debug unit to register file write port contains no permission gate or enable register.

evidence.description: The comment '//TODO: add assertion' at line 391 in the register file mux block explicitly acknowledges missing verification/assertion for the debug write override, indicating the design authors were aware of an unguarded condition that was never completed.
evidence.supports_claim: The absence of a guard assertion is acknowledged in the source code itself, confirming missing protection on the debug register write path.

evidence.description: The debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_addr_i) is exposed as top-level inputs to zeroriscy_core with no hardware-enforced authentication or privilege check in the core's port list. Any external entity with access to these pins can trigger arbitrary register file writes.
evidence.supports_claim: The CPU register file is a security-sensitive resource and the debug interface port that controls it lacks hardware-enforced access control.

finding.reasoning_summary: The chain traces from the debug unit's regfile_wreq_o output through dbg_reg_wreq in zeroriscy_core to dbg_reg_wreq_i in zeroriscy_id_stage. SNIP-0019 shows that dbg_reg_wreq_i unconditionally overrides the normal register file write mux with the highest priority — no privilege check, no debug-enable lock register, and no authentication gate is present in the path. The TODO comment in the same block acknowledges the missing assertion. The debug interface inputs at the top level (SNIP-0004) are plain inputs with no hardware lock. This constitutes a missing guard on a debug path that allows arbitrary general-purpose register overwrites.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or malicious external agent with access to the debug interface pins can write arbitrary values to any CPU general-purpose register at any time, regardless of processor privilege mode or execution state. This can be used to hijack control flow, corrupt security-critical state, or escalate privileges within the system.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is possible that an external debug authentication module exists at a higher level of the SoC hierarchy (e.g., in pulp_soc) that gates debug_req_i before it reaches the core. However, no such guard is visible in the provided source snippets, and the in-code TODO comment strongly suggests the guard was intentionally omitted.

finding.summary: The debug unit (zeroriscy_debug_unit) can directly access and write any CSR register without privilege-level checking, bypassing normal CSR access controls enforced for regular instruction execution.

evidence.description: The CSR address, write data, and operation are muxed between normal pipeline access and debug unit access based solely on dbg_csr_req. When dbg_csr_req==1, the debug unit's csr_addr and csr_wdata are passed directly to the CSR register file with no privilege check or access restriction. The debug unit can issue CSR_OP_WRITE to any CSR address.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug unit bypasses privilege checking when accessing CSRs; any CSR can be read or written via debug interface without enforcing machine-mode privilege requirements.

evidence.description: The zeroriscy_debug_unit exposes csr_req_o, csr_addr_o, csr_we_o, and csr_wdata_o ports, allowing arbitrary CSR address and write data to be driven from the debug interface. There is no visible privilege-level gating on these outputs.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug unit provides an unrestricted path to issue CSR read/write requests to any CSR address.

evidence.description: The CSR register file (zeroriscy_cs_registers) includes security-critical registers: mstatus (interrupt enable), mepc (exception return address), and mcause. These are accessible via csr_access_i/csr_addr_i/csr_wdata_i without any privilege-level input port to enforce access restrictions from the debug path.
evidence.supports_claim: Machine-mode CSRs including mstatus.mie are writable by any caller that drives csr_access_i and csr_we_int, including the debug unit.

evidence.description: The write logic in zeroriscy_cs_registers checks csr_we_int and csr_addr_i but does not check any privilege level input. There is no port or logic that differentiates between a debug-unit-originated write and a normal instruction-originated write to machine-mode CSRs.
evidence.supports_claim: No privilege guard exists in CSR write path to block debug unit from modifying machine-mode registers such as mstatus.

evidence.description: m_irq_enable_o is derived directly from mstatus_q.mie. If the debug unit writes mstatus via the unguarded CSR path, it can disable machine-mode interrupts, affecting the interrupt enable state of the processor.
evidence.supports_claim: Exploitation of the unguarded debug CSR write path can disable interrupt handling, representing a security and availability impact.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0011 shows that csr_addr, csr_wdata, and csr_op are muxed to the debug unit's outputs (dbg_csr_addr, dbg_csr_wdata, dbg_csr_we) whenever dbg_csr_req is asserted, with no privilege-level enforcement. SNIP-0016 confirms the debug unit has full CSR read/write capability via its output ports. SNIP-0012 and SNIP-0013 show the CSR register file has no privilege-level input and no guard distinguishing debug-originated from instruction-originated CSR writes. SNIP-0014 shows that machine-mode security-critical state (mstatus.mie, mepc) is directly writable through this path. This constitutes a missing privilege guard on the debug CSR access path.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can read or write any CSR, including machine-mode privilege registers (mstatus, mepc, mcause), without any privilege enforcement. This can be used to disable interrupts, corrupt exception return addresses, or escalate privileges by manipulating machine-mode state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The debug interface may itself be protected by a higher-level access control (e.g., debug authentication) not visible in these snippets. If debug_req_i is gated by a secure authentication mechanism external to these modules, the risk is mitigated. Additionally, the full logic inside zeroriscy_debug_unit determining when csr_req_o is asserted is only partially shown (SNIP-0016 shows the port declaration but not the full state machine logic controlling csr_req_o assertion conditions).

finding.summary: The debug unit can directly access and modify any CSR without privilege checks. The csr_addr and csr_wdata muxes unconditionally accept debug unit requests (dbg_csr_req) with no authentication, privilege level verification, or lock mechanism guarding the substitution.

evidence.description: Lines 613-617 show that csr_addr, csr_wdata, and csr_op are multiplexed between normal CPU execution and the debug unit solely based on dbg_csr_req. When dbg_csr_req is asserted, the debug unit's dbg_csr_addr and dbg_csr_wdata are used directly with no privilege check, no lock gate, and no authentication guard before they are forwarded to the CSR register file.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit can unconditionally override CSR access (address, write data, and operation) without any privilege level check or access control enforcement.

evidence.description: The zeroriscy_debug_unit module exposes csr_req_o, csr_addr_o, csr_we_o, and csr_wdata_o ports that are driven from the external debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i). There is no privilege level check on the debug interface port; any external debug request can issue arbitrary CSR reads/writes.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface accepts external requests and translates them into CSR operations without verifying any access permission state.

evidence.description: mstatus (0x300), mepc, and mcause are security-sensitive CSRs written in the always_comb block keyed on csr_addr_i and csr_we_int. Because the debug path can override csr_addr and csr_wdata (as shown in SNIP-0009), the debug unit can arbitrarily write to these privilege-controlling registers.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive M-mode CSRs including mstatus.mie, mepc, and mcause are reachable through the unguarded debug CSR override path.

evidence.description: m_irq_enable_o is directly driven from mstatus_q.mie and mepc_o from mepc_q. Since the debug path can modify these registers without privilege verification, interrupt enable state and exception return address can be tampered with by an unprivileged debug accessor.
evidence.supports_claim: Corruption of mstatus.mie via the debug path would disable machine-mode interrupts; corruption of mepc would redirect mret execution flow.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0009 is the key evidence: csr_addr, csr_wdata, and csr_op are multiplexed unconditionally between normal CPU flow and the debug unit based only on dbg_csr_req, with no privilege level guard or lock check at the mux. SNIP-0014 confirms the debug unit drives these signals from an external debug interface without any internal access-control logic. SNIP-0011 and SNIP-0012 confirm that M-mode security CSRs (mstatus, mepc, mcause) are writable through this path, enabling an attacker with debug access to manipulate machine-mode interrupt enable, exception return address, and machine cause register without any authorization check.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical or logical access to the debug interface can read or write any CSR including mstatus (disabling M-mode interrupts), mepc (redirecting mret), and mcause, bypassing all software privilege controls. This enables privilege escalation, denial of interrupt service, and arbitrary control-flow redirection in the processor.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippets do not show whether the debug interface itself requires authentication before debug_req_i is asserted at the SoC boundary (e.g., a JTAG authentication layer above fc_subsystem). If such a layer exists and is correctly implemented, the exploitability at runtime would be reduced, but the RTL itself contains no such guard.

finding.summary: The debug interface can override CSR address selection without privilege or authentication checks, allowing the debug unit to directly access and modify any CSR including security-sensitive registers (mstatus, mepc, mcause).

evidence.description: At line 613, the CSR address mux unconditionally switches to dbg_csr_addr when dbg_csr_req is asserted: 'assign csr_addr = (dbg_csr_req == 1'b0) ? csr_addr_int : dbg_csr_addr;'. Similarly, csr_access is forced to 1'b1 and csr_op is set to CSR_OP_WRITE when dbg_csr_we is asserted. There is no privilege level check, authentication, or lifecycle guard on dbg_csr_req before it overrides normal CSR access.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit can unconditionally override CSR address and write-enable signals to access any CSR register without any permission or privilege check in the RTL path.

evidence.description: dbg_csr_req, dbg_csr_addr, dbg_csr_we, and dbg_csr_wdata are declared as internal logic signals in zeroriscy_core with no visible gating or protection mechanism before being fed into the CSR access mux.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug CSR access path signals flow directly into the CSR mux without any interposing lock, privilege, or lifecycle check.

evidence.description: The CSR register file in zeroriscy_cs_registers contains security-sensitive registers: mstatus (interrupt enable, privilege mode), mepc (exception return address), and mcause (exception cause). These are written based on csr_we_int derived from csr_op_i which can be forced to CSR_OP_WRITE by the debug path.
evidence.supports_claim: Security-critical CSRs controlling privilege mode and interrupt state are reachable through the unguarded debug access path.

evidence.description: The debug interface signals debug_req_i, debug_we_i, debug_addr_i, debug_wdata_i are module-level inputs to zeroriscy_core with no documented authentication or lifecycle state gating in the RTL.
evidence.supports_claim: External debug signals enter the core as plain inputs and feed into the unguarded CSR override mux.

evidence.description: mstatus_q.mie (interrupt enable) and mpp (privilege mode) are directly readable and writable via the CSR interface. The debug path at line 613 can bypass normal instruction-level access controls to write these fields.
evidence.supports_claim: Privilege and interrupt-enable state can be altered by the debug interface without a privilege check, undermining the processor's security state.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source code at zeroriscy_core.sv line 613 shows a 4-way mux that unconditionally overrides the CSR address, access, write-data, and operation signals when dbg_csr_req is asserted. No privilege level verification, lifecycle state check, authentication token, or lock bit is present in any of the provided snippets to gate this debug override path. The debug CSR path feeds directly into the same zeroriscy_cs_registers module that manages mstatus (privilege/interrupt enable), mepc, and mcause. An attacker or untrusted debug agent that can assert dbg_csr_req can read or write any CSR without restriction.

This can disable interrupts, corrupt exception handling state, escalate privilege, or disrupt system operation.

finding.reasoning_summary: In zeroriscy_core, the mux at line 613 routes dbg_csr_addr to the CSR register file whenever dbg_csr_req is asserted, with no privilege-level check, no lifecycle lock, and no authentication. The CSR register file (zeroriscy_cs_registers) performs reads and writes based solely on the address presented in csr_addr_i; there is no field in the interface that carries the requester's privilege level, and no guard that distinguishes a debug-path access from a normal instruction-path access. This allows an external debugger to read and write all machine-mode CSRs (mstatus, mepc, mcause, mhartid) without any privilege or authentication enforcement.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can read sensitive machine state (interrupt enable bits, exception program counter, privilege level fields, hart identity) and overwrite machine-mode CSRs including mstatus.MIE (disabling interrupts), mepc (redirecting exception returns to arbitrary addresses), and mcause, potentially enabling privilege escalation or denial-of-service against the secure execution environment.

finding.summary: The debug unit can read and write any CSR without privilege or mode checks, and CSR read data flows directly to debug_rdata_o exposing sensitive register state to an unauthenticated debug interface.

evidence.description: The CSR read logic exposes mstatus (interrupt enable state), mepc (exception program counter), mcause (exception cause), and mhartid (hardware thread ID) via csr_rdata_int without any privilege-level check in the read path itself.

When the debug unit asserts dbg_csr_we, the mux in SNIP-0007 sets csr_op to CSR_OP_WRITE, which will set csr_we_int, allowing writes to mstatus.mie, mepc, and mcause from the debug interface without privilege verification.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit can overwrite critical machine-mode CSRs (interrupt enable, exception PC, exception cause) without any privilege or authentication check.

evidence.supports_claim: Combined with CSR access, the debug unit can modify both GPRs and CSRs without any authentication guard, giving full control over the CPU state.

finding.reasoning_summary: The chain shows that the debug unit (zeroriscy_debug_unit) drives dbg_csr_req, dbg_csr_we, dbg_csr_wdata, and dbg_csr_addr signals into the core. In zeroriscy_core, a combinational mux (SNIP-0007) unconditionally selects debug-supplied values for csr_wdata, csr_op, and csr_addr whenever dbg_csr_req is high. There is no privilege check, lifecycle lock, or authentication guard at this mux or in the CSR register file (SNIP-0009). The CSR read data (csr_rdata) flows back to the debug unit (edge:connects:270) and is routed to debug_rdata_o (SNIP-0013) without filtering. Additionally, GPR writes from the debug unit bypass normal write-enable logic entirely (SNIP-0018). Any entity able to drive the external debug interface inputs can read or write machine-mode CSRs and GPRs.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can: (1) read sensitive machine-mode CSRs including mstatus (interrupt enable), mepc (exception return address), mcause, and mhartid; (2) write arbitrary values to these CSRs, disabling interrupts, redirecting exception returns, or corrupting exception state; (3) overwrite any general-purpose register. This constitutes a complete privilege escalation and arbitrary CPU state corruption vulnerability.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not confirmed from the provided snippets whether the debug interface itself has an external authentication mechanism (e.g., a debug authentication signal) at the SoC level that would gate debug_req_i before it reaches this module. The module-level RTL contains no such guard.

finding.summary: The MRET instruction handler in zeroriscy_controller asserts csr_restore_mret_id_o unconditionally based solely on mret_insn_i being decoded, without verifying the current privilege level or any authorization check. This allows the CSR mret restore path to be triggered from any privilege context, potentially enabling privilege escalation by restoring machine-mode CSR state (mepc, mstatus.MIE from MPIE) without a legitimate prior exception entry.

evidence.description: At line 429-435, when mret_insn_i is asserted the controller unconditionally sets csr_restore_mret_id_o = 1'b1 and redirects the PC to PC_ERET with no privilege-level check or guard condition. There is no verification that the processor is operating in M-mode before allowing the mret restore to proceed.
evidence.supports_claim: csr_restore_mret_id_o is asserted without any privilege-mode guard, allowing mret to execute from any mode and restore machine-mode CSR state.

evidence.description: The default initialization at line 165 sets csr_restore_mret_id_o = 1'b0. The only place it becomes 1 is the unconditional mret_insn_i branch, confirming the absence of any privilege check in the path.
evidence.supports_claim: No privilege or mode-check state influences the csr_restore_mret_id_o assignment; only the decoded instruction type gates this signal.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_cs_registers receives csr_restore_mret_i as a direct input port (line 69). When asserted, this causes the CSR module to restore machine-mode registers including mepc and mstatus (MIE restored from MPIE), which are privileged resources that should only be accessible from M-mode via a legitimate mret.
evidence.supports_claim: The CSR restore operation directly modifies privileged machine-mode state based solely on the csr_restore_mret_i signal with no further authorization in the CSR module.

evidence.description: The controller port list (lines 33-106) shows no current-privilege-level input signal. Inputs such as m_IE_i (M-mode interrupt enable) exist for IRQ gating, but there is no mmode_i or priv_lvl_i signal that could be used to guard the mret path. The design lacks privilege-level awareness in the controller.
evidence.supports_claim: Absence of a privilege-level input to zeroriscy_controller means the mret guard cannot be privilege-checked at this stage.

finding.reasoning_summary: The RISC-V specification requires that MRET is only executable in M-mode; executing it from a lower privilege level should raise an illegal instruction exception. In this implementation, zeroriscy_controller decodes mret_insn_i and directly asserts csr_restore_mret_id_o without checking the current privilege level. The controller has no privilege-level input port, so no such check is possible. The CSR register file then performs the restore unconditionally when the signal is received. This creates a missing privilege guard on a security-critical operation.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or faulty software executing at a lower privilege level (if the design were extended to support U-mode or S-mode) could execute MRET to restore machine-mode CSR state, including re-enabling machine-mode interrupts (MIE from MPIE) and redirecting the PC to a controlled mepc value. This constitutes a privilege escalation path. Even in the current M-mode-only implementation, the absence of the guard means the design does not enforce the architectural requirement, leaving the door open as the design evolves.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The current zeroriscy design may be intended as M-mode only, in which case all instructions execute at M-mode and the missing check may be considered acceptable by design. The source snippets do not show the full cs_registers restore logic to confirm exactly which registers are modified. Additionally, the illegal_insn_i path for mret in lower-privilege modes is not shown, so it is uncertain whether a separate decoder-level check exists.

finding.summary: The debug unit can unconditionally write arbitrary data to any CSR without privilege or lock checks, bypassing normal instruction-path access controls.

evidence.description: Lines 612-617 show that when dbg_csr_req is asserted, csr_access is forced to 1'b1, csr_addr is taken from dbg_csr_addr, and csr_wdata is taken from dbg_csr_wdata. The csr_op is set to CSR_OP_WRITE when dbg_csr_we is asserted. evidence.description: m_irq_enable_o is directly wired from mstatus_q.mie and mepc_o from mepc_q. An attacker with access to the debug interface can overwrite mstatus to disable machine-mode interrupts or overwrite mepc to redirect exception returns to arbitrary addresses.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0012 conclusively shows that when dbg_csr_req is asserted, the mux unconditionally overrides csr_access, csr_addr, csr_wdata, and csr_op with debug-unit-supplied values and forwards them directly to the CSR register file. finding.security_impact: Any entity with access to the debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_addr_i) can arbitrarily write machine-mode CSRs including mstatus (disabling M-mode interrupts), mepc (redirecting mret to attacker-controlled address), and mcause, without any privilege check or lock enforcement. This can lead to privilege escalation, interrupt disabling, or control-flow hijacking.

finding.summary: The debug unit can directly read and write any CSR without privilege or mode checks. finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0007 conclusively shows the mux: when dbg_csr_req==1, the CSR address, write data, and operation are taken entirely from the debug unit's outputs. There is no privilege level check, no lock register, and no authentication check on this path. The debug unit (SNIP-0011) can request writes to any 12-bit CSR address. The CSR register file (SNIP-0008) enforces only csr_we_int, which is asserted when the debug unit drives CSR_OP_WRITE. This means the debug interface has unrestricted read/write access to all machine-mode CSRs including mstatus, mepc, and mcause, without any privilege enforcement.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can read or overwrite any CSR, including mstatus (interrupt enable, privilege mode), mepc (exception return address), and mcause, effectively compromising the security and integrity of the processor's privilege model. This could be used to escalate privilege, disable interrupts, or corrupt exception handling.

It is possible that the debug unit only asserts dbg_csr_req when the core is halted, but this is a debug-access policy concern and does not constitute a privilege check on which CSR addresses can be accessed or what values can be written.

finding.summary: The debug unit can unconditionally write to any CSR without privilege checking. When dbg_csr_req is asserted, the CSR address, write data, and operation are sourced entirely from the debug unit, bypassing any privilege-level or access-permission guard that would normally gate CSR writes from normal instruction execution.

There is no privilege-level check or lock guard on this debug path; any assertion of dbg_csr_req unconditionally overrides the normal CSR access path.

evidence.description: The debug unit exposes csr_req_o, csr_addr_o, csr_we_o, and csr_wdata_o as outputs driven by the external debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i) with no internal privilege filtering before these are passed to the CSR mux.

When the debug path forces csr_op=CSR_OP_WRITE, csr_we_int will be asserted and these privileged registers will be updated without any additional privilege guard.

evidence.description: m_irq_enable_o is directly driven from mstatus_q.mie, and mepc_o from mepc_q. If a debug-path write corrupts mstatus or mepc, interrupt enable state and exception return address are compromised.

finding.reasoning_summary: The multiplexer at lines 612-617 of zeroriscy_core.sv gives the debug unit complete control over the CSR address, write data, and operation when dbg_csr_req is high. The CSR register file (zeroriscy_cs_registers) has no second-level privilege check; it trusts csr_we_int derived from the incoming csr_op. The debug unit itself performs no privilege-level filtering before asserting its CSR request outputs. This means any external agent that can assert debug_req_i and control debug_addr_i/debug_we_i/debug_wdata_i can freely read or write any implemented CSR, including machine-mode privileged registers like mstatus, mepc, and mcause.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can write arbitrary values to machine-mode CSRs, enabling: disabling machine-mode interrupts (clearing mstatus.mie), redirecting exception return (corrupting mepc), or forging exception cause (mcause). This undermines the entire privilege and exception-handling security model of the RISC-V core.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not shown in the provided snippets whether the debug interface itself is protected by a higher-level authentication or access-control mechanism external to this RTL (e.g., JTAG authentication). If such protection exists at a higher integration level, the severity would be reduced, but within this RTL the bypass is unconditional.

finding.summary: The debug unit can unconditionally overwrite CSR address, CSR write-enable, and write-data (including mstatus) without any privilege check or authentication guard.

evidence.description: The CSR address mux at line 613 selects dbg_csr_addr whenever dbg_csr_req==1. Similarly the op mux at lines 615-617 forces CSR_OP_WRITE when dbg_csr_we==1. evidence.description: The core exposes debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, and debug_wdata_i as top-level inputs with no indication of a hardware lock or privilege filter before they drive dbg_csr_req/dbg_csr_addr/dbg_csr_we/dbg_csr_wdata.
evidence.supports_claim: Any entity that can assert these pins gains write access to all CSRs including security-critical mstatus.

evidence.description: mstatus (address 0x300) controls MIE (machine interrupt enable) and MPIE. Writing it can disable interrupts or manipulate privilege-restore state. The write path at line 197-203 is gated only on csr_we_int, which is set to 1 by default (line 243) and is only cleared for CSR_OP_NONE. A debug write with CSR_OP_WRITE sets csr_we_int=1 unconditionally.
evidence.supports_claim: mstatus can be overwritten via the debug path without any additional guard.

evidence.description: The CSR write logic (lines 189-236) checks only csr_we_int and csr_addr_i. There is no check for a privilege level, no debug-mode lock register, and no write-protect field. csr_we_int defaults to 1'b1 (line 243), so any access with a non-NONE op will write.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing privilege or debug-mode guard on mstatus write path.

evidence.description: Lines 612-617 show that when dbg_csr_req==1 the address is replaced by dbg_csr_addr and the operation is forced to CSR_OP_WRITE (if dbg_csr_we==1), bypassing the normal instruction-driven CSR path and all associated privilege checks. There is no intervening authentication or lock signal gating this substitution.
evidence.supports_claim: Bypass of normal privilege-checked CSR access through the debug port allows unprivileged modification of mstatus (and other M-mode CSRs).

evidence.description: A successful debug-path write to mstatus (0x300) can clear MIE (disabling all machine-mode interrupts) or manipulate MPIE/MPP, potentially allowing privilege escalation upon a subsequent MRET or causing denial-of-service by disabling interrupts.
evidence.supports_claim: Security impact: disabling interrupts or manipulating privilege-restore fields via unauthenticated debug CSR write.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0006 shows that dbg_csr_req unconditionally overrides the CSR address and forces a write operation with no interposed privilege or authentication check. SNIP-0001 confirms the debug signals are raw top-level inputs. SNIP-0007 shows mstatus is writable whenever csr_we_int==1 (the default) with csr_addr_i==0x300, and the write logic contains no privilege-level gate. Together these form a complete, evidenced bypass of privilege protection on mstatus.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface pins can write arbitrary values to mstatus and other M-mode CSRs (mepc, mcause) without satisfying any privilege or authentication requirement, enabling interrupt disabling, privilege escalation via MRET, or corruption of exception-return state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: No evidence of a higher-level debug authentication module (e.g., JTAG DAP with auth) wrapping these signals in the provided snippets; if such a module exists upstream it could mitigate the risk, but no such guard is visible in the provided source.

finding.summary: The debug unit can directly override CSR address and write data without privilege checks, allowing unrestricted modification of security-critical CSRs including mstatus (which controls interrupt enable MIE/MPIE bits governing execution privilege).

evidence.description: Line 613: 'assign csr_addr = (dbg_csr_req == 1'b0) ? csr_addr_int : dbg_csr_addr;' and line 614: 'assign csr_wdata = (dbg_csr_req == 1'b0) ? alu_operand_a_ex : dbg_csr_wdata;' show that when dbg_csr_req is asserted, the debug unit's address (dbg_csr_addr) and write data (dbg_csr_wdata) are substituted directly for the normal CSR access path with no privilege level check or access filtering. The csr_op is set to CSR_OP_WRITE unconditionally when dbg_csr_we is asserted.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug unit CSR access bypasses all privilege and access-control checks, allowing writes to any CSR including mstatus.

evidence.description: Lines 197-203 show mstatus write logic: '12'h300: if (csr_we_int) begin mstatus_n = '{mie: csr_wdata_int[MSTATUS_MIE_BITS], mpie: csr_wdata_int[MSTATUS_MPIE_BITS], mpp: PrivLvl_t'(PRIV_LVL_M)}; end'. The mstatus register, which controls the machine interrupt enable (MIE) bit, is writable via the standard CSR write path that the debug unit can inject into without restriction.
evidence.supports_claim: mstatus including MIE and MPIE bits are modifiable by the debug-unit-injected write path.

evidence.description: Line 272: 'assign m_irq_enable_o = mstatus_q.mie;' shows that the interrupt enable output used throughout the core is directly derived from mstatus_q.mie. If the debug unit arbitrarily modifies mstatus via the bypass path, it can enable or disable machine-mode interrupts.
evidence.supports_claim: Corruption of mstatus.mie via the debug bypass path directly controls machine-mode interrupt enablement.

evidence.description: The write logic at lines 188-236 applies no privilege-level check before updating mstatus, mepc, or mcause. The only gate is 'csr_we_int', which is set based on csr_op and csr_access regardless of privilege level. There is no check that the accessor is M-mode or that the debug interface is authorized to write security-critical CSRs.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing privilege-level or authorization guard on CSR writes allows the debug unit to overwrite mstatus unconditionally.

evidence.description: Lines 612-617 implement the debug mux: debug CSR request (dbg_csr_req) causes the debug address and data to replace the normal pipeline address and data with no additional gating. dbg_csr_we directly controls whether the operation becomes CSR_OP_WRITE.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface has unconditional write access to all CSRs including privilege-sensitive ones like mstatus.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_core implements a debug CSR access path (dbg_csr_req/dbg_csr_addr/dbg_csr_wdata) that is muxed directly onto the CSR bus.
