When the debug unit asserts dbg_csr_req, the CSR address and write data are taken from the debug unit's signals without any privilege check, access-control filtering, or lock mechanism. This injected access flows into zeroriscy_cs_registers where the write logic for mstatus (address 0x300) only checks csr_we_int—which is derived from the operation type, not from a privilege level. As a result, the debug unit can write arbitrary values to mstatus, directly controlling mstatus.mie (machine interrupt enable) and mstatus.mpie, as well as mepc and mcause, without any authorization check. The m_irq_enable_o output, used by the controller and interrupt controller throughout the pipeline, is directly assigned from mstatus_q.mie, so unauthorized modification has immediate security impact.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can arbitrarily set or clear mstatus.mie to suppress or force machine-mode interrupts, corrupt mepc to redirect MRET returns, and tamper with mcause—all without privilege checks. This allows privilege escalation, interrupt masking, and potential control-flow hijacking through CSR manipulation.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippets do not show whether the debug interface itself has an authentication or enable gate upstream (e.g., a debug_enable signal that is itself protected). If such a gate exists and is properly secured, the impact may be limited to scenarios where the debug interface is intentionally enabled. However, no such guard is visible in the provided source evidence.

finding.summary: The debug unit grants access to debug-only registers and can read back sensitive CSR/GPR data without requiring the core to be halted in all code paths. Specifically, debug register reads at address range 6'b10_0000 ("Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug") always grant the request and set rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGS regardless of whether the core is halted, leaking the read path. Additionally, when a CSR write is being completed in the SECOND state, csr_we_o is asserted unconditionally even if the core is not halted.

evidence.description: At line 240-244, the debug unit handles address range 6'b10_0000 ('Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug') by granting the request (debug_gnt_o = 1'b1) and setting rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGS unconditionally, without checking debug_halted_o. This allows reads from registers that the comment says should only be accessible when in debug mode, even when the core is running.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive debug-state registers can be read back even when the core is not halted, bypassing the intended halt guard.

evidence.description: At line 155-159, when state_q == SECOND for a CSR write, csr_we_o is asserted unconditionally (line 158: csr_we_o = 1'b1) without checking debug_halted_o. The guard 'if (debug_halted_o)' only applies to the FIRST state cycle (line 151-154 setting csr_req_n), meaning the second-cycle write enable fires regardless of halt state.
evidence.supports_claim: A CSR write can be committed via the debug interface even when the processor is not halted, bypassing the access-only-when-halted protection.

evidence.description: Lines 611-617 show the mux that routes CSR access through the debug unit: when dbg_csr_req is asserted, it overrides the normal CSR access path with debug-supplied address and write data, with no additional privilege or lifecycle check at this mux.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR registers are the protected resources that can be written by the debug unit without sufficient lifecycle guards.

evidence.description: Lines 384-391 show that the HALT state sets debug_halted_o=1 and stall_o=1, establishing the intended gate. The violation is that the write-enable path in SNIP-0011 does not re-check this state before completing a CSR write.
evidence.supports_claim: Halted state is the required permission state for protected access but is not re-verified in the CSR write completion path.

evidence.description: Lines 88-132 define the debug unit state machine and show that debug_gnt_o defaults to 0 but is explicitly granted in multiple paths. The comment at line 122 ('rdata_sel_n = RD_NONE') shows the intent to default to no read, but the case at SNIP-0013 lines 240-244 overrides this without a halt check.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug access controller architecture is designed to restrict access to halted state but has incomplete guards in multiple branches.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit has two concrete code-level violations of its own access-control comments: (1) reads from the '10_0000' debug register address range (labelled 'only accessible when in debug') grant and set rdata_sel without checking debug_halted_o; (2) CSR writes complete in the SECOND state without re-checking debug_halted_o, only the initiation of the request checks the halt state. These allow an attacker with access to the debug bus to read sensitive debug-state registers or write CSRs while the core is actively executing.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface bus can read sensitive processor debug state registers while the core is running (potential information disclosure) and can write arbitrary values to CSRs without the core being halted (potential privilege escalation, arbitrary code execution, or disruption of security-critical CSR state such as machine-mode privilege level, interrupt enable bits, or exception PC).
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full address decoding logic for all paths in always_comb block is not entirely visible in the provided snippets (some intermediate lines between SNIP-0011 and SNIP-0012 are missing). It is possible that some outer condition gates these paths, but based on available code the violations appear real. The exact threat model (whether the debug interface is accessible post-silicon or only in pre-production) also affects impact severity.

finding.summary: Debug halt can be triggered from the RUNNING state via the external `debug_halt_i` pin without any authentication or privilege check, allowing an attacker with access to the debug interface pins to halt the CPU core unconditionally.

evidence.description: In the RUNNING state, the condition `if (dbg_halt | debug_halt_i | trap_i)` at line 359 unconditionally transitions the FSM to HALT_REQ when `debug_halt_i` is asserted. There is no privilege check, authentication guard, or lock bit evaluated before accepting the external halt request.
evidence.supports_claim: The external `debug_halt_i` signal can halt the CPU from any state without any authorization check.

evidence.description: The write-path logic for DBG_CTRL (line 168-179) checks `debug_halted_o` before allowing a software-initiated halt via `dbg_halt`, but the hardware `debug_halt_i` pin bypasses this path entirely and feeds directly into the stall FSM without any equivalent guard.
evidence.supports_claim: Software-initiated halt has a partial state check (not-halted guard), but the hardware pin `debug_halt_i` has no equivalent protection.

evidence.description: The debug unit module accepts `debug_halt_i` as a direct input port (line 47) with no gating logic before it reaches the stall control FSM.
evidence.supports_claim: The `debug_halt_i` input is an unguarded entry point into the core halt mechanism.

evidence.description: The HALT state (lines 384-390) asserts `stall_o=1` and `debug_halted_o=1`, completely stopping the CPU pipeline. This is a security-sensitive resource that should require authorization before being entered.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthorized assertion of `debug_halt_i` directly causes the CPU to stall and assert debug-halted, which is a high-impact protected resource.

evidence.description: The `debug_halt_i` port is a top-level input to `zeroriscy_core` (line 85) and is wired directly into the debug unit without any SoC-level permission gating shown in the RTL.
evidence.supports_claim: The unguarded external pin propagates all the way to the CPU halt FSM through the core hierarchy.

finding.reasoning_summary: The stall FSM in `zeroriscy_debug_unit` transitions from RUNNING to HALT_REQ whenever `debug_halt_i` is asserted (SNIP-0008, line 359). Unlike the software register path where `dbg_halt` is only set when the core is not already halted and the write matches the correct address/data (SNIP-0006, lines 168-173), the hardware `debug_halt_i` pin has no corresponding guard. Any agent capable of driving this top-level input can unconditionally halt the CPU without any privilege or authentication check.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical or logical access to the `debug_halt_i` pin can halt the CPU at any time, causing denial of service, enabling debug-mode register access (GPR/CSR reads and writes are permitted once halted per SNIP-0006 lines 196-199), and potentially enabling further exploitation of the halted core's state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: No SoC-level wrapper or top-level integration file is present in the snippets to confirm whether `debug_halt_i` is further gated externally. If the SoC integration includes a security controller that gates this pin, the risk would be reduced, but no such evidence is available in the provided RTL.

finding.summary: The debug unit's CSR write path (csr_we_o) in the SECOND state is asserted unconditionally, even when the core is not halted, allowing CSR writes to proceed without the halted guard that protects the FIRST state's csr_req_n assertion.

evidence.description: In the FIRST state, csr_req_n=1 is only set when debug_halted_o is true (line 151-154). However, in the SECOND state (else branch at line 155-159), debug_gnt_o=1 and csr_we_o=1 are set unconditionally without re-checking debug_halted_o. This means a write enable to CSRs can be asserted even if the halted condition is no longer valid between cycles.
evidence.supports_claim: The guard checking debug_halted_o is applied only when setting csr_req_n in FIRST state, but csr_we_o in the SECOND state is asserted without any halted check, creating an inconsistent protection boundary.

evidence.description: For CSR read access (address bit 14 set, read path), csr_req_n=1 is only set when debug_halted_o is asserted (line 226-230), correctly guarding CSR read requests behind the halted check.
evidence.supports_claim: The read path applies the halted guard correctly, highlighting the asymmetry with the write path where csr_we_o is set unconditionally in SECOND state.

evidence.description: In zeroriscy_core, the CSR access mux (lines 611-617) routes dbg_csr_req and dbg_csr_we directly to the CSR register file inputs. When dbg_csr_req==1, the debug unit fully controls csr_access, csr_addr, csr_wdata, and csr_op sent to the CSR registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The CSR register file is the protected resource that can be written by the debug unit without a consistent halted-state guard.

evidence.description: The debug_halted_o signal is only asserted in the HALT state of the stall FSM (line 386). It is deasserted in RUNNING and HALT_REQ states, meaning a race between state transitions could result in csr_we_o being asserted when the core is not actually halted.
evidence.supports_claim: The halted permission state that should gate CSR write access is not re-verified in the SECOND state before asserting csr_we_o.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug unit implements a two-cycle protocol for CSR access. In the FIRST cycle, it correctly guards csr_req_n behind debug_halted_o. However, in the SECOND cycle (SECOND state), csr_we_o is asserted unconditionally (lines 156-158 of SNIP-0018) without re-checking debug_halted_o. Since debug_halted_o can change between the two cycles (e.g., if a resume occurs), this creates a window where a CSR write enable reaches the CSR register file even though the core may no longer be in the halted state. The CSR mux in zeroriscy_core passes dbg_csr_req and dbg_csr_we directly to the CSR file with no additional halted check.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or malformed debug sequence could trigger a CSR write to privileged control/status registers (e.g., machine-mode CSRs like mstatus, mepc, mtvec) while the core is not properly halted, potentially escalating privilege, corrupting exception handling state, or bypassing security controls enforced via CSRs.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full implementation of the state transition timing and whether csr_req_q (the registered version of csr_req_n) gating prevents the issue is not fully visible in the provided snippets. The actual registered output path for csr_req_o versus the combinational csr_we_o also introduces uncertainty about whether the two-cycle latency fully eliminates the race in practice.

finding.summary: The debug unit's CSR write enable (csr_we_o) is asserted in the SECOND state without verifying that the core is halted, allowing CSR writes through the debug interface even when the core is not in a halted/debug state.

evidence.description: In the SECOND state of the debug unit FSM (lines 155-158), csr_we_o is unconditionally set to 1'b1 and debug_gnt_o is asserted. There is no check on debug_halted_o before asserting csr_we_o. Compare this to the FIRST state (lines 151-154) where csr_req_n is only set if debug_halted_o is asserted. The SECOND state skips the halted check entirely when driving the actual CSR write enable.
evidence.supports_claim: csr_we_o is asserted without a debug_halted_o guard in the SECOND state, bypassing the access control that was applied in the FIRST state.

evidence.description: The debug unit initializes csr_we_o = 1'b0 at the start of the always_comb block (line 130), and the CSR write enable port is declared as an output driven solely by this combinational block. The only intended guard is debug_halted_o, but it is only checked in the FIRST state for csr_req_n.
evidence.supports_claim: The intended protection gate (debug_halted_o) is present for the request signal but absent for the actual write-enable output in the SECOND state.

evidence.description: In zeroriscy_core.sv (lines 611-617), when dbg_csr_req is asserted by the debug unit, csr_we_o (mapped to dbg_csr_we) directly controls CSR_OP_WRITE being applied to the CSR register file. An improperly asserted csr_we_o therefore causes an unconditional CSR write.
evidence.supports_claim: The CSR register file is directly written based on the debug unit's csr_we_o signal with no additional privilege or lifecycle gate in the core.

evidence.description: In the read path (lines 226-230), CSR access is explicitly guarded: 'if (debug_halted_o)' before setting csr_req_n and rdata_sel_n = RD_CSR. This confirms the design intent is to restrict debug CSR access to halted state, but the write path in SNIP-0011 lacks the equivalent guard.
evidence.supports_claim: The read path enforces debug_halted_o but the write path does not, creating an asymmetric protection gap.

evidence.description: The fc_subsystem declares debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, and debug_wdata as plain logic signals with no visible access control or privilege filtering before they are forwarded to zeroriscy_core.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug interface inputs arrive at the core without upstream filtering, meaning any agent able to drive these signals can reach the unguarded CSR write path.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit FSM uses a two-cycle handshake (FIRST/SECOND states) for CSR access. In FIRST, csr_req_n is only asserted if debug_halted_o is true, correctly gating the initiation of CSR access. However, in SECOND, csr_we_o is set to 1'b1 unconditionally (SNIP-0011, lines 155-158) without rechecking debug_halted_o. This means that if a debug request for a CSR address arrives while the core transitions out of halted state between cycles, or if the state machine reaches SECOND by other means, the CSR write enable will be asserted without the core being verified as halted. In contrast, the read path (SNIP-0013) correctly gates CSR access behind debug_halted_o. The core (SNIP-0008) directly maps this signal to CSR_OP_WRITE with no additional check, so a spurious csr_we_o assertion causes an unintended privileged CSR write.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or malfunction could cause the debug unit to write arbitrary values to any CSR (including mstatus, mepc, mtvec, and privilege-control CSRs) without the core being in the required halted/debug state. This could be used to escalate privilege, disable interrupt/exception handling, redirect execution flow, or corrupt security-critical machine-mode state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact timing window for the race between FIRST and SECOND states and the debug_halted_o signal is not fully observable from the static snippets alone. It is also possible that the caller (fc_subsystem or higher) enforces that debug requests are only issued when the core is halted, which would mitigate the issue at the system level. The source snippets for fc_subsystem do not show such a guarantee.

finding.summary: In the HALT_REQ state of the debug unit stall controller, a resume signal (dbg_resume or debug_resume_i) can transition the FSM directly back to RUNNING without waiting for dbg_ack_i, allowing the core to escape a pending debug halt request without ever being acknowledged as halted. Additionally, CSR and GPR write accesses in the SECOND state are granted even when debug_halted_o is not asserted, and the read path for RD_DBGS is always set regardless of halt status.

evidence.description: In the HALT_REQ state, if dbg_resume or debug_resume_i is asserted simultaneously with (or before) dbg_ack_i, stall_ns transitions to RUNNING without the core ever entering the HALT state. This means a debug halt request can be silently abandoned: the core never stalls and never asserts debug_halted_o, bypassing the debug halt protocol.
evidence.supports_claim: Lines 380-381 show that while in HALT_REQ, dbg_resume or debug_resume_i unconditionally overrides the pending halt and moves the FSM to RUNNING, with no guard requiring dbg_ack_i to have been received first.

evidence.description: In the SECOND state of the address decoder (CSR write path), csr_we_o is asserted and debug_gnt_o is granted unconditionally regardless of whether debug_halted_o is true. The comment says 'grant it even when invalid access to not block', meaning a CSR write can proceed even when the core is not halted.
evidence.supports_claim: Lines 155-158 show that in state SECOND, csr_we_o=1 and debug_gnt_o=1 are set without any check on debug_halted_o, unlike the FIRST state which does check debug_halted_o before setting csr_req_n.

evidence.description: The RD_DBGS read selector is set unconditionally for the 6'b10_0000 address range without checking debug_halted_o, even though this range is labeled 'Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug'. The comment says 'grant it even when invalid access to not block', effectively leaking debug-mode register readback to non-halted state.
evidence.supports_claim: Lines 240-244 show rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGS is assigned without a debug_halted_o guard, unlike GPR and CSR accesses which check debug_halted_o.

evidence.description: The dbg_ack_i signal is the only guard that advances from HALT_REQ to HALT, but it can be bypassed by dbg_resume/debug_resume_i in the same state, leaving the halt protocol unenforceable.
evidence.supports_claim: Lines 377-381 show both transitions out of HALT_REQ: one via dbg_ack_i to HALT (correct), and one via dbg_resume/debug_resume_i to RUNNING (bypass).

evidence.description: The debug unit exposes direct CSR read/write, GPR read/write, and jump injection interfaces to an external debug requester. The insufficiency of the halt guard (debug_halted_o check) in parts of the address decoder means these powerful operations may be accessible outside of a proper halt.
evidence.supports_claim: Lines 59-85 enumerate regfile_rreq_o, regfile_wreq_o, csr_req_o, csr_we_o, and jump_req_o as outputs controlled by the debug unit, all of which depend on correct halt-state enforcement.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug unit implements a three-state FSM (RUNNING, HALT_REQ, HALT) to coordinate halting the core for debug access. Two concrete vulnerabilities are present in the source: (1) In HALT_REQ, dbg_resume or debug_resume_i can race with dbg_ack_i and return the FSM to RUNNING, meaning the core never enters HALT and debug_halted_o is never asserted, bypassing the halt security boundary. (2) In the read/write address decoder, several access paths (notably CSR writes in the SECOND state and RD_DBGS reads) omit the debug_halted_o guard that is present in other paths, allowing debug register reads and CSR writes even when the core is not properly halted.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or malicious debug agent could: (a) trigger a halt request then immediately assert resume to prevent the core from halting, defeating debug isolation; (b) perform CSR writes or read debug-mode-only registers while the core is still running, potentially corrupting privilege-level state or leaking sensitive internal state without requiring the core to be in a safe halted state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets do not show the full write-path logic for the SECOND state when debug_we_i is asserted vs. not (only the CSR path is shown). The exact trust model for debug_resume_i (whether it is an authenticated signal) is not shown in the provided snippets. The RD_DBGS readback data content is not fully shown, so the exact sensitivity of what is leaked is uncertain.

finding.summary: The debug unit grants read/write access to certain debug register regions regardless of whether the core is halted, and the debug interface is reachable from the fabric controller subsystem without any privilege or lifecycle guard in the RTL path.

evidence.description: Address region 6'b00_0000 ('Debug Registers, always accessible') unconditionally grants the request and sets rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGA without checking debug_halted_o. Similarly, the default branch also unconditionally grants. Only GPR and CSR accesses are guarded by debug_halted_o; the 'always accessible' debug registers are readable/writable at any time.
evidence.supports_claim: Certain debug register reads (RD_DBGA region) are not gated on the core being halted, allowing an attacker with access to the debug bus to read internal debug state while the core is running.

evidence.description: In the write path for the 6'b10_0000 region, debug_gnt_o is asserted unconditionally ('grant it even when invalid access to not block') before the debug_halted_o check. The grant is issued whether or not the core is halted, meaning the bus transaction is accepted even for unauthorized accesses. Settings writes (DBG_SETS_SSTE, DBG_SETS_ECALL, etc.) are gated, but the grant itself is not, and a malformed or repeated access could still affect state.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit grants transactions to the privileged debug register region before verifying core-halted state, which can be exploited to probe or partially influence debug settings.

evidence.description: The fc_subsystem instantiates zeroriscy_core and directly wires debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, and debug_wdata signals from fc_subsystem-level wires into the core's debug interface ports without any interposing access-control logic.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface input signals arrive at the core without any gating or permission check in the fc_subsystem, making the entire debug bus reachable from whoever drives those signals.

evidence.description: debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, and debug_wdata are declared as plain logic signals in fc_subsystem with no associated permission register, lifecycle state check, or lock bit.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no hardware permission state protecting the debug interface entry point at the subsystem boundary.

evidence.description: The RUNNING state transitions to HALT_REQ whenever dbg_halt, debug_halt_i, or trap_i is asserted, with no authentication or privilege check on who asserts those signals. The debug_halt_i pin is wired directly from the external debug interface.
evidence.supports_claim: Any agent capable of asserting debug_halt_i can unconditionally halt the core and subsequently gain full debug access including register and CSR read/write.

evidence.description: The debug unit module has no lifecycle/permission state input (e.g., no 'debug_enable' or 'lifecycle_state' port). The only guard present is debug_halted_o for certain internal register accesses; there is no global debug-enable gating.
evidence.supports_claim: Absence of a global debug-enable guard means the debug interface is permanently active; it cannot be disabled in production/secure lifecycle states.

evidence.description: For CSR write access (state_q == SECOND), csr_we_o is asserted and debug_gnt_o is granted unconditionally in the else branch, without re-checking debug_halted_o. The halted check only gates csr_req_n in the FIRST cycle; the write enable in SECOND cycle is unconditional.
evidence.supports_claim: A two-cycle CSR write via the debug interface may issue csr_we_o even if the core transitions out of halted state between the two cycles, bypassing the halted guard.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit exposes a debug bus that: (1) is reachable from the fc_subsystem with no interposing access-control; (2) lacks a global debug-enable/lifecycle guard; (3) grants transactions to the 'always accessible' debug register region (RD_DBGA) without requiring the core to be halted; (4) allows any agent to assert debug_halt_i and unconditionally halt the core; and (5) contains a race in the two-cycle CSR write path where csr_we_o is asserted in cycle 2 without rechecking debug_halted_o. Together these flaws mean that any bus master with access to the debug port can read internal debug state at runtime, halt the core, and potentially write CSRs.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug bus (e.g., a compromised DMA or peripheral on the same fabric) can: halt the processor core at will; read internal debug-accessible registers without the core being halted; and perform CSR writes that may bypass the halted-state guard due to the two-cycle race. This can lead to privilege escalation, key/secret readout via CSR/GPR access, and denial-of-service through arbitrary core halting.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact connectivity of debug_halt_i and debug_req at the SoC top level is not fully shown; it is possible an upper-level wrapper adds gating not visible in fc_subsystem.sv. The two-cycle CSR write race depends on timing assumptions that would need simulation confirmation. The 'always accessible' RD_DBGA register contents are not shown, so the sensitivity of leaked data is uncertain.

finding.summary: The debug unit exposes sensitive CPU state (GPRs, CSRs, PC, debug settings, halt status) through the debug interface without any privilege/authentication check. The only conditional guard is whether the core is halted (debug_halted_o), but access to 'always accessible' debug registers (RD_DBGA) and grants for invalid/unmatched addresses are provided unconditionally. The RD_DBGS path also sets rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGS without checking debug_halted_o. Furthermore, dbg_rdata (the internal readback mux) is composed and propagated to debug_rdata_o without any lifecycle or privilege check beyond halted state.

evidence.description: The 'always accessible' Debug Registers (addr[13:8]==6'b00_0000) unconditionally grant access and set rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGA regardless of privilege, authentication, or lifecycle state. The 'only accessible when in debug' block (6'b10_0000) sets rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGS and grants without checking debug_halted_o. The GPR path (6'b00_0100) also grants unconditionally and the default branch issues an unconditional grant for any unrecognized address.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing privilege/authentication guard on debug register read access; RD_DBGS set without halted check; unconditional grants for invalid addresses.

evidence.description: dbg_rdata is assembled from debug_halted_o, sleeping_i, settings_q (debug settings including single-step, exception break enable), dbg_ssth_q, dbg_cause_q, CSR data, GPR data, and PC values. This is sensitive CPU state readable via debug_rdata_o.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive CPU internal state is exposed through the debug readback path.

evidence.description: The debug interface accepts debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i without any authentication or privilege-level check at the module boundary. There is no lock or credential check before entering the address decode logic.
evidence.supports_claim: No authentication or privilege check at the debug interface entry point.

evidence.description: CSR access in the write path checks debug_halted_o before issuing csr_req_n, but the grant (debug_gnt_o=1'b1) is issued regardless ('grant it even when invalid access to not block'). The read path at line 226 checks debug_halted_o for CSR reads, providing a partial guard, but this guard is absent for RD_DBGA and RD_DBGS.
evidence.supports_claim: Partial guard: halted check missing for RD_DBGA and RD_DBGS read paths.

evidence.description: The stall/halt FSM transitions the core to HALT state based on dbg_halt, debug_halt_i, or trap_i without any external authentication. Once halted, the debug unit gains full read/write access to CPU internals.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug halt can be triggered externally without authentication, enabling unauthenticated access to sensitive state.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem connects debug_rdata (output of zeroriscy_core.debug_rdata_o) directly at the subsystem level, propagating unguarded debug readback data to upper-level system logic.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthenticated debug readback of CPU internals is propagated to the SoC-level fabric.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit provides a debug interface that exposes CPU registers (GPRs, CSRs), program counters, halt/sleep status, and debug settings through debug_rdata_o. Access control relies solely on the debug_halted_o signal for some paths, but the 'always accessible' RD_DBGA registers and the RD_DBGS path are granted without any halted check. There is no authentication, privilege level, or lifecycle (e.g., production vs. debug mode) gate at the debug interface boundary. Any agent with access to the debug bus can read sensitive CPU state unconditionally, and halt the core via debug_halt_i to gain further access.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or malicious agent with access to the debug bus can read internal CPU registers (GPRs, CSRs), program counter values, exception cause codes, and debug configuration settings without authentication. It can also halt the processor via debug_halt_i and then perform arbitrary read/write operations to all internal CPU state, enabling full compromise of CPU execution and potentially leaking cryptographic keys or other secrets held in registers.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippets do not show whether the debug bus itself is protected by an upstream access-control or authentication layer in the wider SoC (e.g., an APB/AHB firewall). If such a firewall exists and is correctly configured, the severity would be reduced. However, within the RTL of zeroriscy_debug_unit and fc_subsystem as shown, no such protection is visible.

finding.summary: The debug unit can access and write arbitrary CSRs without any privilege or authentication check. The CSR access mux in zeroriscy_core is controlled solely by the dbg_csr_req signal from the debug unit, with no guard preventing unauthorized debug access to sensitive CSRs.

evidence.description: The CSR access mux assigns csr_access, csr_addr, csr_wdata, and csr_op directly based on dbg_csr_req with no intervening privilege check, lock bit, or authentication. When dbg_csr_req==1, the debug unit can unconditionally read or write any CSR address (dbg_csr_addr) with dbg_csr_wdata, overriding normal pipeline CSR access.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard on debug CSR access path allows the debug unit to read/write any CSR without privilege verification.

evidence.description: In the RESET state of the controller FSM, a dbg_req_i signal unconditionally transitions the FSM to DBG_SIGNAL even before fetch_enable_i is asserted, bypassing normal boot flow. There is no check for any authentication or privilege level before granting debug control.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug request is honored at the earliest possible point (before boot) with no access guard.

evidence.description: DBG_SIGNAL and DBG_SIGNAL_SLEEP states unconditionally set dbg_ack_o=1 and transition to DBG_WAIT, handing CPU control to the debugger. No privilege level or lock register is checked before acknowledging the debug request.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug acknowledgment is issued without any authorization check.

evidence.description: In DBG_WAIT, the debug unit can redirect the program counter to any arbitrary address via dbg_jump_req_i and PC_DBG_NPC with no bounds or privilege check. Combined with unrestricted CSR writes, this gives the debug unit full control over execution context.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug unit can redirect PC to arbitrary addresses without authorization checks.

evidence.description: The controller module exposes dbg_req_i, dbg_stall_i, dbg_jump_req_i, and dbg_settings_i as plain inputs with no documented access control mechanism, and these signals directly drive security-sensitive FSM transitions and outputs.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug interface signals are consumed directly with no access control layer at the module boundary.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy core implements a debug interface where dbg_csr_req unconditionally overrides normal CSR access (SNIP-0019), dbg_req_i causes immediate FSM transitions to debug states from any FSM state including RESET (SNIP-0012), and the debug unit is granted full CPU control (halt, PC redirect, CSR r/w) upon any dbg_req assertion without any privilege check, authentication mechanism, lock register, or lifecycle state guard. This constitutes a missing guard vulnerability on the debug access path.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert dbg_req_i (e.g., via a compromised debug peripheral, JTAG interface, or software-accessible debug register) can halt the CPU at any time, read or write any CSR including machine-mode security-critical registers (mstatus, mtvec, PMP registers), and redirect the program counter to arbitrary addresses, achieving full privilege escalation and arbitrary code execution.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The chain does not include the zeroriscy_debug_unit source where dbg_req_o is generated, so it is not possible to confirm whether the debug unit itself performs any authentication before asserting dbg_req_o. However, the core-side logic shown in the snippets contains no guard regardless of what the debug unit does internally.

finding.summary: The debug unit's sensitive register access path (DBG_SETS_SSTE single-step, DBG_IE exception break-in, and register/CSR write ports) is gated only by debug_halted_o for the 'Debug Registers' block, but the debug settings registers (DBG_SETS_SSTE, DBG_SETS_ECALL, DBG_SETS_ELSU, DBG_SETS_EBRK, DBG_SETS_EILL) at address block 6'b01_xxxx are written without verifying debug_halted_o, allowing an external debug master to manipulate core trap/single-step behavior while the core is still running.

evidence.description: The DBG_CTRL register block (address 5'b0_0000) writes settings_n[DBG_SETS_SSTE] directly from debug_wdata_i[0] without checking debug_halted_o. Similarly DBG_IE (5'b0_0010) writes exception trap-into-debug bits (DBG_SETS_ECALL, DBG_SETS_ELSU, DBG_SETS_EBRK, DBG_SETS_EILL) without any privilege or halt-state guard. Only the 'Debug Registers' block at 6'b10_0000 checks `if (debug_halted_o)` before allowing access.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug control settings that govern when the core traps into the debugger (single-step enable, exception intercept bits) can be written by the external debug interface while the core is actively running, with no authentication or halt-state check.

evidence.description: The halt/stall FSM in RUNNING state immediately asserts dbg_req_o and transitions to HALT_REQ whenever dbg_halt, debug_halt_i, or trap_i is asserted. The settings_q[DBG_SETS_SSTE] bit (single-step) is evaluated here to set dbg_ssth_n. Because settings_n[DBG_SETS_SSTE] can be written without a halt-state guard (see SNIP-0018), an attacker controlling the debug bus can enable single-step on a running core.
evidence.supports_claim: The missing guard on settings write propagates into the halt FSM, enabling unauthorized single-step or trap interception.

evidence.description: The debug unit exposes a full memory-mapped interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i) that directly controls core halt, register read/write, CSR read/write, and jump injection. The module accepts these inputs with no privilege level or life-cycle lock check visible in the RTL.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no hardware-enforced access control on the debug interface; any bus master capable of asserting debug_req_i can reach the settings registers.

evidence.description: In fc_subsystem, core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0 when connecting to apb_interrupt_cntrl, meaning secure mode is never asserted. This confirms no runtime privilege state is propagated into the debug path.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure mode is permanently disabled at the subsystem level, so no privilege escalation check can block debug register writes.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0018 shows that the DBG_CTRL and DBG_IE address cases write directly to settings_n bits (single-step enable and exception intercept enables) without any debug_halted_o guard. Only the higher address block (6'b10_0000, 'Debug Registers') has an explicit `if (debug_halted_o)` check. SNIP-0019 shows the running-state FSM uses settings_q[DBG_SETS_SSTE] to set dbg_ssth_n and route trap handling. SNIP-0017 confirms no privilege check exists at the module boundary. SNIP-0007 shows secure_mode is hardwired off. Together these confirm that any debug-bus master can write single-step and exception-interception bits into a live, running core without halting it first.
finding.security_impact: An untrusted debug master (or software with debug-bus access) can enable single-step mode or force the core to trap into the debugger on ecall/ebreak/illegal-instruction/LSU-fault events while the core is still running. This allows stealthy control-flow hijacking, denial-of-service through forced stalls, and exfiltration of architectural state (registers, CSRs) via the debug readback path, all without requiring the core to be in a halted state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full address decoder case statement body is not entirely captured in the snippets, so it is possible there are additional guards higher in the always_comb block. However, SNIP-0018 lines 181-191 are clearly inside the write path for the settings register block and contain no debug_halted_o conditional.

finding.summary: The debug unit grants access to sensitive debug register addresses unconditionally (even when the core is not halted), issuing a grant signal without enforcing the halted-core guard for certain register regions.

evidence.description: At line 241 and 247, the debug unit issues `debug_gnt_o = 1'b1` for the 'Debug Registers only accessible when in debug' (6'b10_0000) and 'General-Purpose Registers' (6'b00_0100) address regions unconditionally, regardless of whether `debug_halted_o` is asserted. The comment explicitly states 'grant it even when invalid access to not block'. While the actual register file read (`regfile_rreq_n`) is gated by `debug_halted_o` at line 249, the grant is issued even for invalid (non-halted) accesses. This means a debug read request to GPR space when the core is running will receive a grant and proceed to the rvalid phase, potentially returning stale or undefined register data without a proper halt guard.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_gnt_o is asserted unconditionally for protected register address ranges even when the core is not halted, bypassing the intended halted-core access guard.

evidence.description: At line 197, for the 'Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug' write path (6'b10_0000), `debug_gnt_o = 1'b1` is granted unconditionally before checking `debug_halted_o` at line 199. The grant is issued even when the access is invalid (core not halted), as noted by the comment. This is an inconsistency between the stated access policy and the actual grant behavior.
evidence.supports_claim: Grant is issued before, and independently of, the halted-core permission check, meaning the bus transaction proceeds even when the protection condition is not met.

evidence.description: CSR access via the debug interface (debug_addr_i[14] set) checks `debug_halted_o` at line 151 before asserting `csr_req_n`, which is correct. However, at line 156, `debug_gnt_o = 1'b1` is unconditionally granted in the SECOND state regardless of the halted status, even though the csr_we_o write enable is also unconditionally set at line 158. This means a CSR write through the debug interface can complete (grant + write-enable issued) even if the core was not properly halted when the request was first evaluated.
evidence.supports_claim: In the SECOND state of CSR debug access, both grant and write-enable are unconditional, bypassing the halted-core guard that was only checked in the FIRST state.

evidence.description: The debug unit module exposes a full register file read/write port, CSR read/write port, and jump control to an external debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i). These are externally driven inputs with no authentication or privilege check beyond the debug_halted_o state, which is itself bypassable due to unconditional grant issuance.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface provides direct access to internal CPU state (GPRs, CSRs) with only a soft halted-state guard that is partially bypassed by unconditional grant behavior.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_core exposes the full debug bus interface as top-level ports (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_gnt_o, debug_rvalid_o, debug_rdata_o). These are connected from fc_subsystem without any intervening permission or authentication filter.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug access entry point is exposed at the top-level core interface and passed through without additional access control.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem instantiates zeroriscy_core and passes debug signals (debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata) directly from subsystem ports to the core debug interface with no intervening permission check or authentication.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug bus signals are routed without access control from the subsystem boundary to the core debug unit.

evidence.description: The HALT state machine controls debug_halted_o and stall_o, which are the only protection gate for debug register access. If grants proceed before the HALT state is reached or while transitioning, the protection is ineffective.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_halted_o signal is the sole access guard for protected registers, but unconditional grant issuance undermines this protection.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit unconditionally issues debug_gnt_o for several protected address regions (GPRs at 6'b00_0100, debug-only registers at 6'b10_0000, and CSR writes in SECOND state) regardless of whether debug_halted_o is asserted. The design intent is that access to internal CPU state is only allowed when the core is halted, but the grant is issued with the comment 'grant it even when invalid access to not block'. For reads, the actual regfile_rreq_n and rdata_sel_n are gated by debug_halted_o, but the bus transaction still completes (grant issued, rvalid will follow), potentially returning undefined or stale data. For CSR writes via the debug path, csr_we_o is unconditionally asserted in the SECOND state regardless of debug_halted_o, meaning a CSR write can complete even if the core was not halted. This represents a bypass of the intended halted-core permission check for debug access.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or untrusted debug master with access to the debug bus can: (1) issue reads to GPR address space and receive a grant+rvalid response even when the core is running, potentially obtaining register values or causing metastability; (2) issue CSR writes through the debug interface that complete (csr_we_o asserted) even without a proper halt, allowing modification of privileged CPU state such as machine-mode control registers without the intended halt synchronization. This undermines the security boundary between debug and normal execution contexts.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippets do not include the full rdata mux logic or the complete sequential update of rdata_sel_q, so it is uncertain whether stale/undefined data is actually returned for non-halted GPR reads, or if the rdata path is safely zeroed. The CSR write bypass (csr_we_o unconditional in SECOND state) is more clearly evidenced. The actual impact depends on whether the debug bus is accessible to untrusted agents in the system integration, which is not shown in the provided context.

finding.summary: The debug unit grants access and issues debug_gnt_o for several register regions without verifying that the core is halted, allowing debug register reads and some debug register writes to proceed even when the CPU is running.

evidence.description: In the read path (SECOND state / non-write path), the 6'b00_0000 debug register region is explicitly labelled 'always accessible' and grants access unconditionally (debug_gnt_o = 1'b1, rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGA) with no check on debug_halted_o. The 6'b10_0000 and 6'b00_0100 (GPR) regions also unconditionally set debug_gnt_o before the debug_halted_o guard, meaning the bus transaction is acknowledged regardless of halt state. The default branch (line 255) also unconditionally grants.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug register reads are granted and return data (RD_DBGA, RD_DBGS, RD_GPR selectors are set) even when the processor is not halted, bypassing the intended 'only accessible when in debug' protection.

evidence.description: In the 6'b10_0000 write path (Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug), debug_gnt_o is unconditionally asserted (line 197) before checking debug_halted_o. The comment acknowledges this: 'grant it even when invalid access to not block'. Settings writes such as DBG_SETS_SSTE, DBG_SETS_ECALL, DBG_SETS_ELSU, DBG_SETS_EBRK, DBG_SETS_EILL and the dbg_resume signal are gated on debug_halted_o, but the grant itself is not, meaning an attacker can probe the bus without stalling.
evidence.supports_claim: Unconditional grant on debug-only register writes means bus-level side effects (transaction completion, potential data leakage through rvalid timing) occur regardless of CPU halt state.

evidence.description: In the CSR write path (state_q == SECOND), debug_gnt_o is unconditionally asserted (line 156) and csr_we_o is set regardless of debug_halted_o. Only the csr_req_n in the FIRST cycle checks debug_halted_o (line 151-154), but the grant and write-enable in the SECOND cycle do not re-verify halt state.
evidence.supports_claim: The halt-state guard is applied only in the first cycle of a two-cycle CSR access; in the second cycle the grant and write-enable are issued unconditionally, potentially completing a CSR write if halt state changed between the two cycles.

evidence.description: The debug unit exposes a full register file read/write port, CSR read/write port, jump request, halt and resume controls directly through the external debug_req_i/debug_addr_i/debug_we_i/debug_wdata_i interface with no authentication or privilege check at the module boundary.
evidence.supports_claim: Any agent that can assert debug_req_i can reach register file, CSR, and execution-control resources with only soft guards (debug_halted_o checks) that are inconsistently applied.

evidence.description: In fc_subsystem the debug signals (debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata) are plain logic wires with no access-control wrapper. They are wired directly to the zeroriscy_core debug interface, meaning any subsystem-level initiator that drives these wires reaches the debug unit without additional filtering.
evidence.supports_claim: No hardware-enforced privilege or lifecycle gate exists between the subsystem debug wires and the CPU debug unit.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit issues debug_gnt_o (the seed signal) unconditionally for several address regions before evaluating debug_halted_o. The 'always accessible' debug register region (6'b00_0000) and the default branch grant unconditionally on every request. The 'only accessible when in debug' regions grant unconditionally and only withhold internal side-effects (register reads, CSR requests). The CSR write path grants and sets csr_we_o in the second cycle without re-checking halt state. This means the debug bus is acknowledged and data transactions are completed even against a running CPU, and the debug subsystem is reachable from fc_subsystem with no authentication layer.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or malicious software with access to the debug bus can read general-purpose registers, CSRs, and debug-state registers, and can issue halt/resume commands, without requiring the CPU to be in a halted debug state for the grant itself. This enables information disclosure of register contents and potential control-flow hijack via jump_req or CSR writes during normal execution.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full address-decode logic for writes in the 6'b00_0000 'always accessible' region is not shown; it is possible some sub-addresses have additional guards not visible in the snippets. The upstream initiator that drives fc_subsystem debug signals is not shown, so the practical exploitability depends on who can assert those signals.

finding.summary: The debug unit grants access to sensitive debug registers (RD_DBGS) and issues a debug grant (debug_gnt_o) even when the core is NOT halted, bypassing the halt-state access protection for certain register ranges.

evidence.description: In the read path for address range 6'b10_0000 (Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug), the code sets debug_gnt_o=1'b1 and rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGS unconditionally, without checking debug_halted_o. The comment says 'only accessible when in debug' but no guard is applied for reads, unlike the GPR access which correctly checks debug_halted_o before enabling regfile_rreq_n.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive debug state registers can be read even when the core is not halted, bypassing the intended halted-only access restriction.

evidence.description: In the write path for address range 6'b10_0000 (Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug), the code correctly wraps write operations inside 'if (debug_halted_o)' at line 199. However, the grant (debug_gnt_o=1'b1 at line 197) is issued unconditionally before the halt check.
evidence.supports_claim: Write-side guard checks debug_halted_o but grant is unconditional; read-side (SNIP-0012) entirely omits the halt check for RD_DBGS selection.

evidence.description: debug_halted_o is only asserted in the HALT state of the stall FSM. The HALT state is only entered after dbg_ack_i is received in HALT_REQ. This means access checks conditioned on debug_halted_o only protect resources when the core is fully halted.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_halted_o signal correctly represents the halted permission state, making the missing check in SNIP-0012 a concrete bypass.

evidence.description: The debug unit exposes a full memory-mapped debug interface including CSR access, GPR access, and debug-register access. The interface is accessible from fc_subsystem through debug_req/debug_addr/debug_we/debug_wdata signals without any privilege or lifecycle gating at the subsystem level.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface is fully externally accessible with no authentication or lifecycle lock, making the missing halt guard in the register read path directly exploitable.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem declares debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, debug_wdata as plain logic signals wired directly to the core, with no access control, privilege check, or lifecycle gating applied before forwarding to zeroriscy_core.
evidence.supports_claim: Any agent that can drive these signals can issue debug accesses without restriction.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit implements a register access policy that is supposed to restrict certain debug registers (address range 6'b10_0000) to when the core is halted. In the write path this check exists (SNIP-0011, line 199 checks debug_halted_o). However, in the read path (SNIP-0012, lines 240-244), the same address range unconditionally sets debug_gnt_o=1 and rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGS without checking debug_halted_o. This allows a debug master to read sensitive debug-state registers (cause, single-step state, etc.) even when the core is running. Additionally, debug_gnt_o is issued even for 'invalid' accesses to not block the bus (lines 197, 223, 241, 247, 255), which means the grant side never enforces access control — only the data mux side does, and it does so inconsistently.
finding.security_impact: An unauthorized or unprivileged debug master can read internal debug state registers (DBG_DBGS range) without halting the core, leaking debug cause, single-step settings, and other security-sensitive processor state. Additionally, the unconditional grant for invalid accesses could be abused to probe the debug address space without triggering bus stalls that might be monitored.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact contents of the RD_DBGS read-data mux output are not shown in the provided snippets, so the precise sensitivity of leaked data cannot be fully assessed. It is also unclear whether there is an upper-level access control mechanism outside fc_subsystem that restricts who can drive the debug_req/addr/we signals.

finding.summary: The debug unit (zeroriscy_debug_unit) allows read access to Debug Registers in the 6'b10_0000 address range (RD_DBGS) without checking that the core is halted (debug_halted_o), exposing sensitive debug state to any requester with debug_req_i asserted.

evidence.description: For the 6'b10_0000 address range (Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug), the write path correctly gates the actual operation behind 'if (debug_halted_o)' (line 199). However, the read path at lines 240-244 sets rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGS unconditionally without any debug_halted_o check. The grant is issued (line 241) and rdata_sel_n is set to RD_DBGS (line 243) regardless of whether the core is halted.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing halt guard on read path for the 6'b10_0000 debug register range allows sensitive register readback without the core being halted.

evidence.description: Lines 240-244: 'begin // Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug / debug_gnt_o = 1'b1; // grant it even when invalid access to not block / rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGS; end' — the comment says 'only accessible when in debug' but there is no 'if (debug_halted_o)' guard wrapping the rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGS assignment, unlike the write path at line 199 which does check debug_halted_o before proceeding.
evidence.supports_claim: Concrete missing guard: RD_DBGS read selection is set without verifying debug_halted_o, allowing readback of debug-only registers while the core is still running.

evidence.description: The RD_DBGS data mux path (dbg_rdata populated from rdata_sel_q == RD_DBGS) feeds debug_rdata_o, which is propagated through zeroriscy_core.debug_rdata_o to fc_subsystem.debug_rdata. This data path carries sensitive architectural state readable via the debug interface.
evidence.supports_claim: The output debug_rdata_o carries sensitive CPU state that should only be accessible when the core is halted.

evidence.description: Lines 196-205 show the write path for 6'b10_0000 correctly guards operations: 'if (debug_halted_o) begin unique case (debug_addr_i[6:2]) 5'b0_0000: jump_req_n = 1'b1; // DNPC'. The asymmetry between guarded writes and unguarded reads in the same address range confirms the missing read guard is a design flaw.
evidence.supports_claim: Write path is correctly guarded by debug_halted_o while the read path is not, confirming the guard was intentionally designed but accidentally omitted for reads.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem instantiates zeroriscy_core and connects debug_rdata (line 89, SNIP-0001) through the debug interface, making the unguarded debug readback accessible from the system-level debug bus.
evidence.supports_claim: The vulnerability is reachable from the system-level debug bus connected in fc_subsystem.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit implements an address-based access control scheme where certain debug registers (6'b10_0000 range) are documented as 'only accessible when in debug' (i.e., when debug_halted_o is asserted). The write path for this range correctly checks debug_halted_o before performing operations. However, the read path (lines 240-244 of SNIP-0012) unconditionally grants the request and sets rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGS without checking debug_halted_o. This means any entity with access to debug_req_i can read from the debug-only register range while the core is still running, bypassing the intended halted-only access restriction. The data flows through debug_rdata_o all the way to the fc_subsystem debug_rdata output.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or untrusted debug initiator can read sensitive debug-only registers (RD_DBGS range) from zeroriscy_debug_unit without the CPU being halted. This may expose internal architectural state, control flow information, or other sensitive processor state that is meant to be protected until the core is placed in a controlled halted debug state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact content of the RD_DBGS register mux output (what data is exposed) is not fully shown in the available snippet of SNIP-0013, which only shows RD_DBGA handling. The RD_DBGS case content would need to be confirmed to assess the full severity of the exposed data, but the missing guard is clearly evidenced in the source.

finding.summary: The debug unit grants access and returns read-valid (debug_rvalid_o) for certain register regions without verifying that the core is halted, allowing unauthorized debug read access to sensitive register data when the core is running.

evidence.description: In the read path of zeroriscy_debug_unit, the 'Debug Registers always accessible' region (6'b00_0000) unconditionally grants debug_gnt_o and sets rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGA without any check on debug_halted_o. Similarly, the 6'b10_0000 region grants access (debug_gnt_o = 1'b1) and sets rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGS regardless of halt state. The default case also unconditionally grants. Only GPR and CSR reads are guarded by debug_halted_o.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug register regions are readable without requiring the core to be halted, bypassing the halt guard that protects GPR and CSR accesses.

evidence.description: CSR write path (state_q == SECOND) unconditionally sets csr_we_o = 1'b1 and grants (debug_gnt_o = 1'b1) without checking debug_halted_o, even though CSR read in the FIRST state is guarded by debug_halted_o. The grant and write-enable are issued even when the core is not halted.
evidence.supports_claim: The two-cycle CSR write sequence grants and enables write in the SECOND state without re-checking the halted condition, allowing a write to proceed if the core exits halt between cycles or if halt was never entered.

evidence.description: Line 255: 'default: debug_gnt_o = 1'b1' — the default case in the read address decoder unconditionally grants any unmatched debug address without authentication or halt check.
evidence.supports_claim: Any debug address not matched by a specific case is silently granted, potentially allowing spurious read completions.

evidence.description: The debug unit exposes register file read/write, CSR read/write, and internal debug register state (dbg_rdata, settings_q) through the debug interface. These are sensitive CPU internals.
evidence.supports_claim: The resources exposed via debug_rvalid_o and debug_rdata_o include CPU architectural state that should only be readable when the core is halted.

evidence.description: The HALT state machine shows that debug_halted_o is only asserted after the full RUNNING -> HALT_REQ -> HALT transition with dbg_ack_i. However, debug grants and some read responses are issued in the address-decode combinational block before this state is reached.
evidence.supports_claim: Grants and rdata_sel can be set before debug_halted_o is asserted, leading to potential data leakage during the halt handshake window.

evidence.description: The zeroriscy_core exposes debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i as external inputs and debug_rvalid_o, debug_rdata_o as outputs with no additional permission gate at the core boundary before passing to the debug unit.
evidence.supports_claim: Any entity that can drive debug_req_i can initiate debug accesses; there is no privilege or authentication check at the core's debug port.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit implements a partially-guarded debug interface. While GPR and CSR read accesses check debug_halted_o before proceeding, the 'always accessible' debug register region (RD_DBGA) and the default grant path bypass this check entirely. Furthermore, the CSR write path does not re-verify the halted condition in the SECOND state. This means an attacker who can drive the debug port can read debug registers and receive valid grants (debug_gnt_o asserted, debug_rvalid_o eventually asserted) without the core being in a halted state. The debug_rvalid_o signal, which is the seed of this chain, is driven by the debug unit and propagates through fc_subsystem to the system debug bus without any additional permission filtering.
finding.security_impact: An unauthorized or malicious debug initiator can read internal debug register state (RD_DBGA region) and potentially trigger CSR writes without the core being halted, leading to information disclosure of CPU internal state and possible integrity violations of control registers. If the debug port is reachable from an unprivileged bus master or via a compromised peripheral, this constitutes a privilege escalation path into CPU architectural state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact contents of the RD_DBGA register region are not fully shown in the snippets, so the precise sensitivity of always-accessible registers is uncertain. It is also unclear whether the debug port in this SoC is protected by a higher-level bus firewall or authentication mechanism outside the scope of these files. The two-cycle race condition in CSR writes is a design observation but may be benign if the halted check in FIRST is sufficient in practice.

finding.summary: The debug interface (debug_we_i / debug_wdata_i) allows write access to certain debug control registers and settings without requiring the core to be halted, and grants access unconditionally in some paths even when the write is invalid.

evidence.description: In the write path for debug address range 6'b10_0000 (Debug Registers 'only accessible when in debug'), debug_gnt_o is asserted unconditionally (line 197) before the debug_halted_o check on line 199. The grant is given even when the core is NOT halted, meaning a write transaction is acknowledged even if the actual register write is silently dropped. This inconsistency could be exploited to probe timing or interfere with debug state.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_gnt_o is asserted unconditionally with a comment 'grant it even when invalid access to not block', bypassing the halted check for the grant signal while still conditionally performing the write.

evidence.description: The debug_we_i input enters zeroriscy_debug_unit directly from external debug interface ports with no authentication or privilege check at the module boundary. The port is exposed through fc_subsystem.debug_we all the way to zeroriscy_debug_unit.debug_we_i with no interposing guard.
evidence.supports_claim: No permission or authentication gate exists on the debug write enable signal path from fc_subsystem to the debug unit.

evidence.description: In the CSR write path (debug_addr_i[14] set, state_q == SECOND), csr_we_o is asserted unconditionally (line 158) regardless of debug_halted_o. The debug_halted_o guard is only applied in the FIRST cycle (line 151) to gate csr_req_n, but in the SECOND cycle csr_we_o=1 is set without re-checking if the core is still halted.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR write enable (csr_we_o) is issued in the SECOND state without verifying debug_halted_o, potentially allowing CSR writes when core is not properly halted.

evidence.description: In the read path for range 6'b00_0000 (Debug Registers 'always accessible'), debug_gnt_o and rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGA are set unconditionally without any debug_halted_o check, exposing these registers regardless of core state.
evidence.supports_claim: Certain debug register addresses are accessible (granted and read data selected) regardless of whether the core is halted, providing unrestricted read access.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem instantiates zeroriscy_core and passes debug_req, debug_addr, debug_we, and debug_wdata directly as port connections with no interposing permission check or lock register.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug write channel propagates from subsystem boundary to core without any access control layer.

evidence.description: The CSR mux in zeroriscy_core shows that when dbg_csr_req is asserted, csr_op is forced to CSR_OP_WRITE unconditionally based only on dbg_csr_we, with no privilege-level check. Any entity that can drive the debug interface can issue CSR writes.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug-driven CSR writes bypass normal instruction-level privilege checks, relying solely on the unguarded debug interface.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug write interface (debug_we_i) propagates from fc_subsystem through zeroriscy_core to zeroriscy_debug_unit without any authentication or privilege check. Within the debug unit, the halted-state guard (debug_halted_o) is inconsistently applied: some register ranges grant access unconditionally, CSR writes in the SECOND state do not re-verify halted status, and the grant signal is issued even for 'invalid' accesses. The CSR mux in zeroriscy_core further shows that debug-sourced CSR writes skip normal pipeline privilege checks. Together these create a path where an external entity with access to the debug interface can read certain debug registers at any time, and potentially write CSRs or debug settings with only partial halted-state enforcement.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical or logical access to the debug interface can read always-accessible debug registers regardless of processor state, issue CSR writes that bypass normal privilege checks, and potentially alter debug settings (single-step enable, exception breakpoint enables) or core state without proper authorization. This could lead to privilege escalation, information disclosure of CSR contents, or disruption of secure execution.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippets do not show the complete address decode logic for all write paths (lines between SNIP-0009 and SNIP-0010 are partial), so there may be additional guards not visible. The top-level connection of the debug interface to a trusted debug host vs. an untrusted bus is not shown. Whether the 'always accessible' registers contain sensitive data is not confirmed by the snippets alone.

finding.summary: The debug unit exposes register file and CSR read data through the debug interface without sufficient privilege or lifecycle guards. Specifically, the RD_DBGS (debug-halted-only registers) selector is set unconditionally upon a read request to the 6'b10_0000 address range without checking debug_halted_o, meaning those registers can be read even when the core is not halted. Additionally, the grant signal is issued even for invalid or unauthorized accesses (commented 'grant it even when invalid access to not block'), and register file read data flows to debug_rdata_o without authentication beyond debug_req_i being asserted.

evidence.description: At lines 240-244, when debug_addr_i[13:8] == 6'b10_0000 (Debug Registers 'only accessible when in debug'), debug_gnt_o is unconditionally granted and rdata_sel_n is set to RD_DBGS without checking debug_halted_o. The comment says 'only accessible when in debug' but no halted guard is applied to the read path, unlike the write path at lines 196-205 which does check debug_halted_o.
evidence.supports_claim: Read access to debug-halted-only registers (RD_DBGS) is not guarded by debug_halted_o, allowing readback even when core is not halted.

evidence.description: Write path for 6'b10_0000 at lines 196-205 correctly checks 'if (debug_halted_o)' before performing the write (jump_req_n). The corresponding read path at lines 240-244 lacks this check, creating an asymmetric protection.
evidence.supports_claim: Write path has a debug_halted_o guard but the read path for the same address range does not, demonstrating the missing guard on the read side.

evidence.description: The entire address decode block is entered whenever debug_req_i is asserted (line 139: 'if (debug_req_i)'). No additional authentication or privilege level check exists beyond this signal. The debug interface is accessible to any entity that can assert debug_req_i.
evidence.supports_claim: Access to sensitive CPU internal state (GPRs, CSRs, debug registers) is gated only by debug_req_i with no privilege or lifecycle check.

evidence.description: Line 435: 'assign dbg_reg_rdata_o = regfile_data_rb_id' directly exposes the register file read port output to the debug interface without any masking or access control beyond the regfile_rreq_n signal set in the debug unit.
evidence.supports_claim: General-purpose register file contents are read out to the debug interface unconditionally once regfile_rreq is asserted.

evidence.description: Multiple grant statements include comments 'grant it even when invalid access to not block' (lines 197, 208, 215, 223, 241, 247, 255), indicating the design intentionally issues grants for unauthorized or invalid accesses, which also triggers rvalid and thus data readback on the debug bus.
evidence.supports_claim: Grants are issued for invalid or unauthorized debug accesses, potentially causing rvalid to fire and stale or unintended data to be returned on debug_rdata_o.

evidence.description: debug_rdata (line 89 in fc_subsystem.sv) is a 32-bit wire connected to zeroriscy_core debug_rdata_o, which aggregates GPR, CSR, and internal debug register data. This signal is accessible at the subsystem level and can be read by any debug master that can assert debug_req.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive CPU state (registers, CSRs, PC values) exposed on debug_rdata at the subsystem boundary without sufficient access control.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit implements a debug register interface that allows reading CPU internal state (GPRs, CSRs, debug control registers). While write accesses to certain address ranges correctly gate behind debug_halted_o, the corresponding read path for the 6'b10_0000 address range (labeled 'Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug') does not check debug_halted_o before setting rdata_sel_n = RD_DBGS and issuing a grant. This asymmetry allows a debug requester to read these registers even when the core is running. Additionally, the design explicitly grants debug bus transactions for 'invalid' accesses to avoid blocking, which could cause unintended data readback. The overall access control relies solely on debug_req_i with no privilege gating, meaning any entity controlling the debug interface can read sensitive CPU state.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or unprivileged entity with access to the debug interface can read CPU internal registers (GPRs, debug control/status registers) without the core being halted, bypassing the intended 'only accessible when in debug' protection. This enables extraction of sensitive data (cryptographic keys, program state, privilege escalation artifacts) from a running system via the debug port.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact security policy for debug_req_i access (whether it is expected to be externally protected at the SoC integration level) is not shown. The fc_subsystem instantiation connects debug signals but the upstream arbiter or authentication mechanism for the debug bus is not in scope. If the debug port is expected to be protected at a higher integration level, the severity would be lower. However, based on the RTL evidence, the missing debug_halted_o guard on the read path is a concrete coding error relative to the stated access policy.

finding.summary: The debug unit grants register file read requests (regfile_rreq_o) and issues debug_gnt_o for GPR and some debug register accesses without consistently enforcing the debug_halted_o guard, and critically always grants (debug_gnt_o=1) for several address regions regardless of whether the core is halted, potentially allowing data exfiltration or state corruption.

evidence.description: At line 241 and 247, debug_gnt_o is unconditionally set to 1'b1 for 'Debug Registers only accessible when in debug' (6'b10_0000) and for GPR access (6'b00_0100) respectively, before the debug_halted_o guard is checked. The grant is issued even when the core is NOT halted. For GPR reads specifically (lines 249-252), regfile_rreq_n is only asserted when debug_halted_o is true, but the grant (debug_gnt_o=1) and rdata_sel (RD_DBGS) are issued unconditionally, meaning the debug interface receives a grant response regardless of halt state. The comment 'grant it even when invalid access to not block' confirms this is intentional bypass.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_gnt_o is issued unconditionally for sensitive register regions, bypassing the debug_halted_o protection requirement and allowing the debug bus to proceed even on unauthorized accesses

evidence.description: At line 197, debug_gnt_o is set to 1'b1 unconditionally for the 'Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug' address region (6'b10_0000) on write path, with the guard (debug_halted_o) only controlling the actual register write logic inside. The grant leaks timing information and allows the debug protocol to complete even without halt authorization.
evidence.supports_claim: Write grants are issued for protected debug-only registers regardless of halt state

evidence.description: The debug_halted_o check at line 249 is the intended guard for GPR read requests (regfile_rreq_n), but the grant (debug_gnt_o=1 at line 247) and selector (RD_DBGS at line 243) are set before this guard is evaluated, meaning the bus handshake completes regardless.
evidence.supports_claim: Guard is present for the actual resource access but absent for the bus grant, creating an inconsistent protection boundary

evidence.description: The debug unit module (zeroriscy_debug_unit) exposes regfile_rreq_o, regfile_raddr_o, regfile_rdata_i, csr_req_o, csr_addr_o, and csr_wdata_o as an unrestricted debug interface accepting commands via debug_req_i/debug_addr_i/debug_we_i/debug_wdata_i with no authentication or privilege level check beyond the debug_halted_o state.
evidence.supports_claim: No authentication or privilege check exists on the debug interface beyond core-halt state

evidence.description: The HALT state (lines 384-391) sets debug_halted_o=1'b1 and stall_o=1'b1, which is the protection gate. However, in HALT_REQ state (lines 374-381), if dbg_resume or debug_resume_i is asserted, the core transitions back to RUNNING without completing the halt, and any pending debug grants already issued could proceed against a running core.
evidence.supports_claim: Race condition between halt-request state and resume signal could allow debug grants to be processed against a non-halted core

evidence.description: In fc_subsystem, core_secure_mode_i is hardwired to 1'b0 at line 283 when instantiating apb_interrupt_cntrl, meaning the system always operates in non-secure mode, which may reduce the bar for debug access exploitation.
evidence.supports_claim: Secure mode is permanently disabled, removing an additional layer of protection that could restrict debug access

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_debug_unit unconditionally issues debug_gnt_o for multiple sensitive address regions (GPR, debug-only registers) before evaluating the debug_halted_o guard. While the actual register read/write operations (regfile_rreq_n, csr_req_n) are conditionally guarded by debug_halted_o, the bus grant is not, meaning a debug requester receives protocol-level acknowledgment even for unauthorized accesses. Additionally, the system's secure mode is hardwired off. Together these constitute a missing guard on the bus grant path for protected resources.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to the debug interface can issue requests to GPR and CSR-equivalent debug registers and receive bus grants regardless of whether the core is halted. While the actual register read data may not be returned (regfile_rreq_n gated by halt), the unconditional grant breaks the intended access control model, can be exploited for timing side-channels, and in certain address regions (RD_DBGS selector is set unconditionally) may expose debug state data through the rdata path.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual rdata mux logic (selecting what data is returned based on rdata_sel_q) is not included in the snippets, so it is uncertain whether RD_DBGS or RD_DBGA selections actually return sensitive data when debug_halted_o is false. The full impact depends on what data is multiplexed onto debug_rdata_o for each selector value. The race condition in HALT_REQ->RUNNING transition also requires further timing analysis.

finding.summary: The debug unit (zeroriscy_debug_unit) grants access to certain debug registers unconditionally, without requiring the core to be halted, allowing register file write requests (regfile_wreq_o) to potentially be issued without adequate privilege gating. Additionally, the debug interface exposes full GPR and CSR read/write capability through an external debug port with minimal access control.

evidence.description: The 'Debug Registers, always accessible' block at address 6'b00_0000 grants debug_gnt_o and sets rdata_sel_n=RD_DBGA unconditionally, without checking debug_halted_o. Other register regions (CSR access, GPR access) do check debug_halted_o, but this region is always open.
evidence.supports_claim: Certain debug register regions are accessible regardless of whether the core is halted, bypassing the halt-state guard.
