evidence.description: The 'Debug Registers, only accessible when in debug' block (6'b10_0000) grants debug_gnt_o unconditionally ('grant it even when invalid access to not block') and only then conditionally gates the actual register file write request on debug_halted_o. Granting without halting could cause protocol confusion.
evidence.supports_claim: Grant is issued even when core is not halted for the debug-register region, with only the data operation gated on debug_halted_o.

evidence.description: The always_comb block in zeroriscy_debug_unit initializes regfile_wreq=1'b0 and other signals, then the subsequent case logic drives regfile_wreq based on debug address and write conditions. The combinational nature means any incoming debug_req without authentication can drive the write path.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit processes all incoming debug requests combinationally, with no authentication or privilege check at the entry point.

evidence.description: The CSR mux in zeroriscy_core directly selects between normal CSR access and debug-unit-driven CSR access (dbg_csr_req) with full write capability (CSR_OP_WRITE). The debug unit can write any CSR when dbg_csr_req and dbg_csr_we are asserted, bypassing normal instruction-flow privilege checks.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug interface has direct write access to all CSRs without additional privilege-level filtering beyond the debug_halted_o check in the debug unit.

evidence.description: The zeroriscy_core exposes a full debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_addr_i) as top-level inputs. These flow directly into the debug unit without any authentication or lifecycle-based lockout visible in the RTL.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug port is permanently enabled with no hardware lifecycle or authentication gate on the core boundary.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem directly connects debug_req and debug signals to the zeroriscy_core debug interface without any intervening access control or privilege check.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug requests reach the CPU core directly from the subsystem boundary with no intermediate permission check.

evidence.description: The supervisor_mode_o signal is hardwired to 1'b1 and core_secure_mode_i is driven as 1'b0 to the interrupt controller, indicating the security mode signal is not used to gate debug access.
evidence.supports_claim: Security/supervisor mode signals are hardwired and not used to restrict debug interface access, confirming there is no runtime privilege enforcement for the debug path.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy debug unit provides register file write access (regfile_wreq_o) and CSR write access through the external debug interface. While some operations check debug_halted_o before proceeding, the grant signal is issued unconditionally for certain address regions, and the always-accessible debug register region (6'b00_0000) has no halt-state guard at all. The debug interface is connected at the fc_subsystem level without authentication, and supervisor_mode/secure_mode signals are hardwired rather than used to gate debug access. This means an attacker with access to the debug interface port can issue requests that receive grants even when the core is not halted, and can read/write GPRs and CSRs with minimal restriction.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical or logical access to the debug interface can read and write general-purpose registers and CSRs (including security-sensitive registers like mstatus, mepc) without proper authentication. The hardwired security mode signals mean this path cannot be locked out at runtime. This could lead to privilege escalation, extraction of secrets, or arbitrary code execution by redirecting the program counter via the debug jump mechanism.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full address decode logic of zeroriscy_debug_unit is not completely shown in snippets (only partial case branches visible). It is unclear whether there is a higher-level authentication mechanism (e.g., JTAG password) upstream of fc_subsystem that gates the debug_req signal before it reaches the RTL shown here. The regfile_wreq_o assignment logic beyond the combinational block initialization is not fully visible in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: csr_access_o is not deasserted when deassert_we_i is active, unlike other write-enabling signals, allowing CSR access to proceed during stalls or pipeline flushes.

evidence.description: In the deassert-we block at the end of zeroriscy_decoder.sv, signals such as regfile_we_o, data_req_o, csr_op_o, mult_int_en_o, div_int_en_o, jump_in_id_o, and branch_in_id_o are all gated by deassert_we_i. However, csr_access_o is conspicuously absent from this list and is never deasserted when deassert_we_i is high.
evidence.supports_claim: csr_access_o remains asserted even when the pipeline should be stalled or writes suppressed, bypassing the deassert-we protection applied to all other write-enabling control signals.

evidence.description: The always_comb block shows csr_access_o is set to 1'b0 as a default but is driven to 1'b1 for CSR instructions inside the decode logic. There is no conditional gating on deassert_we_i for csr_access_o within the block itself, and the final assign statements (SNIP-0012) confirm no deassert gate is applied.
evidence.supports_claim: Confirms that csr_access_o flows from decoder to the EX stage without any suppression when deassert_we_i is active, unlike every comparable write-enabling signal.

evidence.description: In zeroriscy_core.sv, csr_access_ex (driven from csr_access_o) is used to compute csr_addr_int and gate csr address selection. If csr_access_o is incorrectly asserted during a stall cycle, a spurious CSR address is presented to the CSR register file, potentially causing an unintended CSR read or write to be initiated.
evidence.supports_claim: An erroneously asserted csr_access_o can propagate to the CSR address path and trigger unintended CSR access, affecting privileged machine-mode registers.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_cs_registers exposes machine-mode CSRs including mstatus, mepc, mcause, and performance counters via the csr_access_i / csr_op_i interface. Spurious access could corrupt privileged state.
evidence.supports_claim: The CSR register file contains security-critical machine-mode state that should only be written when a valid, non-stalled CSR instruction is in the execute stage.

finding.reasoning_summary: All write-enabling decoder outputs (regfile_we_o, data_req_o, csr_op_o, mult_int_en_o, div_int_en_o, jump_in_id_o, branch_in_id_o) are gated with deassert_we_i in the final assign statements of zeroriscy_decoder.sv. The csr_access_o output is not present in this gating list, meaning it can remain asserted during pipeline stalls, multi-cycle operations, or other conditions that trigger deassert_we_i. This asymmetry allows csr_access_ex to be high in the execute stage while csr_op_ex has been forced to CSR_OP_NONE, creating an inconsistent state that could result in an unintended CSR address being driven and potentially a spurious read or write to machine-mode registers.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or a buggy instruction sequence that causes deassert_we_i to be raised (e.g., during a data misalignment stall on a following instruction, or a multi-cycle operation) while a CSR instruction is in the ID stage could trigger an unintended access to privileged CSRs such as mstatus or mepc. This could corrupt privilege-level tracking, interrupt-enable bits, or exception return addresses, potentially enabling privilege escalation or denial-of-service against the exception handling mechanism.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact micro-architectural conditions under which deassert_we_i is raised simultaneously with a CSR instruction in the decode stage are not fully enumerated from the provided snippets alone. The controller (zeroriscy_controller.sv) drives deassert_we_o but its full combinational logic is not shown in the snippets. However, the source-code evidence that csr_access_o is unguarded while all peer signals are guarded is unambiguous and sufficient to establish the vulnerability.

finding.summary: The CSR access permission check via csr_illegal is incomplete: only the CSR operation encoding (bits [13:12]) is validated for legality, but there is no privilege-level check on the CSR address being accessed. Any unprivileged software can access machine-mode CSRs including mstatus (0x300) without a privilege guard.

evidence.description: The decoder sets csr_illegal=1 only when bits [13:12] of the instruction are 2'b00 (default case in the unique case). The check at line 532 then grants access to mstatus (CSR address 0x300) if csr_illegal is not set. There is no check of the current privilege level (e.g., machine vs user mode) before allowing the CSR access to proceed, meaning any privilege level that can issue a CSR instruction can access machine-mode registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The csr_illegal signal is driven only by the CSR operation encoding bits [13:12], not by the CSR address privilege requirements. No privilege-level comparison is performed before asserting csr_status_o or allowing csr_access_o.

evidence.description: The csr_illegal signal is declared as a local combinational variable initialized to 1'b0 at line 127. It is set to 1 only on the default (invalid operation encoding) case. No privilege-level input port exists in the decoder module interface to inform a privilege check.
evidence.supports_claim: The decoder module has no privilege-level input, so it structurally cannot enforce privilege-based CSR access restrictions.

evidence.description: The mstatus CSR (address 0x300) is identified at line 532 and flagged via csr_status_o, but is only conditionally protected by csr_illegal which does not include a privilege check. A CSR instruction with valid operation encoding (2'b01, 2'b10, or 2'b11) targeting 0x300 will always succeed regardless of current privilege mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Access to the machine-mode status register mstatus is gated only on csr_illegal, which does not validate privilege level.

evidence.description: The final output assignments at lines 574-581 deassert signals on stalls but do not apply any privilege-level filtering. csr_op_o propagates the unchecked csr_op to the CSR file without privilege enforcement at the decoder level.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR write operations to privileged registers flow through to execution without any privilege-level guard in the decoder.

finding.reasoning_summary: The zeroriscy_decoder module performs a CSR legality check (csr_illegal) based solely on the CSR operation encoding bits [13:12] of the instruction. It does not have a privilege-level input port and performs no check on whether the current execution privilege mode is sufficient to access the target CSR address. Specifically, access to mstatus (0x300) is identified but guarded only by csr_illegal, which cannot detect privilege violations. This means that if the processor supports user mode (or any mode below machine mode), a lower-privilege instruction could successfully access machine-mode CSRs.
finding.security_impact: An attacker executing code at a lower privilege level could issue CSR instructions targeting machine-mode registers (such as mstatus at 0x300) and the decoder would not flag them as illegal. This could allow privilege escalation, modification of interrupt enable bits, or corruption of machine-mode state from unprivileged code.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The zero-riscy core may only implement machine mode (no user mode), in which case there is no privilege separation to violate and this would not be exploitable. The source snippets do not include the CSR register file implementation or privilege mode tracking logic, so it cannot be confirmed whether a privilege check exists downstream. The finding confidence is medium because the structural absence of a privilege input in the decoder is clear, but the system-level impact depends on whether multiple privilege levels are actually supported.

finding.summary: The CSR access path in zeroriscy_decoder suppresses csr_op via deassert_we but does not suppress csr_access_o, allowing a stalled or flushed CSR instruction to still assert csr_access_o while csr_op_o is forced to CSR_OP_NONE, creating an inconsistent permission state that could be exploited or cause unintended CSR side effects.

evidence.description: Line 579 shows csr_op_o is gated to CSR_OP_NONE when deassert_we_i is asserted. However, csr_access_o (line 125 in SNIP-0003, initialized to 1'b0 combinatorially) is set during instruction decode but there is no corresponding deassert gate on csr_access_o visible in the assign statements at lines 575-581. The deassert block covers regfile_we, mult_int_en, div_int_en, data_req, csr_op, jump_in_id, and branch_in_id — but csr_access_o is not in this list.
evidence.supports_claim: csr_access_o is not suppressed by deassert_we_i while csr_op_o is, creating an inconsistent signal pair that may cause unintended CSR reads or access-side effects during stalls.

evidence.description: csr_access_o is set to 1'b0 as default in always_comb (line 125) and conditionally set to 1'b1 during CSR instruction decode. Unlike csr_op, there is no combinatorial deassert assignment guarding csr_access_o.
evidence.supports_claim: The absence of a deassert gate on csr_access_o means that during pipeline stalls or flushes, csr_access_o may remain asserted while csr_op_o is forced to NONE, which is an inconsistent protection state.

evidence.description: CSR operations are decoded at lines 524-536: csr_op is assigned CSR_OP_WRITE/SET/CLEAR and csr_access_o is implicitly enabled (set to 1 during the SYSTEM opcode path). The csr_illegal signal blocks illegal_insn_o but both csr_access_o and csr_op propagate through for valid CSR addresses.
evidence.supports_claim: Both csr_access_o and csr_op are set together during a valid CSR instruction, but only csr_op is suppressed on deassert, breaking the intended pairing.

evidence.description: In zeroriscy_id_stage at line 405, regfile_wdata_mux is set from csr_rdata_i when csr_access is asserted. If csr_access remains asserted during a stall (while csr_op is NONE), CSR read data could still be routed to the register file write data mux, potentially causing an unintended register file write or CSR readback.
evidence.supports_claim: An unsuppressed csr_access_o during a deasserted cycle could cause CSR register contents to be exposed through the register file write-data path.

evidence.description: The assign block at lines 575-581 explicitly gates regfile_we_o, mult_int_en_o, div_int_en_o, data_req_o, csr_op_o, jump_in_id_o, and branch_in_id_o with deassert_we_i, but csr_access_o is absent from this list. This is a structural omission — csr_access_o is not deasserted when the pipeline is stalled or the instruction is being killed.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing deassert guard on csr_access_o is a concrete RTL violation where a permission/access signal is not suppressed under the same stall conditions that suppress the corresponding operation signal.

finding.reasoning_summary: The decoder explicitly gates multiple write-enable and operation signals through a deassert_we_i mux at the bottom of the module (SNIP-0008, lines 575-581). The signal csr_op_o is gated to CSR_OP_NONE on deassert, but csr_access_o is not present in this gating list. Since csr_access_o is used downstream in the ID stage to route CSR read data into the register file write-data mux (SNIP-0014, line 405), an unsuppressed csr_access_o during a stall or kill cycle creates a window where CSR data can be unintentionally read and routed, even though the CSR operation itself is suppressed.
finding.security_impact: During pipeline stalls or instruction kills (deassert_we_i=1), the CSR access signal remains active while the operation is suppressed. This can cause: (1) unintended CSR read side effects if the CSR implements read-sensitive registers; (2) CSR sensitive data (e.g., from mstatus, mtvec, or custom security CSRs) being routed through the register-file write-data mux path; and (3) a potential information leakage channel if an attacker can trigger stall conditions during CSR read instructions targeting sensitive registers.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The downstream behavior depends on whether the regfile write-enable (regfile_we_o) is also deasserted (it is, per line 575), which may prevent the actual register file write. However, the routing of csr_rdata_i to regfile_wdata_mux based on unsuppressed csr_access still occurs combinatorially. Additionally, whether csr_access_o feeds into any other logic (e.g., CSR module read-enable with side effects) cannot be fully determined from the provided snippets alone. The zeroriscy_cs_registers module behavior on csr_access is not provided.

finding.summary: Debug unit can unconditionally write to the register file bypassing normal write-enable controls, and can directly modify CSRs without privilege checks in the decoder/controller pipeline.

evidence.description: In zeroriscy_id_stage, the register file mux logic gives dbg_reg_wreq_i unconditional priority: when dbg_reg_wreq_i is asserted, regfile_we_mux is forced to 1'b1 and the write address/data are taken directly from the debug interface inputs (dbg_reg_waddr_i, dbg_reg_wdata_i), completely bypassing all normal write-enable qualification (including deassert_we, privilege checks, or instruction validity gates).
evidence.supports_claim: Debug register write request bypasses all normal permission/qualifier guards and forces a register file write unconditionally.

evidence.description: In zeroriscy_core, the CSR address, write data, and operation are muxed directly from debug interface signals (dbg_csr_addr, dbg_csr_wdata, dbg_csr_we) when dbg_csr_req is asserted, with no privilege level check, no illegal-CSR filtering, and no deassert_we qualification. The debug path can issue CSR_OP_WRITE to any CSR address supplied by the debug interface.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug CSR access path bypasses the decoder's csr_illegal check and privilege filtering, allowing arbitrary CSR writes from the debug interface.

evidence.description: The zeroriscy_debug_unit exposes regfile_wreq_o, regfile_waddr_o, and regfile_wdata_o as outputs driven by the external debug interface (debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_addr_i). These are accepted without any authentication or privilege gating inside the unit itself.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug unit provides a direct, ungated path for an external debug master to write arbitrary values to internal registers.

evidence.description: The decoder's csr_op_o is gated by deassert_we_i (set to CSR_OP_NONE when deassert_we_i is high), but this guard applies only to the normal instruction path. The debug CSR path in SNIP-0005 completely bypasses this gate.
evidence.supports_claim: The deassert_we guard in the decoder does not protect against debug-path CSR writes.

evidence.description: The decoder sets csr_illegal when the CSR instruction encoding is invalid and propagates illegal_insn_o, but this check is only performed for instruction-driven CSR accesses. The debug mux in zeroriscy_core selects the debug CSR address/data/op before these checks can apply.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR illegal-instruction checks in the decoder are bypassed by the debug interface CSR mux.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug unit in zero-riscy provides two separate bypass paths into security-sensitive resources: (1) a register file write path that unconditionally overrides regfile_we with 1'b1 whenever dbg_reg_wreq_i is asserted, with no authentication or privilege verification; (2) a CSR write path that muxes debug-supplied address, data, and operation directly into the CSR access logic before any privilege or legality filtering. Both paths are gated only by the presence of an external debug request signal, with no additional runtime privilege checks (e.g., machine-mode gating, lock bits, or lifecycle state). This allows an agent with access to the debug interface to write any general-purpose register or any CSR at any time, regardless of the processor's current privilege level or execution state.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or untrusted debug master with access to the debug interface can arbitrarily corrupt general-purpose registers or overwrite any CSR (including security-critical registers such as mstatus, mtvec, mepc, or PMP configuration registers) without triggering normal privilege or legality checks. This can lead to privilege escalation, disabling of interrupt/trap handling, bypassing of memory protection, and complete compromise of the processor's security state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets do not show whether the debug interface itself is protected by a higher-level authentication mechanism (e.g., a debug authentication register or lifecycle fuse). If such external gating exists upstream of the debug_req_i/debug_we_i signals, the impact would be reduced. However, no such protection is visible in the provided RTL context.

finding.summary: The csr_status_i signal (indicating a CSR status instruction) is used in the controller's DECODE state to unconditionally trigger a pipeline FLUSH without any privilege or permission check, allowing any instruction-level manipulation of CSR status registers without authorization guards.

evidence.description: In the DECODE state, line 306-311, the condition `csr_status_i` alone (ORed with other exception signals) unconditionally causes a transition to FLUSH state with halt signals asserted. There is no privilege level check, lock, or authentication guard before acting on a CSR status instruction. Any instruction that sets csr_status_i triggers a flush without verifying the caller's privilege.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing privilege/permission guard on CSR status instruction handling - any decoded CSR status instruction triggers the FLUSH path without checking privilege mode or authorization.

evidence.description: In the decoder's always_comb block, csr_status_o is initialized to 1'b0 by default and is set based purely on the decoded instruction opcode/fields with no privilege check in the decoder itself.
evidence.supports_claim: The csr_status_o signal is generated from instruction decode without any privilege filtering, feeding an unguarded control path in the controller.

evidence.description: The controller module interface at line 54 shows csr_status_i as a plain input from the decoder with no associated privilege level or mode signal gating it. The module accepts no current privilege mode input that could be used to guard CSR status operations.
evidence.supports_claim: No privilege mode input is present in the controller interface to condition CSR status instruction handling, confirming the absence of a permission guard.

evidence.description: The deassert_we logic at line 579 suppresses csr_op_o when deassert_we_i is set, but csr_status_o itself is not subject to this deassert gate (it is a separate signal), meaning CSR status flush behavior is not suppressed even when write-enable is deasserted.
evidence.supports_claim: The deassert_we mechanism does not prevent csr_status_i from triggering the FLUSH path in the controller, leaving the control flow manipulation unguarded.

finding.reasoning_summary: The csr_status_i signal is generated by the decoder from raw instruction decoding without any privilege level check, and is consumed in the controller's DECODE state to trigger a pipeline FLUSH unconditionally. The controller module interface contains no privilege mode input, and there is no guard logic conditioning the csr_status_i path on current privilege level or any lock/auth state. This means an unprivileged instruction (if it encodes as a CSR status instruction) can cause a pipeline flush, potentially enabling denial-of-service or control-flow manipulation by circumventing the expected privilege boundary for CSR operations.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged software context may be able to trigger CSR-status-induced pipeline flushes without holding the required machine-mode privilege, potentially allowing control-flow disruption or denial-of-service. In a more severe scenario, combined with other vulnerabilities, this could be used to bypass privilege enforcement around CSR state modifications.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet context does not show the full CSR instruction decode logic that sets csr_status_o, so it is uncertain whether privilege filtering occurs elsewhere in the decoder (not shown). Additionally, the exact security model of the target platform (whether M-mode only or multi-privilege) is not confirmed from the snippets alone. The impact severity depends on whether the FLUSH state itself performs privileged operations or merely stalls the pipeline.

finding.summary: The RV32E illegal register check is permanently disabled, allowing instructions referencing the upper 16 registers (which are non-existent in RV32E) to proceed without being flagged as illegal, bypassing the architectural privilege/protection constraint.

evidence.description: Lines 287-290 show that the RV32E register range check is commented out and replaced with a hardcoded `assign illegal_reg_rv32e = 1'b0`. This means even when the core is configured as RV32E (which only has 16 registers), instructions referencing registers x16-x31 are never flagged as illegal.
evidence.supports_claim: The guard logic that should raise an illegal instruction signal for out-of-range register accesses in RV32E mode is permanently deasserted, bypassing the architectural protection check.

evidence.description: The regfile_we_o assignment at line 575 is gated only by deassert_we_i. Because illegal_reg_rv32e is always 0 (never contributes to illegal_insn), a write-enable for an illegal RV32E register access will not be suppressed by the illegal-instruction path.
evidence.supports_claim: The write-enable suppression mechanism relies on deassert_we_i which is driven by controller responses to illegal instructions; since the RV32E check never fires, regfile_we is never deasserted due to this class of violation.

evidence.description: regfile_we (line 123 and output regfile_we_o line 67) controls write-enable to the register file. This is the protected resource that should be gated off for illegal register references in RV32E mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Writes to architecturally non-existent registers in RV32E mode are not prevented because the guard (illegal_reg_rv32e) is disabled.

finding.reasoning_summary: In SNIP-0012, the conditional block that would set illegal_reg_rv32e=1 when any of regfile_addr_ra_id[4], regfile_addr_rb_id[4], or regfile_alu_waddr_id[4] is set (indicating register indices ≥ 16, which are illegal in RV32E) is entirely commented out. The replacement `assign illegal_reg_rv32e = 1'b0` permanently disables this check. As a result, when the core is instantiated with RV32E=1, instructions referencing non-existent registers x16-x31 will not be flagged as illegal, will not trigger the deassert_we path in the controller, and regfile_we_o will remain asserted, allowing register file writes to undefined/out-of-range destinations.
finding.security_impact: In an RV32E-configured core, an attacker or buggy software can reference register addresses x16-x31, which do not architecturally exist. These accesses are silently allowed, potentially writing to unexpected physical register file locations (wrap-around or undefined behavior depending on implementation), corrupting architectural state, bypassing security checks that rely on proper illegal-instruction trapping, and possibly enabling privilege escalation if trap handlers are not invoked for these violations.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not directly shown in the provided snippets whether the physical register file actually has storage for indices 16-31 when RV32E=1, or whether the RV32E parameter is used anywhere else to further constrain behavior. However, the commented-out check is explicit source-code evidence of a missing guard.

finding.summary: The debug register write path in zeroriscy_id_stage bypasses the normal regfile_we gating (including deassert_we and privilege checks), allowing unconditional register file writes when dbg_reg_wreq_i is asserted.

evidence.description: When dbg_reg_wreq_i is asserted, regfile_we_mux is set to 1'b1 unconditionally, bypassing the normal regfile_we signal which is gated by deassert_we_i from the controller. This means debug register writes are not subject to the same stall/hazard/privilege suppression logic that governs normal instruction writes.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug write path sets regfile_we_mux=1 without going through the deassert_we_i gate, bypassing the protection that prevents writes during stalls, misaligned accesses, or illegal instructions.

evidence.description: Normal regfile_we_o is gated by deassert_we_i: 'assign regfile_we_o = (deassert_we_i) ? 1'b0 : regfile_we'. This protection is absent for the debug path.
evidence.supports_claim: The deassert_we guard applies only to the decoder output path, not to the debug register write mux, confirming the bypass.

evidence.description: deassert_we_o is produced by the controller based on stall and hazard conditions (data_misaligned_i, illegal instructions, etc.), and is intended to suppress write enables during unsafe pipeline states.
evidence.supports_claim: The controller's deassert_we signal is the architectural mechanism to suppress writes, but it is bypassed by the debug write path.

evidence.description: regfile_we_mux controls actual writes to the CPU register file. When dbg_reg_wreq_i is high, dbg_reg_wdata_i and dbg_reg_waddr_i are used without any privilege or state validation visible in this logic.
evidence.supports_claim: The register file is the protected resource; the debug path can write any register at any address unconditionally.

finding.reasoning_summary: In zeroriscy_id_stage, the register file write mux (SNIP-0015) has a debug override: when dbg_reg_wreq_i is asserted, it sets regfile_we_mux=1'b1 and uses debug-supplied address/data without consulting the deassert_we_i signal. The normal path (regfile_we) flows through the decoder's deassert_we gate (SNIP-0009), which suppresses writes during stalls, misaligned accesses, and illegal instructions (SNIP-0001). The debug path does not go through this gate, meaning a debug write request can commit a register write even when the pipeline would otherwise suppress it. There is no visible privilege check gating dbg_reg_wreq_i at this level.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or privileged debug interface that can assert dbg_reg_wreq_i can write arbitrary values to any CPU register at any time, bypassing pipeline safety checks. This could corrupt processor state, redirect control flow, or escalate privilege if debug access controls are weak or absent at a higher level.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The chain context does not include the source of dbg_reg_wreq_i or the debug unit's own access control logic. If the debug unit itself enforces privilege checks before asserting dbg_reg_wreq_i, the effective risk is reduced. The RV32E illegal register check is also commented out (SNIP-0014), which may compound the issue.

finding.summary: The debug unit can directly read and write CSRs and general-purpose registers without any privilege or authentication check, and CSR read data is unconditionally forwarded to the debug unit regardless of the core's privilege state.

evidence.description: Lines 613-618 show that the CSR address, write data, and operation are muxed between the normal pipeline path and the debug unit path (dbg_csr_req). When dbg_csr_req is asserted, the debug unit directly drives csr_addr and csr_wdata with no privilege-level check, lock, or authentication gate. Any external debug request immediately gains full CSR read/write access.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug interface bypasses all privilege/permission guards to access CSRs directly.

evidence.description: The zeroriscy_debug_unit module exposes regfile_wreq_o, regfile_waddr_o, regfile_wdata_o (GPR write), and csr_rdata_i (CSR readback) as plain ports with no documented authentication or privilege enforcement inside the unit itself.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug unit provides unrestricted register file write and CSR readback capability through its external interface.

evidence.description: Internal signals dbg_csr_req, dbg_reg_wreq, dbg_reg_waddr, dbg_reg_wdata, and dbg_csr_addr are wired from the debug unit directly into core logic. There is no intermediate permission-state register or lock signal visible that would gate these accesses.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug-originated CSR and GPR write paths exist and are live in the core without a visible privilege gate.

evidence.description: csr_access, csr_op_ex, csr_rdata, and csr_access_ex are the CSR control signals. csr_rdata (line 156) is the sensitive read-data bus that is connected both to the normal pipeline and directly to the debug unit (edge:connects:270), enabling CSR content readback via debug at any time.
evidence.supports_claim: CSR read data is a sensitive resource that flows unconditionally to the debug unit.

evidence.description: The core's top-level debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_we_i, debug_addr_i, debug_wdata_i) is a raw external input with no hardware privilege level or lifecycle state check shown before it is routed into the debug unit that then drives CSR and GPR access signals.
evidence.supports_claim: No hardware permission state guards the activation of the debug access path.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0008 concretely shows that asserting dbg_csr_req routes an externally supplied address and data directly to the CSR file with a CSR_OP_WRITE operation. SNIP-0010 confirms the debug unit has ports to write GPRs and read back CSR data. SNIP-0004 shows the internal wiring is live. SNIP-0002 shows the debug interface is a plain external input. No source snippet shows any privilege-level check, authentication token, life-cycle lock, or debug-enable fuse gating any of these paths. The result is that an attacker with access to the debug interface pins can read and overwrite any CSR or GPR in the core regardless of the software privilege level.
finding.security_impact: An adversary with physical or bus-level access to the debug interface can read sensitive CSRs (e.g., machine-mode status, EPC, cause registers) and overwrite any CSR or general-purpose register without restriction, enabling privilege escalation, information disclosure, and arbitrary code execution in machine mode.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full body of zeroriscy_debug_unit.sv beyond line 102 is not shown; there could be an internal state machine that enforces a halted-core precondition before allowing writes, but no such guard is visible in the provided snippets. Similarly, a system-level debug authentication module upstream of debug_req_i is possible but not evidenced here.

finding.summary: The debug unit can write to the CPU general-purpose register file (dbg_reg_wreq / regfile_wreq_o) without any privilege, lifecycle, or authentication guard. The debug request signal originates from an external pin routed directly into the core, and the debug unit exposes a register-file write port that is only qualified by whether the debug unit itself is active—there is no check against a secure-boot state, life-cycle fuse, or any other access-control mechanism.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem declares the raw `debug_req` signal with no visible privilege qualification. It is a plain logic wire with no tie to any permission or lifecycle register.
evidence.supports_claim: External debug request enters the SoC as an unguarded signal.

evidence.description: fc_subsystem instantiates zeroriscy_core and connects rst_ni and debug_req directly to the core's debug_req_i port without any interposing enable or permission check.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_req is forwarded to the CPU core with no access control gating.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_core exposes a full debug interface (debug_req_i, debug_addr_i, debug_we_i, debug_wdata_i, debug_rdata_o, debug_halt_i, debug_resume_i) as top-level ports with no internal privilege gating visible in the module port list.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug interface is a direct top-level input with no privilege level check.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_debug_unit exposes regfile_wreq_o and regfile_waddr_o/regfile_wdata_o outputs that allow writing any register file entry. The internal signal `regfile_wreq` (line 102) gates the write, but it is only gated by the debug unit's own FSM state, not by any external privilege or lifecycle check.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug unit can assert regfile_wreq_o to write arbitrary GPR values once debug_req_i is asserted.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_core wires dbg_reg_wreq, dbg_reg_waddr, and dbg_reg_wdata directly from the debug unit to the register file write path, and dbg_csr_req enables debug CSR writes routed through csr_addr/csr_wdata muxes.
evidence.supports_claim: GPR and CSR writes via debug interface reach the register file and CSR bank with no additional permission gate.

evidence.description: The CSR mux selects between normal and debug CSR access based solely on dbg_csr_req without any privilege-level or lifecycle check: `assign csr_addr = (dbg_csr_req == 1'b0) ? csr_addr_int : dbg_csr_addr;`. Any asserter of debug_req_i can write any CSR.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard: debug CSR writes are unconditionally accepted when dbg_csr_req is asserted, bypassing normal privilege checks.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_cs_registers holds machine-mode CSRs (mstatus, mepc, mcause, etc.) and exposes csr_rdata_o. All CSR content is readable and writable via the debug path without privilege filtering.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive CSRs are reachable through the unguarded debug interface.

finding.reasoning_summary: The debug_req signal flows from fc_subsystem directly to zeroriscy_core's debug interface port. Inside zeroriscy_core, the debug unit (zeroriscy_debug_unit) can assert regfile_wreq_o to overwrite any GPR and csr_req_o to read or write any CSR. The mux that routes debug CSR access (SNIP-0011) uses only dbg_csr_req as its selector—no privilege level, no lifecycle fuse, no secure-boot lock, no authentication handshake is checked anywhere in the chain. The debug request pin is therefore an unguarded privileged backdoor into both the GPR file and all machine-mode CSRs of the RISC-V core.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with physical or bus-level access to the debug_req pin (or any entity that can assert it at the SoC integration level) can halt the CPU, read and write all general-purpose registers, and read and write all machine-mode CSRs including mstatus and mepc—effectively achieving full CPU takeover, privilege escalation, and secret extraction without any authentication.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippets do not show the full body of zeroriscy_debug_unit's FSM (only the port list and signal declarations are included), so the precise conditions under which regfile_wreq is asserted cannot be fully verified from these snippets alone. However, the absence of any permission/lifecycle signal in the module's port list (SNIP-0013) and the unguarded CSR mux (SNIP-0011) are sufficient concrete evidence of the vulnerability.

finding.summary: The apb_interrupt_cntrl module unconditionally hardwires core_clock_en_o and fetch_en_o to 1'b1, ignoring the core_secure_mode_i input and any permission/privilege state, and fc_subsystem passes core_secure_mode_i as a constant 1'b0 to the interrupt controller, while supervisor_mode_o is also hardwired to 1'b1. Together these mean security-mode gating and supervisor privilege enforcement are permanently bypassed.

evidence.description: Lines 85-86 show 'assign core_clock_en_o = 1'b1' and 'assign fetch_en_o = 1'b1'. Despite the module accepting core_secure_mode_i as an input (line 47), these two security-relevant outputs are hardwired to always-enabled, making any downstream permission check based on clock enable or fetch enable ineffective.
evidence.supports_claim: Security gating outputs are permanently asserted regardless of secure mode state.

evidence.description: Line 283 shows '.core_secure_mode_i ( 1'b0 )' — the secure mode input to the interrupt controller is permanently driven to zero at the subsystem level, meaning the controller can never observe a secure mode condition even if its internal logic were to use it.
evidence.supports_claim: The secure-mode signal is structurally neutralised before reaching the module that could act on it.

evidence.description: Line 273 shows 'assign supervisor_mode_o = 1'b1' — supervisor mode is unconditionally asserted regardless of any runtime privilege state, bypassing the lifecycle-based privilege model.
evidence.supports_claim: Privilege level output is hardwired, removing any runtime privilege enforcement.

finding.reasoning_summary: Three concrete source-code facts establish the finding. First, apb_interrupt_cntrl accepts core_secure_mode_i but its security-critical outputs (core_clock_en_o, fetch_en_o) are unconditionally driven to 1'b1 (SNIP-0001 lines 85-86), so the secure-mode input has no effect on clock or fetch gating. Second, fc_subsystem drives that input with the constant 1'b0 (SNIP-0007 line 283), ensuring the interrupt controller never sees a secure-mode assertion. Third, supervisor_mode_o is hardwired to 1'b1 (SNIP-0007 line 273), permanently advertising supervisor privilege to the rest of the SoC. The combined effect is that the permission chain protecting instruction fetch and clock gating under secure/supervisor modes is completely bypassed at the RTL level.
finding.security_impact: Any software or peripheral on the fabric can trigger interrupts and the core will always be clock-enabled and fetch-enabled regardless of the intended security domain. Secure-mode isolation is non-functional: a non-secure context cannot be clock-gated or fetch-disabled through this path, and the supervisor privilege signal is always asserted, potentially granting elevated access to downstream consumers of supervisor_mode_o.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The downstream consumers of supervisor_mode_o and core_clock_en_o/fetch_en_o are not fully visible in the provided snippets, so the precise exploitable impact of the supervisor-mode hardwiring depends on how those signals are used elsewhere in the SoC. However, the bypass of secure-mode gating is unambiguous from the source snippets provided.

finding.summary: The interrupt controller (zeroriscy_int_controller) latches the IRQ ID into irq_id_q at the moment an interrupt is accepted in IDLE state, but the interrupt enable check (irq_enable_ext) is only evaluated at that single transition. Once the FSM moves to IRQ_PENDING, the irq_req_ctrl_o output is asserted purely based on the FSM state (exc_ctrl_cs == IRQ_PENDING) with no re-evaluation of the interrupt enable bit (m_IE_i). Additionally, in zeroriscy_controller the SLEEP state wakes the core on irq_i directly (line 254) without checking irq_enable_ext or m_IE_i, bypassing the interrupt-enable guard entirely for WFI wakeup.

evidence.description: irq_enable_ext is computed as simply m_IE_i (line 51). The guard is only applied in the IDLE->IRQ_PENDING transition (line 68: if(irq_enable_ext & irq_i)). Once in IRQ_PENDING, irq_req_ctrl_o is driven directly from the FSM state (line 52: exc_ctrl_cs == IRQ_PENDING) without re-checking m_IE_i.
evidence.supports_claim: The interrupt enable bit is checked only on the edge that latches the interrupt; if m_IE_i is cleared while the FSM is in IRQ_PENDING, irq_req_ctrl_o remains asserted, potentially delivering an interrupt that should be masked.

evidence.description: IRQ_PENDING state transitions to IRQ_DONE on ctrl_ack_i without re-checking irq_enable_ext or m_IE_i. The IRQ_DONE state unconditionally returns to IDLE, meaning a masked interrupt can still be fully acknowledged.
evidence.supports_claim: The IRQ_PENDING and IRQ_DONE states never re-evaluate the interrupt enable guard, allowing an interrupt accepted while enabled to proceed even after the enable bit is cleared.

evidence.description: In zeroriscy_controller SLEEP state (lines 254-257), the core wakes up on irq_i alone: 'if (irq_i) begin ctrl_fsm_ns = FIRST_FETCH; end'. There is no check of irq_enable_int or m_IE_i at this point. The comment says 'transparent interrupts are checked to wake up from wfi even if the interrupt is not taken', but this exposes a discrepancy: the core resumes execution even when m_IE_i=0 prevents the interrupt from being taken, potentially disrupting the WFI sleep semantics.
evidence.supports_claim: An interrupt line assertion wakes the processor from WFI regardless of the machine interrupt enable bit, constituting a missing guard on the privileged sleep/wake lifecycle.

evidence.description: zeroriscy_controller receives irq_i (external interrupt line) and irq_req_ctrl_i (from interrupt controller) as separate inputs. irq_i is used directly for WFI wakeup while irq_req_ctrl_i (which went through the enable check) is used for actual exception taking. This dual path means irq_i bypasses the permission check.
evidence.supports_claim: The architectural split between irq_i (unenabled path) and irq_req_ctrl_i (enabled path) creates a bypass: the raw interrupt line drives privileged state changes (WFI exit) without any enable check.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two related issues exist in the interrupt permission chain. First, in zeroriscy_int_controller, the m_IE_i guard is evaluated only at the IDLE->IRQ_PENDING transition; once an interrupt is latched, subsequent changes to m_IE_i do not suppress irq_req_ctrl_o. Second, and more concretely evidenced by source code, the zeroriscy_controller SLEEP state wakes the CPU on irq_i without checking m_IE_i, meaning the machine interrupt enable bit does not gate WFI wakeup. Both issues represent missing re-evaluation of the permission/enable state at protected control points.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or faulty peripheral that asserts an interrupt line can wake a processor from WFI sleep even when machine interrupts are disabled (mstatus.MIE=0). This can break trusted-execution assumptions where the CPU is expected to remain sleeping until explicitly enabled, potentially causing premature execution out of a protected sleep state. It can also cause a masked interrupt to be delivered to the CPU after software has disabled it, leading to unexpected privilege escalation or control flow.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The comment in zeroriscy_controller explicitly notes 'transparent interrupts are checked to wake up from wfi even if the interrupt is not taken', suggesting this WFI transparent-wakeup behavior may be intentional per the RISC-V specification (WIA bits). The RISC-V privileged spec does allow implementations to optionally wake from WFI on a pending-but-disabled interrupt. Whether this constitutes a security violation depends on the security policy of the specific deployment. The m_IE_i re-check issue in IRQ_PENDING is clearer but may have negligible practical impact if the window is narrow. Full confirmation would require the zeroriscy_defines package and system-level interrupt routing logic.

finding.summary: irq_id_ctrl_o is driven directly from the latched irq_id_q register regardless of the interrupt-enable guard, allowing a stale or pending interrupt ID to be readable/usable even when interrupts are disabled

evidence.description: irq_enable_ext is computed as a simple wire assignment from m_IE_i (line 51). irq_req_ctrl_o is gated on the FSM state (exc_ctrl_cs == IRQ_PENDING, line 52), but irq_id_ctrl_o is unconditionally assigned from irq_id_q (line 53) with no guard on irq_enable_ext or irq_req_ctrl_o.
evidence.supports_claim: The interrupt ID output is not gated by the interrupt-enable bit or the IRQ-pending state, so it leaks the latched interrupt ID at all times.

evidence.description: assign irq_id_ctrl_o = irq_id_q is a bare combinational assignment with no conditional on irq_enable_ext or exc_ctrl_cs. The guard that does exist (irq_enable_ext / exc_ctrl_cs == IRQ_PENDING) only gates irq_req_ctrl_o, not irq_id_ctrl_o.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard on irq_id_ctrl_o output; the enable check is absent.

evidence.description: In the controller FSM default assignments, irq_id_o is set to irq_id_ctrl_i (line 186) and irq_enable_int is set to m_IE_i (line 187). irq_id_ctrl_i flows from irq_id_ctrl_o with no additional masking, so the ungated interrupt ID propagates into the controller and is stored in irq_id_o unconditionally in the default path.
evidence.supports_claim: The ungated interrupt ID from int_controller reaches the controller output irq_id_o without re-checking the enable condition.

evidence.description: irq_id_ctrl_o = irq_id_q is always driven regardless of whether m_IE_i is set (irq_enable_ext) or whether the FSM is in IRQ_PENDING state. A consumer reading irq_id_ctrl_o when interrupts are disabled (m_IE_i=0, exc_ctrl_cs=IDLE) still observes the last latched interrupt ID, constituting a missing-guard violation.
evidence.supports_claim: The interrupt ID is accessible/readable even when the interrupt-enable permission bit is clear.

finding.reasoning_summary: In zeroriscy_int_controller, irq_id_ctrl_o is assigned unconditionally from irq_id_q with no dependency on the interrupt-enable signal (irq_enable_ext / m_IE_i) and no dependency on the FSM state (exc_ctrl_cs). Only irq_req_ctrl_o is gated on exc_ctrl_cs == IRQ_PENDING. Consequently, irq_id_ctrl_o continuously exposes the latched interrupt ID to the controller even when interrupts are globally disabled. The controller then copies this value to its default irq_id_o output on every cycle. This is a missing guard on a sensitive control signal: the interrupt ID should only be valid/readable when an interrupt is actually pending and enabled.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or privileged software that can observe irq_id_o (or irq_id_ctrl_o) can determine which interrupt was last active even after disabling interrupts (m_IE_i=0). Additionally, because irq_id_o is set to irq_id_ctrl_i in every FSM default path (not just IRQ_TAKEN), there is a window where a stale interrupt ID could be acted upon if downstream logic does not independently re-check irq_ack_o, potentially leading to incorrect exception-cause values written to CSRs.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full FSM body for IRQ_TAKEN and other states is not shown in the snippets, so it is possible that downstream consumers always qualify irq_id_o with irq_ack_o. The impact of information leakage depends on whether irq_id_o is observable outside the CPU. The missing guard is clear from the source, but exploitability depends on system-level context not fully visible here.

finding.summary: In the SLEEP state, the controller wakes up and transitions to FIRST_FETCH based solely on the raw `irq_i` signal without checking `irq_enable_int` (the machine interrupt enable gate), allowing interrupts to wake the core even when M-mode interrupts are disabled. Conversely, in FIRST_FETCH and DECODE states, the IRQ transition correctly requires both `irq_req_ctrl_i & irq_enable_int`. This asymmetry means a masked/disabled interrupt can still wake the processor from WFI sleep.

evidence.description: In the SLEEP FSM state (lines 236-258), the transition to FIRST_FETCH is conditioned only on `irq_i` with no check of `irq_enable_int`. The comment explicitly notes 'transparent interrupts are checked to wake up from wfi', but the machine interrupt enable bit (m_IE_i / irq_enable_int) is not gated here.
evidence.supports_claim: An interrupt can wake the CPU from WFI sleep even when M-mode interrupts are globally disabled (MIE=0), bypassing the privilege/permission check enforced in other states.

evidence.description: In FIRST_FETCH state, the IRQ transition to IRQ_TAKEN correctly requires `irq_req_ctrl_i & irq_enable_int`, gating on the interrupt enable signal.
evidence.supports_claim: The interrupt enable guard exists and is correctly applied in FIRST_FETCH, confirming the missing guard in SLEEP is an inconsistency, not an intentional design.

evidence.description: In DECODE state (line 316), IRQ transitions also require `irq_req_ctrl_i & irq_enable_int & ~instr_multicyle_i & ~branch_in_id_i`, again properly gating on irq_enable_int.
evidence.supports_claim: Consistent use of irq_enable_int in DECODE vs. absence in SLEEP confirms the guard is intentionally applied elsewhere but missing in the SLEEP wake-up path.

evidence.description: `irq_enable_int` is declared at line 127 as the internal interrupt enable signal, combining m_IE_i and other factors to gate IRQ acceptance.
evidence.supports_claim: The existence of irq_enable_int as the correct permission gate makes its absence from the SLEEP wake-up condition a concrete guard bypass.

evidence.description: IRQ_TAKEN state (lines 378-390) unconditionally sets PC to the exception vector, saves CSR state, and begins interrupt handling. If reached after an improperly-gated wake from SLEEP, the interrupt handler executes despite interrupts being disabled.
evidence.supports_claim: The missing guard in SLEEP can lead to unintended execution of interrupt handlers when MIE=0, violating the RISC-V privilege specification that WFI should not cause a trap when interrupts are disabled (depending on interpretation) and can break security-sensitive low-power states.

finding.reasoning_summary: The SLEEP state wakes on `irq_i` alone (raw interrupt line) without checking `irq_enable_int` (which incorporates m_IE_i). All other interrupt-taking paths (FIRST_FETCH, DECODE) correctly gate on `irq_req_ctrl_i & irq_enable_int`. This means a disabled interrupt can still pull the processor out of WFI sleep, potentially leading to unexpected execution flow and violation of the intended privilege-based interrupt masking.
finding.security_impact: A peripheral or attacker-controlled interrupt line can wake the CPU from sleep and redirect execution to an interrupt handler even when the software has disabled M-mode interrupts (MIE=0). This can break security-critical low-power modes or cause unintended privilege escalation if interrupt handlers assume interrupts were properly enabled when invoked.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact definition of irq_enable_int (whether it solely wraps m_IE_i or includes additional conditions) is not shown in the snippets, but its systematic use as the IRQ gate in FIRST_FETCH and DECODE states makes the SLEEP omission clearly anomalous. The RISC-V spec allows implementation-defined WFI behavior regarding disabled interrupts, so this may be partially intentional, but the inconsistency with other states and lack of any comment justifying it in SLEEP suggests a bug.

finding.summary: test_en_i bypasses clock-gating write-enable guard in the register file, allowing writes to proceed regardless of we_a_i during test mode

evidence.description: The global clock gate CG_WE_GLOBAL passes test_en_i directly to the cluster_clock_gating cell alongside we_a_i. When test_en_i is asserted, the gated clock clk_int is enabled independently of the write-enable signal we_a_i.
evidence.supports_claim: test_en_i can force clk_int active even when we_a_i is deasserted, bypassing the intended write-enable guard.

evidence.description: Each per-word clock gate CG_Inst also receives test_en_i. With test_en_i asserted, every word's mem_clocks[x] can be enabled regardless of waddr_onehot_a[x], collapsing the per-word write-address selection.
evidence.supports_claim: test_en_i bypasses the per-word address-decode clock gate, meaning any word could receive a write when test mode is active.

evidence.description: The latch-based write path stores wdata_a_q into mem[k] whenever mem_clocks[k]==1. If mem_clocks[k] is forced high by test_en_i without a valid write address, arbitrary register file words can be overwritten.
evidence.supports_claim: The register file contents (architectural state) are the protected resource exposed by the clock-gate bypass.

evidence.description: No privilege, authentication, or lifecycle check gates test_en_i in this module. The signal is an unguarded primary input that, when asserted, defeats both levels of clock-gating write protection (global and per-word).
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard on test_en_i constitutes a permission violation: test mode can write any register file entry without a valid we_a_i or correct waddr_a_i.

evidence.description: The flip-flop variant (zeroriscy_register_file_ff.sv) guards writes with we_a_dec[i] derived from both we_a_i and waddr_a_i without a test_en_i bypass at the storage element level, illustrating that the latch-based implementation provides a weaker protection model.
evidence.supports_claim: Demonstrates that the latch-based path has a structurally weaker permission model than the FF-based path, confirming the bypass is specific to the clock-gated latch variant.

finding.reasoning_summary: In zeroriscy_register_file.sv the write path is protected by two levels of clock gating: a global gate (CG_WE_GLOBAL) that enables clk_int only when we_a_i is asserted, and per-word gates (CG_Inst) that enable mem_clocks[x] only for the addressed word. Both gates accept test_en_i as an unconditional bypass. Because test_en_i is an unguarded primary input with no privilege or lifecycle check inside this module, asserting it forces all clock gates open, enabling writes to any register file entry regardless of we_a_i or waddr_a_i. The latch storage in always_latch then transparently captures wdata_a_q into any mem[k] whose clock is high.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or misconfigured test infrastructure that can assert test_en_i gains the ability to write arbitrary values into any CPU architectural register (x1–x31) without the normal write-enable or address-decode controls. This can corrupt CPU state, redirect execution, or leak sensitive register values via subsequent read ports.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The context does not show how test_en_i is driven at the SoC level (whether it is tied off, fuse-protected, or debug-lifecycle gated). If test_en_i is permanently tied to 0 in production or is gated by a secure lifecycle signal upstream, the practical exploitability is reduced. No such upstream guard is visible in the provided snippets.
